In the Edo period, a practice known as "dual pilgrimage" emerged, encouraging worshippers to visit both {{ill|Yuga Daigongen|ja|瑜伽大権現|en}} and {{ill|Konpira Gongen|en}} in {{ill|Sanuki Province|en}}. == Deities Enshrined == The deities enshrined are {{ill|Hikosasaji no Mikoto|ja|彦狭知命}}, {{ill|Kaminahibi no Kami|ja|直毘神}}, {{ill|Yuga Daigongen|ja|瑜伽大権現|en}} (associated Buddhas: {{ill|Amitābha|ja|阿弥陀如来}}, {{ill|Yakushi Nyorai|ja|薬師如来}}), {{ill|Teokihobufu no Mikoto|ja|手置帆負命}}, {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja|天之御中主神}}, {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞鳴命}}, {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|倉稲魂命}}, {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|菅原道真}}, and {{ill|Watatsumi|lt=Ōwatatsumi no Mikoto|ja|大綿津見命}}. == Cultural Properties == * **main hall** – Built in the early Edo period, it is designated as an {{ill|Important Cultural Property of Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県指定重要文化財}} (1961).{{Cite web|url=http://www.pref.okayama.jp/cgi-bin/bunka/culture/controller/client/resultDetail.cgi?id=174 |title=main hall of Yuga Shrine |publisher=Okayama Prefecture |accessdate=2018-06-24}} == Access == * **By Car:** About 15 minutes from [[Seto-Chuo Expressway]]'s {{ill|Mizushima Interchange|en}}. * **By Taxi:** About 15 minutes from {{ill|Kojima Station|en}} on the {{ill|Seto-Ohashi Line|en}}. * **By Bus:** A limited {{ill|Shimoden Bus|en}} service runs from Kojima Station to Yuga Shrine (twice daily). However, buses do not operate on weekends, holidays, or during the New Year period. == References == {{reflist}} == Further Reading == * Okayama Prefectural High School Social Studies Department History Section, ''Revised Edition: History Walks in Okayama Prefecture,'' Yamakawa Publishing, 1991, pp. 96–97. * On-site informational plaques. == Related Articles == * {{ill|Rendaiji (Kurashiki)|en}} * {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|en}} * {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)|en}} * {{ill|Kotohira-gū|en}} == External Links == * [https://yugasan.or.jp/ Yuga Shrine Hongū Official Website] {{Shinto-stub}} {{Buddhism-stub}} {{Shinto shrines}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Tandoku shrines]] [[Category:Yuga faith]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Kurashiki]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:dd]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Yuga Shrine]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[ja:由加神社本宮]]

{{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Yuga Shrine Hongū | location = 2852 Yuga, Kojima, Kurashiki City, [[Okayama Prefecture]] | type = 34.505917 | longitude = 133.851056 | map = Japan Okayama | deity = {{ill|Hikosasaji no Mikoto|ja|彦狭知命}}, {{ill|Kaminahibi no Kami|ja|直毘神}}, {{ill|Yuga Daigongen|ja|瑜伽大権現|en}} and others | founded = {{nihongo|Tenpyō 5|733 CE}} | type = Former [[Prefectural Shrine|Prefectural Shrine]] | festivals = {{nihongo|Spring Grand festival|April first Sunday}}
{{nihongo|Autumn Grand festival|November 3}} | main hall = Three-bay-wide [[Hiyoku Irimoya-zukuri]] style | image = [[File:Yuga Jinja 04.jpg|270px]]
Haiden (worship hall) {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=270}} }} [[File:Hiroshige Bizen Tanokuchi.jpg|thumb|[[:en:Hiroshige|Utagawa Hiroshige]]]] '''Yuga Shrine Hongū''' ({{lang|ja|由加神社本宮}}) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en}} located on Mount Yuga in the Kojima area of southern {{ill|Kurashiki|en}}, {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|en}}, Japan. It is a {{ill|Standalone Shrine|ja|単立神社}} unaffiliated with the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|en}}. Its modern status under the shrine ranking system is a former {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社}}. == Overview == Mount Yuga has been a center of {{ill|Iwakura rock|lt=Iwakura faith|ja|磐座信仰}} since ancient times. In 733 CE, the monk {{ill|Gyōki|en}} enshrined an {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-faced Kannon|ja|十一面観音}} statue here, establishing a religious complex that merged Shinto and Buddhism under the name '''{{nihongo|{{ill|Yuga Daigongen|ja|瑜伽大権現|en}}|瑜伽大権現}}''' (Yuga Great Incarnation). According to the history of {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)|lt=Kumano Shrine|ja|熊野神社 (倉敷市林)}}, five disciples of {{ill|En no Gyōja|en}}, a prominent ascetic, brought Kumano deities from {{ill|Kii Province|en}} and established Kumano Shrine in the northern foothills of Mount Yuga. They later founded Nachi Shrine on Mount Yuga as one of the "New Kumano Three Mountains." From the mid-{{ill|Edo period|en}}, the shrine became a place of worship for the {{ill|Ikeda clan (Okayama)|lt=Ikeda clan|ja|池田氏|en}}, rulers of {{ill|Bizen Province|en}}. The Ikeda clan sponsored the construction of the shrine’s buildings and nearby {{ill|Rendaiji (Kurashiki)|lt=Rendaiji|ja|蓮台寺_(倉敷市)|en}}. The shrine is located in a bustling {{ill|Monzen town|ja|門前町}}, with numerous souvenir shops and food stalls. During the New Year period, the shrine attracts many visitors. A specialty of Mount Yuga is the locally famous "ankoro mochi" (sweet red bean rice cakes). In the {{ill|Meiji period|en}}, the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|en}} (separation of Shinto and Buddhism) policy split Yuga Shrine from Rendaiji Temple. Yuga Shrine now has 52 branch shrines across western Japan. After {{ill|World War II|en}}, the shrine and temple informally resumed cooperation, with temple monks conducting prayers at the shrine. However, Yuga Shrine formally resumed independent religious activities in 1997, leading to a dispute with Rendaiji over property and operations. In the Edo period, a practice known as "dual pilgrimage" emerged, encouraging worshippers to visit both {{ill|Yuga Daigongen|ja|瑜伽大権現|en}} and {{ill|Konpira Gongen|en}} in {{ill|Sanuki Province|en}}. == Deities Enshrined == The deities enshrined are {{ill|Hikosasaji no Mikoto|ja|彦狭知命}}, {{ill|Kaminahibi no Kami|ja|直毘神}}, {{ill|Yuga Daigongen|ja|瑜伽大権現|en}} (associated Buddhas: {{ill|Amitābha|ja|阿弥陀如来}}, {{ill|Yakushi Nyorai|ja|薬師如来}}), {{ill|Teokihobufu no Mikoto|ja|手置帆負命}}, {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja|天之御中主神}}, {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞鳴命}}, {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|倉稲魂命}}, {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|菅原道真}}, and {{ill|Watatsumi|lt=Ōwatatsumi no Mikoto|ja|大綿津見命}}. == Cultural Properties == * **main hall** – Built in the early Edo period, it is designated as an {{ill|Important Cultural Property of Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県指定重要文化財}} (1961).{{Cite web|url=http://www.pref.okayama.jp/cgi-bin/bunka/culture/controller/client/resultDetail.cgi?id=174 |title=main hall of Yuga Shrine |publisher=Okayama Prefecture |accessdate=2018-06-24}} == Access == * **By Car:** About 15 minutes from [[Seto-Chuo Expressway]]'s {{ill|Mizushima Interchange|en}}. * **By Taxi:** About 15 minutes from {{ill|Kojima Station|en}} on the {{ill|Seto-Ohashi Line|en}}. * **By Bus:** A limited {{ill|Shimoden Bus|en}} service runs from Kojima Station to Yuga Shrine (twice daily). However, buses do not operate on weekends, holidays, or during the New Year period. == References == {{reflist}} == Further Reading == * Okayama Prefectural High School Social Studies Department History Section, ''Revised Edition: History Walks in Okayama Prefecture,'' Yamakawa Publishing, 1991, pp. 96–97. * On-site informational plaques. == Related Articles == * {{ill|Rendaiji (Kurashiki)|en}} * {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|en}} * {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)|en}} * {{ill|Kotohira-gū|en}} == External Links == * [https://yugasan.or.jp/ Yuga Shrine Hongū Official Website] {{Shinto-stub}} {{Buddhism-stub}} {{Shinto shrines}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Tandoku shrines]] [[Category:Yuga faith]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Kurashiki]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:dd]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Yuga Shrine]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:Https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/]] [[ja:由加神社本宮]]

In the Edo period, a practice known as "dual pilgrimage" emerged, encouraging worshippers to visit both {{ill|Yuga Daigongen|ja|瑜伽大権現|en}} and {{ill|Konpira Gongen|en}} in {{ill|Sanuki Province|en}}. == Deities Enshrined == The deities enshrined are {{ill|Hikosasaji no Mikoto|ja|彦狭知命}}, {{ill|Kaminahibi no Kami|ja|直毘神}}, {{ill|Yuga Daigongen|ja|瑜伽大権現|en}} (associated Buddhas: {{ill|Amitābha|ja|阿弥陀如来}}, {{ill|Yakushi Nyorai|ja|薬師如来}}), {{ill|Teokihobufu no Mikoto|ja|手置帆負命}}, {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja|天之御中主神}}, {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞鳴命}}, {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|倉稲魂命}}, {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|菅原道真}}, and {{ill|Watatsumi|lt=Ōwatatsumi no Mikoto|ja|大綿津見命}}. == Cultural Properties == * **main hall** – Built in the early Edo period, it is designated as an {{ill|Important Cultural Property of Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県指定重要文化財}} (1961).{{Cite web|url=http://www.pref.okayama.jp/cgi-bin/bunka/culture/controller/client/resultDetail.cgi?id=174 |title=main hall of Yuga Shrine |publisher=Okayama Prefecture |accessdate=2018-06-24}} == Access == * **By Car:** About 15 minutes from [[Seto-Chuo Expressway]]'s {{ill|Mizushima Interchange|en}}. * **By Taxi:** About 15 minutes from {{ill|Kojima Station|en}} on the {{ill|Seto-Ohashi Line|en}}. * **By Bus:** A limited {{ill|Shimoden Bus|en}} service runs from Kojima Station to Yuga Shrine (twice daily). However, buses do not operate on weekends, holidays, or during the New Year period. == References == {{reflist}} == Further Reading == * Okayama Prefectural High School Social Studies Department History Section, ''Revised Edition: History Walks in Okayama Prefecture,'' Yamakawa Publishing, 1991, pp. 96–97. * On-site informational plaques. == Related Articles == * {{ill|Rendaiji (Kurashiki)|en}} * {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|en}} * {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)|en}} * {{ill|Kotohira-gū|en}} == External Links == * [https://yugasan.or.jp/ Yuga Shrine Hongū Official Website] {{Shinto-stub}} {{Buddhism-stub}} {{Shinto shrines}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Tandoku shrines]] [[Category:Yuga faith]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Kurashiki]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:dd]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Yuga Shrine]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:Mass editing to refresh the first new Wikipedia redirects]] [[ja:由加神社本宮]]

{{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Yuga Shrine Hongū | location = 2852 Yuga, Kojima, Kurashiki City, [[Okayama Prefecture]] | type = 34.505917 | longitude = 133.851056 | map = Japan Okayama | deity = {{ill|Hikosasaji no Mikoto|ja|彦狭知命}}, {{ill|Kaminahibi no Kami|ja|直毘神}}, {{ill|Yuga Daigongen|ja|瑜伽大権現|en}} and others | founded = {{nihongo|Tenpyō 5|733 CE}} | type = Former [[Prefectural Shrine|Prefectural Shrine]] | festivals = {{nihongo|Spring Grand festival|April first Sunday}}
{{nihongo|Autumn Grand festival|November 3}} | main hall = Three-bay-wide [[Hiyoku Irimoya-zukuri]] style | image = [[File:Yuga Jinja 04.jpg|270px]]
Haiden (worship hall) {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=270}} }} [[File:Hiroshige Bizen Tanokuchi.jpg|thumb|[[:en:Hiroshige|Utagawa Hiroshige]]]] '''Yuga Shrine Hongū''' ({{lang|ja|由加神社本宮}}) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en}} located on Mount Yuga in the Kojima area of southern {{ill|Kurashiki|en}}, {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|en}}, Japan. It is a {{ill|Standalone Shrine|ja|単立神社}} unaffiliated with the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|en}}. Its modern status under the shrine ranking system is a former {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社}}. == Overview == Mount Yuga has been a center of {{ill|Iwakura rock|lt=Iwakura faith|ja|磐座信仰}} since ancient times. In 733 CE, the monk {{ill|Gyōki|en}} enshrined an {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-faced Kannon|ja|十一面観音}} statue here, establishing a religious complex that merged Shinto and Buddhism under the name '''{{nihongo|{{ill|Yuga Daigongen|ja|瑜伽大権現|en}}|瑜伽大権現}}''' (Yuga Great Incarnation). According to the history of {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)|lt=Kumano Shrine|ja|熊野神社 (倉敷市林)}}, five disciples of {{ill|En no Gyōja|en}}, a prominent ascetic, brought Kumano deities from {{ill|Kii Province|en}} and established Kumano Shrine in the northern foothills of Mount Yuga. They later founded Nachi Shrine on Mount Yuga as one of the "New Kumano Three Mountains." From the mid-{{ill|Edo period|en}}, the shrine became a place of worship for the {{ill|Ikeda clan (Okayama)|lt=Ikeda clan|ja|池田氏|en}}, rulers of {{ill|Bizen Province|en}}. The Ikeda clan sponsored the construction of the shrine’s buildings and nearby {{ill|Rendaiji (Kurashiki)|lt=Rendaiji|ja|蓮台寺_(倉敷市)|en}}. The shrine is located in a bustling {{ill|Monzen town|ja|門前町}}, with numerous souvenir shops and food stalls. During the New Year period, the shrine attracts many visitors. A specialty of Mount Yuga is the locally famous "ankoro mochi" (sweet red bean rice cakes). In the {{ill|Meiji period|en}}, the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|en}} (separation of Shinto and Buddhism) policy split Yuga Shrine from Rendaiji Temple. Yuga Shrine now has 52 branch shrines across western Japan. After {{ill|World War II|en}}, the shrine and temple informally resumed cooperation, with temple monks conducting prayers at the shrine. However, Yuga Shrine formally resumed independent religious activities in 1997, leading to a dispute with Rendaiji over property and operations. In the Edo period, a practice known as "dual pilgrimage" emerged, encouraging worshippers to visit both {{ill|Yuga Daigongen|ja|瑜伽大権現|en}} and {{ill|Konpira Gongen|en}} in {{ill|Sanuki Province|en}}. == Deities Enshrined == The deities enshrined are {{ill|Hikosasaji no Mikoto|ja|彦狭知命}}, {{ill|Kaminahibi no Kami|ja|直毘神}}, {{ill|Yuga Daigongen|ja|瑜伽大権現|en}} (associated Buddhas: {{ill|Amitābha|ja|阿弥陀如来}}, {{ill|Yakushi Nyorai|ja|薬師如来}}), {{ill|Teokihobufu no Mikoto|ja|手置帆負命}}, {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja|天之御中主神}}, {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞鳴命}}, {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|倉稲魂命}}, {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|菅原道真}}, and {{ill|Watatsumi|lt=Ōwatatsumi no Mikoto|ja|大綿津見命}}. == Cultural Properties == * **main hall** – Built in the early Edo period, it is designated as an {{ill|Important Cultural Property of Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県指定重要文化財}} (1961).{{Cite web|url=http://www.pref.okayama.jp/cgi-bin/bunka/culture/controller/client/resultDetail.cgi?id=174 |title=main hall of Yuga Shrine |publisher=Okayama Prefecture |accessdate=2018-06-24}} == Access == * **By Car:** About 15 minutes from [[Seto-Chuo Expressway]]'s {{ill|Mizushima Interchange|en}}. * **By Taxi:** About 15 minutes from {{ill|Kojima Station|en}} on the {{ill|Seto-Ohashi Line|en}}. * **By Bus:** A limited {{ill|Shimoden Bus|en}} service runs from Kojima Station to Yuga Shrine (twice daily). However, buses do not operate on weekends, holidays, or during the New Year period. == References == {{reflist}} == Further Reading == * Okayama Prefectural High School Social Studies Department History Section, ''Revised Edition: History Walks in Okayama Prefecture,'' Yamakawa Publishing, 1991, pp. 96–97. * On-site informational plaques. == Related Articles == * {{ill|Rendaiji (Kurashiki)|en}} * {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|en}} * {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)|en}} * {{ill|Kotohira-gū|en}} == External Links == * [https://yugasan.or.jp/ Yuga Shrine Hongū Official Website] {{Shinto-stub}} {{Buddhism-stub}} {{Shinto shrines}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Tandoku shrines]] [[Category:Yuga faith]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Kurashiki]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:dd]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Yuga Shrine]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:Mass editing to refresh the first new Wikipedia redirects]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[ja:由加神社本宮]]

The Ikeda clan sponsored the construction of the shrine’s buildings and nearby {{ill|Rendaiji (Kurashiki)|lt=Rendaiji|ja|蓮台寺_(倉敷市)|en}}. However, buses do not operate on weekends, holidays, or during the New Year period. == References == {{reflist}} == Further Reading == * Okayama Prefectural High School Social Studies Department History Section, ''Revised Edition: History Walks in Okayama Prefecture,'' Yamakawa Publishing, 1991, pp. 96–97. * On-site informational plaques. == Related Articles == * {{ill|Rendaiji (Kurashiki)|en}} * {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|en}} * {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)|en}} * {{ill|Kotohira-gū|en}} == External Links == * [https://yugasan.or.jp/ Yuga Shrine Hongū Official Website] {{Shinto-stub}} {{Buddhism-stub}} {{Shinto shrines}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Tandoku shrines]] [[Category:Yuga faith]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Kurashiki]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:dd]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Yuga Shrine]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:Mass editing to refresh the first new Wikipedia redirects]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Yuga Shrine]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[ja:由加神社本宮]] {{Shrine | name = Yuga Shrine Main Shrine | location = 2852 Ko-jima Yuga, [[Kurashiki]], [[Okayama Prefecture]] | coordinates = {{Coord|34|30|21.3|N|133|51|3.8|E|region:JP-33_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | Deity = Hikosa no Oshinomikoto, Kamunaobi-no-Kami, Daigongen, among others | Foundation = Traditionally [[Tenpyō]] 5 (733) | Shrine rank = Former Prefectural Shrine (県社) | Annual festival = Spring Grand Festival (First Sunday of April)
Autumn Grand Festival (November 3) | Main hall style = Sanma-sha Hiyoku Irimoya-zukuri | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Yuga Jinja 04.jpg|270px]]
Worship hall {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=270}} }} [[File:Hiroshige Bizen Tanokuchi.jpg|thumb|[[Utagawa Hiroshige]]]] '''Yuga Shrine Main Shrine''' (由加神社本宮, Yuga Jinja Hongū) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in the southern part of [[Kurashiki]], [[Okayama Prefecture]], on [[Yuga Mountain]] in the [[Ko-jima region (Kurashiki)|Ko-jima]] area. Under the {{ill|Modern shrine ranking system|ja|近代社格制度}}, it held the rank of a prefectural shrine and is an independent shrine unaffiliated with the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}}. == Overview == Yuga Mountain has been a site of {{ill|Iwakura rock|ja|磐座信仰|Iwakura rock=Iwakura rock|lt=Iwakura worship}} since ancient times. In [[Tenpyō]] 5 (733), {{ill|Gyōki|ja|行基}} enshrined the {{ill|Eleven-Faced Kannon|ja|十一面観音}} on the mountain, transforming it into a {{ill|syncretic|ja|神仏習合}} site known as '''Yuga Daigongen'''. According to the origin legend of {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)|ja|熊野神社 (倉敷市林)}}, five disciples of {{ill|En no Gyōja|ja|役小角}} brought Kumano worship from {{ill|Kii Province|ja|紀伊国}} to the area during the early {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, founding Kumano Shrine. They later established Nachi Shrine on Yuga Mountain as part of the "New Kumano Three Shrines." In the mid-{{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, the shrine became a place of worship for the {{ill|Ikeda clan|ja|池田氏}}, lords of {{ill|Bizen Province|ja|備前国}}. The Ikeda clan constructed the shrine's buildings and the guest hall of {{ill|Rendai-ji|ja|蓮台寺}}. The approach to the shrine is lined with numerous souvenir shops, forming a {{ill|Monzenmachi|ja|門前町}}. During {{ill|Hatsumōde|ja|初詣}} and other festivals, the area bustles with visitors. The local specialty "Ankoro Mochi" is a notable delicacy sold in this area. With the {{ill|Shinbutsu Bunri|ja|神仏分離}} order in the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}}, Yuga Shrine and Rendai-ji were separated. Today, the shrine oversees 52 branch shrines, primarily in western Japan. In [[1945]] (Shōwa 20), after the dissolution of {{ill|State Shinto|ja|国家神道}}, Yuga Shrine and Rendai-ji began to operate collaboratively, with rituals conducted by monks in the shrine's main hall. However, in [[1997]], Yuga Shrine began its independent religious activities, leading to disputes with Rendai-ji over property use. These conflicts stemmed from disagreements between the then-abbot of Rendai-ji and the chief priest of Yuga Shrine. During the Edo period, pilgrims practiced {{ill|Dual Pilgrimage|ja|両参り}}, visiting both Yuga Daigongen (Rendai-ji on Yuga Mountain) and {{ill|Konpira Gongen|ja|金毘羅大権現|lt=Konpira Daigongen}} ({{ill|Matsuo-dera (Kompira, Kotohira, Kagawa)|ja|松尾寺}} in {{ill|Sanuki Province|ja|讃岐国}}). Consequently, the southern port town of {{ill|Tanokuchi|ja|田の口地区}} developed as a gateway for Yuga Mountain pilgrims. == Deities == The shrine enshrines Hikosa no Oshinomikoto, Kamunaobi-no-Kami, Daigongen (Honji Buddha: {{ill|Amitābha|ja|阿弥陀如来}}, {{ill|Medicine Buddha|ja|薬師如来}}), and others, including: * {{ill|Hikosa-no-Oshinomikoto|ja|彦狭知命}} * {{ill|Kamunaobi-no-Kami|ja|神直日神}} * {{ill|Daigongen|ja|権現}} * {{ill|Teoki-hofu-no-Mikoto|ja|手置帆負命}} * {{ill|Amenominakanushi|ja|天之御中主神}} * {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞鳴命}} * {{ill|Ukanomitama|lt=Ukanomitama-no-Kami|ja|倉稲魂命}} * {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|菅原道真}} * {{ill|Watatsumi|lt=Watatsumi-no-Mikoto|ja|大綿津見命}} == Cultural Properties == * Main Hall - An early Edo period construction. Designated as an Okayama Prefecture Important Cultural Property in [[1961]].{{Cite web|url=http://www.pref.okayama.jp/cgi-bin/bunka/culture/controller/client/resultDetail.cgi?id=174 |title=Yuga Shrine Main Hall |publisher=Okayama Prefecture |accessdate=2018-06-24}} == Access == * Approximately 15 minutes by car from [[Mizushima Interchange]] on the [[Seto-Chūō Expressway]]. * Approximately 15 minutes by taxi from [[Kojima Station]] on the [[Seto-Ōhashi Line]]. * [http://www.shimoden.net/rosen/rosen/oujigatake.html Shimoden Bus Yuga Mountain Line] operates two round-trip services daily from Kojima Station. However, buses do not operate on weekends, holidays, or during New Year's, making them unsuitable for tourism. == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Topics == * {{ill|Rendai-ji|ja|蓮台寺 (倉敷市)}} * {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} * {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)|ja|熊野神社 (倉敷市林)}} * {{ill|Kotohira-gū|ja|金刀比羅宮}} == External Links == * [https://yugasan.or.jp/ Official website of Yuga Shrine Main Shrine] {{shinto-stub}} {{Buddhism-stub}} {{Shinto side}} [[Category:Former prefectural shrines in Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Independent shrines]] [[Category:Yuga faith]] [[Category:Syncretic shrines]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Shrines in Kurashiki]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]] {{citation needed|Yuga Shrine Honmiya}}[[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[ja:由加神社本宮]]

{{otheruses}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Yuga Shrine Hongū | location = 2852 Yuga, Kojima, Kurashiki City, [[Okayama Prefecture]] | type = 34.505917 | longitude = 133.851056 | map = Japan Okayama | deity = {{ill|Hikosasaji no Mikoto|ja|彦狭知命}}, {{ill|Kaminahibi no Kami|ja|直毘神}}, {{ill|Yuga Daigongen|ja|瑜伽大権現|en}} and others | founded = {{nihongo|Tenpyō 5|733 CE}} | type = Former [[Prefectural Shrine|Prefectural Shrine]] | festivals = {{nihongo|Spring Grand festival|April first Sunday}}
{{nihongo|Autumn Grand festival|November 3}} | main hall = Three-bay-wide [[Hiyoku Irimoya-zukuri]] style | image = [[File:Yuga Jinja 04.jpg|270px]]
Haiden (worship hall) {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=270}} }} [[File:Hiroshige Bizen Tanokuchi.jpg|thumb|[[:en:Hiroshige|Utagawa Hiroshige]]]] '''Yuga Shrine Hongū''' ({{lang|ja|由加神社本宮}}) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en}} located on Mount Yuga in the Kojima area of southern {{ill|Kurashiki|en}}, {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|en}}, Japan. It is a {{ill|Standalone Shrine|ja|単立神社}} unaffiliated with the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|en}}. Its modern status under the shrine ranking system is a former {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社}}. == Overview == Mount Yuga has been a center of {{ill|Iwakura rock|lt=Iwakura faith|ja|磐座信仰}} since ancient times. In 733 CE, the monk {{ill|Gyōki|en}} enshrined an {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-faced Kannon|ja|十一面観音}} statue here, establishing a religious complex that merged Shinto and Buddhism under the name '''{{nihongo|{{ill|Yuga Daigongen|ja|瑜伽大権現|en}}|瑜伽大権現}}''' (Yuga Great Incarnation). According to the history of {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)|lt=Kumano Shrine|ja|熊野神社 (倉敷市林)}}, five disciples of {{ill|En no Gyōja|en}}, a prominent ascetic, brought Kumano deities from {{ill|Kii Province|en}} and established Kumano Shrine in the northern foothills of Mount Yuga. They later founded Nachi Shrine on Mount Yuga as one of the "New Kumano Three Mountains." From the mid-{{ill|Edo period|en}}, the shrine became a place of worship for the {{ill|Ikeda clan (Okayama)|lt=Ikeda clan|ja|池田氏|en}}, rulers of {{ill|Bizen Province|en}}. The Ikeda clan sponsored the construction of the shrine’s buildings and nearby {{ill|Rendaiji (Kurashiki)|lt=Rendaiji|ja|蓮台寺_(倉敷市)|en}}. The shrine is located in a bustling {{ill|Monzen town|ja|門前町}}, with numerous souvenir shops and food stalls. During the New Year period, the shrine attracts many visitors. A specialty of Mount Yuga is the locally famous "ankoro mochi" (sweet red bean rice cakes). In the {{ill|Meiji period|en}}, the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|en}} (separation of Shinto and Buddhism) policy split Yuga Shrine from Rendaiji Temple. Yuga Shrine now has 52 branch shrines across western Japan. After {{ill|World War II|en}}, the shrine and temple informally resumed cooperation, with temple monks conducting prayers at the shrine. However, Yuga Shrine formally resumed independent religious activities in 1997, leading to a dispute with Rendaiji over property and operations. In the Edo period, a practice known as "dual pilgrimage" emerged, encouraging worshippers to visit both {{ill|Yuga Daigongen|ja|瑜伽大権現|en}} and {{ill|Konpira Gongen|en}} in {{ill|Sanuki Province|en}}. == Deities Enshrined == The deities enshrined are {{ill|Hikosasaji no Mikoto|ja|彦狭知命}}, {{ill|Kaminahibi no Kami|ja|直毘神}}, {{ill|Yuga Daigongen|ja|瑜伽大権現|en}} (associated Buddhas: {{ill|Amitābha|ja|阿弥陀如来}}, {{ill|Yakushi Nyorai|ja|薬師如来}}), {{ill|Teokihobufu no Mikoto|ja|手置帆負命}}, {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja|天之御中主神}}, {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞鳴命}}, {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|倉稲魂命}}, {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|菅原道真}}, and {{ill|Watatsumi|lt=Ōwatatsumi no Mikoto|ja|大綿津見命}}. == Cultural Properties == * **main hall** – Built in the early Edo period, it is designated as an {{ill|Important Cultural Property of Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県指定重要文化財}} (1961).{{Cite web|url=http://www.pref.okayama.jp/cgi-bin/bunka/culture/controller/client/resultDetail.cgi?id=174 |title=main hall of Yuga Shrine |publisher=Okayama Prefecture |accessdate=2018-06-24}} == Access == * **By Car:** About 15 minutes from [[Seto-Chuo Expressway]]'s {{ill|Mizushima Interchange|en}}. * **By Taxi:** About 15 minutes from {{ill|Kojima Station|en}} on the {{ill|Seto-Ohashi Line|en}}. * **By Bus:** A limited {{ill|Shimoden Bus|en}} service runs from Kojima Station to Yuga Shrine (twice daily). However, buses do not operate on weekends, holidays, or during the New Year period. == References == {{reflist}} == Further Reading == * Okayama Prefectural High School Social Studies Department History Section, ''Revised Edition: History Walks in Okayama Prefecture,'' Yamakawa Publishing, 1991, pp. 96–97. * On-site informational plaques. == Related Articles == * {{ill|Rendaiji (Kurashiki)|en}} * {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|en}} * {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)|en}} * {{ill|Kotohira-gū|en}} == External Links == * [https://yugasan.or.jp/ Yuga Shrine Hongū Official Website] {{Shinto-stub}} {{Buddhism-stub}} {{Shinto shrines}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Tandoku shrines]] [[Category:Yuga faith]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Kurashiki]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:dd]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Yuga Shrine]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:Mass editing to refresh the first new Wikipedia redirects]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Yuga Shrine]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[ja:由加神社本宮]] {{Shrine | name = Yuga Shrine Main Shrine | location = 2852 Ko-jima Yuga, [[Kurashiki]], [[Okayama Prefecture]] | coordinates = {{Coord|34|30|21.3|N|133|51|3.8|E|region:JP-33_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | Deity = Hikosa no Oshinomikoto, Kamunaobi-no-Kami, Daigongen, among others | Foundation = Traditionally [[Tenpyō]] 5 (733) | Shrine rank = Former Prefectural Shrine (県社) | Annual festival = Spring Grand Festival (First Sunday of April)
Autumn Grand Festival (November 3) | Main hall style = Sanma-sha Hiyoku Irimoya-zukuri | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Yuga Jinja 04.jpg|270px]]
Worship hall {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=270}} }} [[File:Hiroshige Bizen Tanokuchi.jpg|thumb|[[Utagawa Hiroshige]]]] '''Yuga Shrine Main Shrine''' (由加神社本宮, Yuga Jinja Hongū) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in the southern part of [[Kurashiki]], [[Okayama Prefecture]], on [[Yuga Mountain]] in the [[Ko-jima region (Kurashiki)|Ko-jima]] area. Under the {{ill|Modern shrine ranking system|ja|近代社格制度}}, it held the rank of a prefectural shrine and is an independent shrine unaffiliated with the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}}. == Overview == Yuga Mountain has been a site of {{ill|Iwakura rock|ja|磐座信仰|Iwakura rock=Iwakura rock|lt=Iwakura worship}} since ancient times. In [[Tenpyō]] 5 (733), {{ill|Gyōki|ja|行基}} enshrined the {{ill|Eleven-Faced Kannon|ja|十一面観音}} on the mountain, transforming it into a {{ill|syncretic|ja|神仏習合}} site known as '''Yuga Daigongen'''. According to the origin legend of {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)|ja|熊野神社 (倉敷市林)}}, five disciples of {{ill|En no Gyōja|ja|役小角}} brought Kumano worship from {{ill|Kii Province|ja|紀伊国}} to the area during the early {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, founding Kumano Shrine. They later established Nachi Shrine on Yuga Mountain as part of the "New Kumano Three Shrines." In the mid-{{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, the shrine became a place of worship for the {{ill|Ikeda clan|ja|池田氏}}, lords of {{ill|Bizen Province|ja|備前国}}. The Ikeda clan constructed the shrine's buildings and the guest hall of {{ill|Rendai-ji|ja|蓮台寺}}. The approach to the shrine is lined with numerous souvenir shops, forming a {{ill|Monzenmachi|ja|門前町}}. During {{ill|Hatsumōde|ja|初詣}} and other festivals, the area bustles with visitors. The local specialty "Ankoro Mochi" is a notable delicacy sold in this area. With the {{ill|Shinbutsu Bunri|ja|神仏分離}} order in the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}}, Yuga Shrine and Rendai-ji were separated. Today, the shrine oversees 52 branch shrines, primarily in western Japan. In [[1945]] (Shōwa 20), after the dissolution of {{ill|State Shinto|ja|国家神道}}, Yuga Shrine and Rendai-ji began to operate collaboratively, with rituals conducted by monks in the shrine's main hall. However, in [[1997]], Yuga Shrine began its independent religious activities, leading to disputes with Rendai-ji over property use. These conflicts stemmed from disagreements between the then-abbot of Rendai-ji and the chief priest of Yuga Shrine. During the Edo period, pilgrims practiced {{ill|Dual Pilgrimage|ja|両参り}}, visiting both Yuga Daigongen (Rendai-ji on Yuga Mountain) and {{ill|Konpira Gongen|ja|金毘羅大権現|lt=Konpira Daigongen}} ({{ill|Matsuo-dera (Kompira, Kotohira, Kagawa)|ja|松尾寺}} in {{ill|Sanuki Province|ja|讃岐国}}). Consequently, the southern port town of {{ill|Tanokuchi|ja|田の口地区}} developed as a gateway for Yuga Mountain pilgrims. == Deities == The shrine enshrines Hikosa no Oshinomikoto, Kamunaobi-no-Kami, Daigongen (Honji Buddha: {{ill|Amitābha|ja|阿弥陀如来}}, {{ill|Medicine Buddha|ja|薬師如来}}), and others, including: * {{ill|Hikosa-no-Oshinomikoto|ja|彦狭知命}} * {{ill|Kamunaobi-no-Kami|ja|神直日神}} * {{ill|Daigongen|ja|権現}} * {{ill|Teoki-hofu-no-Mikoto|ja|手置帆負命}} * {{ill|Amenominakanushi|ja|天之御中主神}} * {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞鳴命}} * {{ill|Ukanomitama|lt=Ukanomitama-no-Kami|ja|倉稲魂命}} * {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|菅原道真}} * {{ill|Watatsumi|lt=Watatsumi-no-Mikoto|ja|大綿津見命}} == Cultural Properties == * Main Hall - An early Edo period construction. Designated as an Okayama Prefecture Important Cultural Property in [[1961]].{{Cite web|url=http://www.pref.okayama.jp/cgi-bin/bunka/culture/controller/client/resultDetail.cgi?id=174 |title=Yuga Shrine Main Hall |publisher=Okayama Prefecture |accessdate=2018-06-24}} == Access == * Approximately 15 minutes by car from [[Mizushima Interchange]] on the [[Seto-Chūō Expressway]]. * Approximately 15 minutes by taxi from [[Kojima Station]] on the [[Seto-Ōhashi Line]]. * [http://www.shimoden.net/rosen/rosen/oujigatake.html Shimoden Bus Yuga Mountain Line] operates two round-trip services daily from Kojima Station. However, buses do not operate on weekends, holidays, or during New Year's, making them unsuitable for tourism. == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Topics == * {{ill|Rendai-ji|ja|蓮台寺 (倉敷市)}} * {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} * {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)|ja|熊野神社 (倉敷市林)}} * {{ill|Kotohira-gū|ja|金刀比羅宮}} == External Links == * [https://yugasan.or.jp/ Official website of Yuga Shrine Main Shrine] {{shinto-stub}} {{Buddhism-stub}} {{Shinto side}} [[Category:Former prefectural shrines in Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Independent shrines]] [[Category:Yuga faith]] [[Category:Syncretic shrines]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Shrines in Kurashiki]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]] {{citation needed|Yuga Shrine Honmiya}}[[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[ja:由加神社本宮]]

According to the origin legend of {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)|ja|熊野神社 (倉敷市林)}}, five disciples of {{ill|En no Gyōja|ja|役小角}} brought Kumano worship from {{ill|Kii Province|ja|紀伊国}} to the area during the early {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, founding Kumano Shrine. They later established Nachi Shrine on Yuga Mountain as part of the "New Kumano Three Shrines." In the mid-{{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, the shrine became a place of worship for the {{ill|Ikeda clan|ja|池田氏}}, lords of {{ill|Bizen Province|ja|備前国}}. The Ikeda clan constructed the shrine's buildings and the guest hall of {{ill|Rendai-ji|ja|蓮台寺}}. The approach to the shrine is lined with numerous souvenir shops, forming a {{ill|Monzenmachi|ja|門前町}}. During {{ill|Hatsumōde|ja|初詣}} and other festivals, the area bustles with visitors. The local specialty "Ankoro Mochi" is a notable delicacy sold in this area. With the {{ill|Shinbutsu Bunri|ja|神仏分離}} order in the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}}, Yuga Shrine and Rendai-ji were separated. Today, the shrine oversees 52 branch shrines, primarily in western Japan. In [[1945]] (Shōwa 20), after the dissolution of {{ill|State Shinto|ja|国家神道}}, Yuga Shrine and Rendai-ji began to operate collaboratively, with rituals conducted by monks in the shrine's main hall. However, in [[1997]], Yuga Shrine began its independent religious activities, leading to disputes with Rendai-ji over property use. These conflicts stemmed from disagreements between the then-abbot of Rendai-ji and the chief priest of Yuga Shrine. During the Edo period, pilgrims practiced {{ill|Dual Pilgrimage|ja|両参り}}, visiting both Yuga Daigongen (Rendai-ji on Yuga Mountain) and {{ill|Konpira Gongen|ja|金毘羅大権現|lt=Konpira Daigongen}} ({{ill|Matsuo-dera (Kompira, Kotohira, Kagawa)|ja|松尾寺}} in {{ill|Sanuki Province|ja|讃岐国}}). Consequently, the southern port town of {{ill|Tanokuchi|ja|田の口地区}} developed as a gateway for Yuga Mountain pilgrims. == Deities == The shrine enshrines Hikosa no Oshinomikoto, Kamunaobi-no-Kami, Daigongen (Honji Buddha: {{ill|Amitābha|ja|阿弥陀如来}}, {{ill|Medicine Buddha|ja|薬師如来}}), and others, including: * {{ill|Hikosa-no-Oshinomikoto|ja|彦狭知命}} * {{ill|Kamunaobi-no-Kami|ja|神直日神}} * {{ill|Daigongen|ja|権現}} * {{ill|Teoki-hofu-no-Mikoto|ja|手置帆負命}} * {{ill|Amenominakanushi|ja|天之御中主神}} * {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞鳴命}} * {{ill|Ukanomitama|lt=Ukanomitama-no-Kami|ja|倉稲魂命}} * {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|菅原道真}} * {{ill|Watatsumi|lt=Watatsumi-no-Mikoto|ja|大綿津見命}} == Cultural Properties == * Main Hall - An early Edo period construction. Designated as an Okayama Prefecture Important Cultural Property in [[1961]].{{Cite web|url=http://www.pref.okayama.jp/cgi-bin/bunka/culture/controller/client/resultDetail.cgi?id=174 |title=Yuga Shrine Main Hall |publisher=Okayama Prefecture |accessdate=2018-06-24}} == Access == * Approximately 15 minutes by car from [[Mizushima Interchange]] on the [[Seto-Chūō Expressway]]. * Approximately 15 minutes by taxi from [[Kojima Station]] on the [[Seto-Ōhashi Line]]. * [http://www.shimoden.net/rosen/rosen/oujigatake.html Shimoden Bus Yuga Mountain Line] operates two round-trip services daily from Kojima Station. However, buses do not operate on weekends, holidays, or during New Year's, making them unsuitable for tourism. == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Topics == * {{ill|Rendai-ji|ja|蓮台寺 (倉敷市)}} * {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} * {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)|ja|熊野神社 (倉敷市林)}} * {{ill|Kotohira-gū|ja|金刀比羅宮}} == External Links == * [https://yugasan.or.jp/ Official website of Yuga Shrine Main Shrine] {{shinto-stub}} {{Buddhism-stub}} {{Shinto side}} [[Category:Former prefectural shrines in Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Independent shrines]] [[Category:Yuga faith]]{{Yuga Faith}}[[Category:Yuga faith]] [[Category:Syncretic shrines]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Shrines in Kurashiki]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]] {{citation needed|Yuga Shrine Honmiya}}[[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[ja:由加神社本宮]]

{{otheruses}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Yuga Shrine Hongū | location = 2852 Yuga, Kojima, Kurashiki City, [[Okayama Prefecture]] | type = 34.505917 | longitude = 133.851056 | map = Japan Okayama | deity = {{ill|Hikosasaji no Mikoto|ja|彦狭知命}}, {{ill|Kaminahibi no Kami|ja|直毘神}}, {{ill|Yuga Daigongen|ja|瑜伽大権現|en}} and others | founded = {{nihongo|Tenpyō 5|733 CE}} | type = Former [[Prefectural Shrine|Prefectural Shrine]] | festivals = {{nihongo|Spring Grand festival|April first Sunday}}
{{nihongo|Autumn Grand festival|November 3}} | main hall = Three-bay-wide [[Hiyoku Irimoya-zukuri]] style | image = [[File:Yuga Jinja 04.jpg|270px]]
Haiden (worship hall) {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=270}} }} [[File:Hiroshige Bizen Tanokuchi.jpg|thumb|[[:en:Hiroshige|Utagawa Hiroshige]]]] '''Yuga Shrine Hongū''' ({{lang|ja|由加神社本宮}}) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en}} located on Mount Yuga in the Kojima area of southern {{ill|Kurashiki|en}}, {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|en}}, Japan. It is a {{ill|Standalone Shrine|ja|単立神社}} unaffiliated with the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|en}}. Its modern status under the shrine ranking system is a former {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社}}. == Overview == Mount Yuga has been a center of {{ill|Iwakura rock|lt=Iwakura faith|ja|磐座信仰}} since ancient times. In 733 CE, the monk {{ill|Gyōki|en}} enshrined an {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-faced Kannon|ja|十一面観音}} statue here, establishing a religious complex that merged Shinto and Buddhism under the name '''{{nihongo|{{ill|Yuga Daigongen|ja|瑜伽大権現|en}}|瑜伽大権現}}''' (Yuga Great Incarnation). According to the history of {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)|lt=Kumano Shrine|ja|熊野神社 (倉敷市林)}}, five disciples of {{ill|En no Gyōja|en}}, a prominent ascetic, brought Kumano deities from {{ill|Kii Province|en}} and established Kumano Shrine in the northern foothills of Mount Yuga. They later founded Nachi Shrine on Mount Yuga as one of the "New Kumano Three Mountains." From the mid-{{ill|Edo period|en}}, the shrine became a place of worship for the {{ill|Ikeda clan (Okayama)|lt=Ikeda clan|ja|池田氏|en}}, rulers of {{ill|Bizen Province|en}}. The Ikeda clan sponsored the construction of the shrine’s buildings and nearby {{ill|Rendaiji (Kurashiki)|lt=Rendaiji|ja|蓮台寺_(倉敷市)|en}}. The shrine is located in a bustling {{ill|Monzen town|ja|門前町}}, with numerous souvenir shops and food stalls. During the New Year period, the shrine attracts many visitors. A specialty of Mount Yuga is the locally famous "ankoro mochi" (sweet red bean rice cakes). In the {{ill|Meiji period|en}}, the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|en}} (separation of Shinto and Buddhism) policy split Yuga Shrine from Rendaiji Temple. Yuga Shrine now has 52 branch shrines across western Japan. After {{ill|World War II|en}}, the shrine and temple informally resumed cooperation, with temple monks conducting prayers at the shrine. However, Yuga Shrine formally resumed independent religious activities in 1997, leading to a dispute with Rendaiji over property and operations. In the Edo period, a practice known as "dual pilgrimage" emerged, encouraging worshippers to visit both {{ill|Yuga Daigongen|ja|瑜伽大権現|en}} and {{ill|Konpira Gongen|en}} in {{ill|Sanuki Province|en}}. == Deities Enshrined == The deities enshrined are {{ill|Hikosasaji no Mikoto|ja|彦狭知命}}, {{ill|Kaminahibi no Kami|ja|直毘神}}, {{ill|Yuga Daigongen|ja|瑜伽大権現|en}} (associated Buddhas: {{ill|Amitābha|ja|阿弥陀如来}}, {{ill|Yakushi Nyorai|ja|薬師如来}}), {{ill|Teokihobufu no Mikoto|ja|手置帆負命}}, {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja|天之御中主神}}, {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞鳴命}}, {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|倉稲魂命}}, {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|菅原道真}}, and {{ill|Watatsumi|lt=Ōwatatsumi no Mikoto|ja|大綿津見命}}. == Cultural Properties == * **main hall** – Built in the early Edo period, it is designated as an {{ill|Important Cultural Property of Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県指定重要文化財}} (1961).{{Cite web|url=http://www.pref.okayama.jp/cgi-bin/bunka/culture/controller/client/resultDetail.cgi?id=174 |title=main hall of Yuga Shrine |publisher=Okayama Prefecture |accessdate=2018-06-24}} == Access == * **By Car:** About 15 minutes from [[Seto-Chuo Expressway]]'s {{ill|Mizushima Interchange|en}}. * **By Taxi:** About 15 minutes from {{ill|Kojima Station|en}} on the {{ill|Seto-Ohashi Line|en}}. * **By Bus:** A limited {{ill|Shimoden Bus|en}} service runs from Kojima Station to Yuga Shrine (twice daily). However, buses do not operate on weekends, holidays, or during the New Year period. == References == {{reflist}} == Further Reading == * Okayama Prefectural High School Social Studies Department History Section, ''Revised Edition: History Walks in Okayama Prefecture,'' Yamakawa Publishing, 1991, pp. 96–97. * On-site informational plaques. == Related Articles == * {{ill|Rendaiji (Kurashiki)|en}} * {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|en}} * {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)|en}} * {{ill|Kotohira-gū|en}} == External Links == * [https://yugasan.or.jp/ Yuga Shrine Hongū Official Website] {{Shinto-stub}} {{Buddhism-stub}} {{Shinto shrines}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Tandoku shrines]] [[Category:Yuga faith]][[Category:new pages]] {{Yuga Faith}}[[Category:Yuga faith]][[Category:new pages]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Kurashiki]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:dd]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Yuga Shrine]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:Mass editing to refresh the first new Wikipedia redirects]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Yuga Shrine]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[ja:由加神社本宮]] {{Shrine | name = Yuga Shrine Main Shrine | location = 2852 Ko-jima Yuga, [[Kurashiki]], [[Okayama Prefecture]] | coordinates = {{Coord|34|30|21.3|N|133|51|3.8|E|region:JP-33_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | Deity = Hikosa no Oshinomikoto, Kamunaobi-no-Kami, Daigongen, among others | Foundation = Traditionally [[Tenpyō]] 5 (733) | Shrine rank = Former Prefectural Shrine (県社) | Annual festival = Spring Grand Festival (First Sunday of April)
Autumn Grand Festival (November 3) | Main hall style = Sanma-sha Hiyoku Irimoya-zukuri | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Yuga Jinja 04.jpg|270px]]
Worship hall {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=270}} }} [[File:Hiroshige Bizen Tanokuchi.jpg|thumb|[[Utagawa Hiroshige]]]] '''Yuga Shrine Main Shrine''' (由加神社本宮, Yuga Jinja Hongū) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in the southern part of [[Kurashiki]], [[Okayama Prefecture]], on [[Yuga Mountain]] in the [[Ko-jima region (Kurashiki)|Ko-jima]] area. Under the {{ill|Modern shrine ranking system|ja|近代社格制度}}, it held the rank of a prefectural shrine and is an independent shrine unaffiliated with the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}}. == Overview == Yuga Mountain has been a site of {{ill|Iwakura rock|ja|磐座信仰|Iwakura rock=Iwakura rock|lt=Iwakura worship}} since ancient times. In [[Tenpyō]] 5 (733), {{ill|Gyōki|ja|行基}} enshrined the {{ill|Eleven-Faced Kannon|ja|十一面観音}} on the mountain, transforming it into a {{ill|syncretic|ja|神仏習合}} site known as '''Yuga Daigongen'''. According to the origin legend of {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)|ja|熊野神社 (倉敷市林)}}, five disciples of {{ill|En no Gyōja|ja|役小角}} brought Kumano worship from {{ill|Kii Province|ja|紀伊国}} to the area during the early {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, founding Kumano Shrine. They later established Nachi Shrine on Yuga Mountain as part of the "New Kumano Three Shrines." In the mid-{{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, the shrine became a place of worship for the {{ill|Ikeda clan|ja|池田氏}}, lords of {{ill|Bizen Province|ja|備前国}}. The Ikeda clan constructed the shrine's buildings and the guest hall of {{ill|Rendai-ji|ja|蓮台寺}}. The approach to the shrine is lined with numerous souvenir shops, forming a {{ill|Monzenmachi|ja|門前町}}. During {{ill|Hatsumōde|ja|初詣}} and other festivals, the area bustles with visitors. The local specialty "Ankoro Mochi" is a notable delicacy sold in this area. With the {{ill|Shinbutsu Bunri|ja|神仏分離}} order in the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}}, Yuga Shrine and Rendai-ji were separated. Today, the shrine oversees 52 branch shrines, primarily in western Japan. In [[1945]] (Shōwa 20), after the dissolution of {{ill|State Shinto|ja|国家神道}}, Yuga Shrine and Rendai-ji began to operate collaboratively, with rituals conducted by monks in the shrine's main hall. However, in [[1997]], Yuga Shrine began its independent religious activities, leading to disputes with Rendai-ji over property use. These conflicts stemmed from disagreements between the then-abbot of Rendai-ji and the chief priest of Yuga Shrine. During the Edo period, pilgrims practiced {{ill|Dual Pilgrimage|ja|両参り}}, visiting both Yuga Daigongen (Rendai-ji on Yuga Mountain) and {{ill|Konpira Gongen|ja|金毘羅大権現|lt=Konpira Daigongen}} ({{ill|Matsuo-dera (Kompira, Kotohira, Kagawa)|ja|松尾寺}} in {{ill|Sanuki Province|ja|讃岐国}}). Consequently, the southern port town of {{ill|Tanokuchi|ja|田の口地区}} developed as a gateway for Yuga Mountain pilgrims. == Deities == The shrine enshrines Hikosa no Oshinomikoto, Kamunaobi-no-Kami, Daigongen (Honji Buddha: {{ill|Amitābha|ja|阿弥陀如来}}, {{ill|Medicine Buddha|ja|薬師如来}}), and others, including: * {{ill|Hikosa-no-Oshinomikoto|ja|彦狭知命}} * {{ill|Kamunaobi-no-Kami|ja|神直日神}} * {{ill|Daigongen|ja|権現}} * {{ill|Teoki-hofu-no-Mikoto|ja|手置帆負命}} * {{ill|Amenominakanushi|ja|天之御中主神}} * {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞鳴命}} * {{ill|Ukanomitama|lt=Ukanomitama-no-Kami|ja|倉稲魂命}} * {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|菅原道真}} * {{ill|Watatsumi|lt=Watatsumi-no-Mikoto|ja|大綿津見命}} == Cultural Properties == * Main Hall - An early Edo period construction. Designated as an Okayama Prefecture Important Cultural Property in [[1961]].{{Cite web|url=http://www.pref.okayama.jp/cgi-bin/bunka/culture/controller/client/resultDetail.cgi?id=174 |title=Yuga Shrine Main Hall |publisher=Okayama Prefecture |accessdate=2018-06-24}} == Access == * Approximately 15 minutes by car from [[Mizushima Interchange]] on the [[Seto-Chūō Expressway]]. * Approximately 15 minutes by taxi from [[Kojima Station]] on the [[Seto-Ōhashi Line]]. * [http://www.shimoden.net/rosen/rosen/oujigatake.html Shimoden Bus Yuga Mountain Line] operates two round-trip services daily from Kojima Station. However, buses do not operate on weekends, holidays, or during New Year's, making them unsuitable for tourism. == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Topics == * {{ill|Rendai-ji|ja|蓮台寺 (倉敷市)}} * {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} * {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)|ja|熊野神社 (倉敷市林)}} * {{ill|Kotohira-gū|ja|金刀比羅宮}} == External Links == * [https://yugasan.or.jp/ Official website of Yuga Shrine Main Shrine] {{shinto-stub}} {{Buddhism-stub}} {{Shinto side}} [[Category:Former prefectural shrines in Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Independent shrines]] [[Category:Yuga faith]][[Category:new pages]] {{Yuga Faith}}[[Category:Yuga faith]][[Category:new pages]] [[Category:Syncretic shrines]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Shrines in Kurashiki]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]] {{citation needed|Yuga Shrine Honmiya}}[[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[ja:由加神社本宮]]

However, buses do not operate on weekends, holidays, or during the New Year period. == References == {{reflist}} == Further Reading == * Okayama Prefectural High School Social Studies Department History Section, ''Revised Edition: History Walks in Okayama Prefecture,'' Yamakawa Publishing, 1991, pp. 96–97. * On-site informational plaques. == Related Articles == * {{ill|Rendaiji (Kurashiki)|en}} * {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|en}} * {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)|en}} * {{ill|Kotohira-gū|en}} == External Links == * [https://yugasan.or.jp/ Yuga Shrine Hongū Official Website] {{Shinto-stub}} {{Buddhism-stub}} {{Shinto shrines}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Tandoku shrines]] [[Category:Yuga faith]][[Category:new pages]] {{Yuga Faith}}[[Category:Yuga faith]][[Category:new pages]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Kurashiki]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:dd]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Yuga Shrine]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:Mass editing to refresh the first new Wikipedia redirects]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Yuga Shrine]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[ja:由加神社本宮]] {{Shrine | name = Yuga Shrine Main Shrine | location = 2852 Ko-jima Yuga, [[Kurashiki]], [[Okayama Prefecture]] | coordinates = {{Coord|34|30|21.3|N|133|51|3.8|E|region:JP-33_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | Deity = Hikosa no Oshinomikoto, Kamunaobi-no-Kami, Daigongen, among others | Foundation = Traditionally [[Tenpyō]] 5 (733) | Shrine rank = Former Prefectural Shrine (県社) | Annual festival = Spring Grand Festival (First Sunday of April)
Autumn Grand Festival (November 3) | Main hall style = Sanma-sha Hiyoku Irimoya-zukuri | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Yuga Jinja 04.jpg|270px]]
Worship hall {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=270}} }} [[File:Hiroshige Bizen Tanokuchi.jpg|thumb|[[Utagawa Hiroshige]]]] '''Yuga Shrine Main Shrine''' (由加神社本宮, Yuga Jinja Hongū) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in the southern part of [[Kurashiki]], [[Okayama Prefecture]], on [[Yuga Mountain]] in the [[Ko-jima region (Kurashiki)|Ko-jima]] area. Under the {{ill|Modern shrine ranking system|ja|近代社格制度}}, it held the rank of a prefectural shrine and is an independent shrine unaffiliated with the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}}. == Overview == Yuga Mountain has been a site of {{ill|Iwakura rock|ja|磐座信仰|Iwakura rock=Iwakura rock|lt=Iwakura worship}} since ancient times. In [[Tenpyō]] 5 (733), {{ill|Gyōki|ja|行基}} enshrined the {{ill|Eleven-Faced Kannon|ja|十一面観音}} on the mountain, transforming it into a {{ill|syncretic|ja|神仏習合}} site known as '''Yuga Daigongen'''. According to the origin legend of {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)|ja|熊野神社 (倉敷市林)}}, five disciples of {{ill|En no Gyōja|ja|役小角}} brought Kumano worship from {{ill|Kii Province|ja|紀伊国}} to the area during the early {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, founding Kumano Shrine. They later established Nachi Shrine on Yuga Mountain as part of the "New Kumano Three Shrines." In the mid-{{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, the shrine became a place of worship for the {{ill|Ikeda clan|ja|池田氏}}, lords of {{ill|Bizen Province|ja|備前国}}. The Ikeda clan constructed the shrine's buildings and the guest hall of {{ill|Rendai-ji|ja|蓮台寺}}. The approach to the shrine is lined with numerous souvenir shops, forming a {{ill|Monzenmachi|ja|門前町}}. During {{ill|Hatsumōde|ja|初詣}} and other festivals, the area bustles with visitors. The local specialty "Ankoro Mochi" is a notable delicacy sold in this area. With the {{ill|Shinbutsu Bunri|ja|神仏分離}} order in the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}}, Yuga Shrine and Rendai-ji were separated. Today, the shrine oversees 52 branch shrines, primarily in western Japan. In [[1945]] (Shōwa 20), after the dissolution of {{ill|State Shinto|ja|国家神道}}, Yuga Shrine and Rendai-ji began to operate collaboratively, with rituals conducted by monks in the shrine's main hall. However, in [[1997]], Yuga Shrine began its independent religious activities, leading to disputes with Rendai-ji over property use. These conflicts stemmed from disagreements between the then-abbot of Rendai-ji and the chief priest of Yuga Shrine. During the Edo period, pilgrims practiced {{ill|Dual Pilgrimage|ja|両参り}}, visiting both Yuga Daigongen (Rendai-ji on Yuga Mountain) and {{ill|Konpira Gongen|ja|金毘羅大権現|lt=Konpira Daigongen}} ({{ill|Matsuo-dera (Kompira, Kotohira, Kagawa)|ja|松尾寺}} in {{ill|Sanuki Province|ja|讃岐国}}). Consequently, the southern port town of {{ill|Tanokuchi|ja|田の口地区}} developed as a gateway for Yuga Mountain pilgrims. == Deities == The shrine enshrines Hikosa no Oshinomikoto, Kamunaobi-no-Kami, Daigongen (Honji Buddha: {{ill|Amitābha|ja|阿弥陀如来}}, {{ill|Medicine Buddha|ja|薬師如来}}), and others, including: * {{ill|Hikosa-no-Oshinomikoto|ja|彦狭知命}} * {{ill|Kamunaobi-no-Kami|ja|神直日神}} * {{ill|Daigongen|ja|権現}} * {{ill|Teoki-hofu-no-Mikoto|ja|手置帆負命}} * {{ill|Amenominakanushi|ja|天之御中主神}} * {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞鳴命}} * {{ill|Ukanomitama|lt=Ukanomitama-no-Kami|ja|倉稲魂命}} * {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|菅原道真}} * {{ill|Watatsumi|lt=Watatsumi-no-Mikoto|ja|大綿津見命}} == Cultural Properties == * Main Hall - An early Edo period construction. Designated as an Okayama Prefecture Important Cultural Property in [[1961]].{{Cite web|url=http://www.pref.okayama.jp/cgi-bin/bunka/culture/controller/client/resultDetail.cgi?id=174 |title=Yuga Shrine Main Hall |publisher=Okayama Prefecture |accessdate=2018-06-24}} == Access == * Approximately 15 minutes by car from [[Mizushima Interchange]] on the [[Seto-Chūō Expressway]]. * Approximately 15 minutes by taxi from [[Kojima Station]] on the [[Seto-Ōhashi Line]]. * [http://www.shimoden.net/rosen/rosen/oujigatake.html Shimoden Bus Yuga Mountain Line] operates two round-trip services daily from Kojima Station. However, buses do not operate on weekends, holidays, or during New Year's, making them unsuitable for tourism. == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Topics == * {{ill|Rendai-ji|ja|蓮台寺 (倉敷市)}} * {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|ja|熊野三山}} * {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)|ja|熊野神社 (倉敷市林)}} * {{ill|Kotohira-gū|ja|金刀比羅宮}} == External Links == * [https://yugasan.or.jp/ Official website of Yuga Shrine Main Shrine] {{shinto-stub}} {{Buddhism-stub}} {{Shinto side}} [[Category:Former prefectural shrines in Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Independent shrines]] [[Category:Yuga faith]][[Category:new pages]] {{Yuga Faith}}[[Category:Yuga faith]][[Category:new pages]] [[Category:Syncretic shrines]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Shrines in Kurashiki]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]] {{citation needed|Yuga Shrine Honmiya}}[[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[ja:由加神社本宮]]

'''Yuga Shrine Main Shrine''' (由加神社本宮, Yuga Jinja Hongū) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in the southern part of [[Kurashiki]], [[Okayama Prefecture]], on [[Yuga Mountain]] in the [[Ko-jima region (Kurashiki)|Ko-jima]] area. Under the {{ill|Modern shrine ranking system|ja|近代社格制度}}, it held the rank of a prefectural shrine and is an independent shrine unaffiliated with the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}}. == Overview == Mount Yuga has been a center of {{ill|Iwakura rock|lt=Iwakura faith|ja|磐座信仰}} since ancient times. In 733 CE, the monk {{ill|Gyōki|en}} enshrined an {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-faced Kannon|ja|十一面観音}} statue here, establishing a religious complex that merged Shinto and Buddhism under the name '''{{nihongo|{{ill|Yuga Daigongen|ja|瑜伽大権現|en}}|瑜伽大権現}}''' (Yuga Great Incarnation). According to the history of {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)|lt=Kumano Shrine|ja|熊野神社 (倉敷市林)}}, five disciples of {{ill|En no Gyōja|en}}, a prominent ascetic, brought Kumano deities from {{ill|Kii Province|en}} and established Kumano Shrine in the northern foothills of Mount Yuga. They later founded Nachi Shrine on Mount Yuga as one of the "New Kumano Three Mountains." From the mid-{{ill|Edo period|en}}, the shrine became a place of worship for the {{ill|Ikeda clan (Okayama)|lt=Ikeda clan|ja|池田氏|en}}, rulers of {{ill|Bizen Province|en}}. The Ikeda clan sponsored the construction of the shrine’s buildings and nearby {{ill|Rendaiji (Kurashiki)|lt=Rendaiji|ja|蓮台寺_(倉敷市)|en}}. The shrine is located in a bustling {{ill|Monzen town|ja|門前町}}, with numerous souvenir shops and food stalls. During the New Year period, the shrine attracts many visitors. A specialty of Mount Yuga is the locally famous "ankoro mochi" (sweet red bean rice cakes). In the {{ill|Meiji period|en}}, the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|en}} (separation of Shinto and Buddhism) policy split Yuga Shrine from Rendaiji Temple. Yuga Shrine now has 52 branch shrines across western Japan. After {{ill|World War II|en}}, the shrine and temple informally resumed cooperation, with temple monks conducting prayers at the shrine. However, Yuga Shrine formally resumed independent religious activities in 1997, leading to a dispute with Rendaiji over property and operations. In the Edo period, a practice known as "dual pilgrimage" emerged, encouraging worshippers to visit both {{ill|Yuga Daigongen|ja|瑜伽大権現|en}} and {{ill|Konpira Gongen|en}} in {{ill|Sanuki Province|en}}. == Overview == Yuga Mountain has been a site of {{ill|Iwakura rock|ja|磐座信仰|Iwakura rock=Iwakura rock|lt=Iwakura worship}} since ancient times. In [[Tenpyō]] 5 (733), {{ill|Gyōki|ja|行基}} enshrined the {{ill|Eleven-Faced Kannon|ja|十一面観音}} on the mountain, transforming it into a {{ill|syncretic|ja|神仏習合}} site known as '''Yuga Daigongen'''. According to the origin legend of {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)|ja|熊野神社 (倉敷市林)}}, five disciples of {{ill|En no Gyōja|ja|役小角}} brought Kumano worship from {{ill|Kii Province|ja|紀伊国}} to the area during the early {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, founding Kumano Shrine. They later established Nachi Shrine on Yuga Mountain as part of the "New Kumano Three Shrines." In the mid-{{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, the shrine became a place of worship for the {{ill|Ikeda clan|ja|池田氏}}, lords of {{ill|Bizen Province|ja|備前国}}. The Ikeda clan constructed the shrine's buildings and the guest hall of {{ill|Rendai-ji|ja|蓮台寺}}. The approach to the shrine is lined with numerous souvenir shops, forming a {{ill|Monzenmachi|ja|門前町}}. During {{ill|Hatsumōde|ja|初詣}} and other festivals, the area bustles with visitors. The local specialty "Ankoro Mochi" is a notable delicacy sold in this area. With the {{ill|Shinbutsu Bunri|ja|神仏分離}} order in the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}}, Yuga Shrine and Rendai-ji were separated. Today, the shrine oversees 52 branch shrines, primarily in western Japan. In [[1945]] (Shōwa 20), after the dissolution of {{ill|State Shinto|ja|国家神道}}, Yuga Shrine and Rendai-ji began to operate collaboratively, with rituals conducted by monks in the shrine's main hall. However, in [[1997]], Yuga Shrine began its independent religious activities, leading to disputes with Rendai-ji over property use. These conflicts stemmed from disagreements between the then-abbot of Rendai-ji and the chief priest of Yuga Shrine. During the Edo period, pilgrims practiced {{ill|Dual Pilgrimage|ja|両参り}}, visiting both Yuga Daigongen (Rendai-ji on Yuga Mountain) and {{ill|Konpira Gongen|ja|金毘羅大権現|lt=Konpira Daigongen}} ({{ill|Matsuo-dera (Kompira, Kotohira, Kagawa)|ja|松尾寺}} in {{ill|Sanuki Province|ja|讃岐国}}). Consequently, the southern port town of {{ill|Tanokuchi|ja|田の口地区}} developed as a gateway for Yuga Mountain pilgrims. == Deities Enshrined == The deities enshrined are {{ill|Hikosasaji no Mikoto|ja|彦狭知命}}, {{ill|Kaminahibi no Kami|ja|直毘神}}, {{ill|Yuga Daigongen|ja|瑜伽大権現|en}} (associated Buddhas: {{ill|Amitābha|ja|阿弥陀如来}}, {{ill|Yakushi Nyorai|ja|薬師如来}}), {{ill|Teokihobufu no Mikoto|ja|手置帆負命}}, {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja|天之御中主神}}, {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞鳴命}}, {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|倉稲魂命}}, {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|菅原道真}}, and {{ill|Watatsumi|lt=Ōwatatsumi no Mikoto|ja|大綿津見命}}. == Deities == The shrine enshrines Hikosa no Oshinomikoto, Kamunaobi-no-Kami, Daigongen (Honji Buddha: {{ill|Amitābha|ja|阿弥陀如来}}, {{ill|Medicine Buddha|ja|薬師如来}}), and others, including: * {{ill|Hikosa-no-Oshinomikoto|ja|彦狭知命}} * {{ill|Kamunaobi-no-Kami|ja|神直日神}} * {{ill|Daigongen|ja|権現}} * {{ill|Teoki-hofu-no-Mikoto|ja|手置帆負命}} * {{ill|Amenominakanushi|ja|天之御中主神}} * {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞鳴命}} * {{ill|Ukanomitama|lt=Ukanomitama-no-Kami|ja|倉稲魂命}} * {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|菅原道真}} * {{ill|Watatsumi|lt=Watatsumi-no-Mikoto|ja|大綿津見命}} == Cultural Properties == * Main Hall - An early Edo period construction. Designated as an Okayama Prefecture Important Cultural Property in [[1961]].{{Cite web|url=http://www.pref.okayama.jp/cgi-bin/bunka/culture/controller/client/resultDetail.cgi?id=174|title=Yuga Shrine Main Hall|publisher=Okayama Prefecture|accessdate=2018-06-24}} == Access == * Approximately 15 minutes by car from [[Mizushima Interchange]] on the [[Seto-Chūō Expressway]]. * Approximately 15 minutes by taxi from [[Kojima Station]] on the [[Seto-Ōhashi Line]]. * [http://www.shimoden.net/rosen/rosen/oujigatake.html Shimoden Bus Yuga Mountain Line] operates two round-trip services daily from Kojima Station. However, buses do not operate on weekends, holidays, or during New Year's, making them unsuitable for tourism. == Cultural Properties == * '''main hall''' – Built in the early Edo period, it is designated as an {{ill|Important Cultural Property of Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県指定重要文化財}} (1961).{{Cite web|url=http://www.pref.okayama.jp/cgi-bin/bunka/culture/controller/client/resultDetail.cgi?id=174 |title=main hall of Yuga Shrine |publisher=Okayama Prefecture |accessdate=2018-06-24}} == Access == * '''By Car:''' About 15 minutes from [[Seto-Chuo Expressway]]'s {{ill|Mizushima Interchange|en}}. * '''By Taxi:''' About 15 minutes from {{ill|Kojima Station|en}} on the {{ill|Seto-Ohashi Line|en}}. * '''By Bus:''' A limited {{ill|Shimoden Bus|en}} service runs from Kojima Station to Yuga Shrine (twice daily). However, buses do not operate on weekends, holidays, or during the New Year period. == References == {{reflist}} == Further Reading == * Okayama Prefectural High School Social Studies Department In the Edo period, a practice known as "dual pilgrimage" emerged, encouraging worshippers to visit both {{ill|Yuga Daigongen|ja|瑜伽大権現|en}} and {{ill|Konpira Gongen|en}} in {{ill|Sanuki Province|en}}. == Overview == Yuga Mountain has been a site of {{ill|Iwakura rock|ja|磐座信仰|Iwakura rock=Iwakura rock|lt=Iwakura worship}} since ancient times. In [[Tenpyō]] 5 (733), {{ill|Gyōki|ja|行基}} enshrined the {{ill|Eleven-Faced Kannon|ja|十一面観音}} on the mountain, transforming it into a {{ill|syncretic|ja|神仏習合}} site known as '''Yuga Daigongen'''. According to the origin legend of {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)|ja|熊野神社 (倉敷市林)}}, five disciples of {{ill|En no Gyōja|ja|役小角}} brought Kumano worship from {{ill|Kii Province|ja|紀伊国}} to the area during the early {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, founding Kumano Shrine. They later established Nachi Shrine on Yuga Mountain as part of the "New Kumano Three Shrines." In the mid-{{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, the shrine became a place of worship for the {{ill|Ikeda clan|ja|池田氏}}, lords of {{ill|Bizen Province|ja|備前国}}. The Ikeda clan constructed the shrine's buildings and the guest hall of {{ill|Rendai-ji|ja|蓮台寺}}. The approach to the shrine is lined with numerous souvenir shops, forming a {{ill|Monzenmachi|ja|門前町}}. During {{ill|Hatsumōde|ja|初詣}} and other festivals, the area bustles with visitors. The local specialty "Ankoro Mochi" is a notable delicacy sold in this area. With the {{ill|Shinbutsu Bunri|ja|神仏分離}} order in the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}}, Yuga Shrine and Rendai-ji were separated. Today, the shrine oversees 52 branch shrines, primarily in western Japan. In [[1945]] (Shōwa 20), after the dissolution of {{ill|State Shinto|ja|国家神道}}, Yuga Shrine and Rendai-ji began to operate collaboratively, with rituals conducted by monks in the shrine's main hall. However, in [[1997]], Yuga Shrine began its independent religious activities, leading to disputes with Rendai-ji over property use. These conflicts stemmed from disagreements between the then-abbot of Rendai-ji and the chief priest of Yuga Shrine. During the Edo period, pilgrims practiced {{ill|Dual Pilgrimage|ja|両参り}}, visiting both Yuga Daigongen (Rendai-ji on Yuga Mountain) and {{ill|Konpira Gongen|ja|金毘羅大権現|lt=Konpira Daigongen}} ({{ill|Matsuo-dera (Kompira, Kotohira, Kagawa)|ja|松尾寺}} in {{ill|Sanuki Province|ja|讃岐国}}). Consequently, the southern port town of {{ill|Tanokuchi|ja|田の口地区}} developed as a gateway for Yuga Mountain pilgrims. == Deities Enshrined == The deities enshrined are {{ill|Hikosasaji no Mikoto|ja|彦狭知命}}, {{ill|Kaminahibi no Kami|ja|直毘神}}, {{ill|Yuga Daigongen|ja|瑜伽大権現|en}} (associated Buddhas: {{ill|Amitābha|ja|阿弥陀如来}}, {{ill|Yakushi Nyorai|ja|薬師如来}}), {{ill|Teokihobufu no Mikoto|ja|手置帆負命}}, {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja|天之御中主神}}, {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞鳴命}}, {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|倉稲魂命}}, {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|菅原道真}}, and {{ill|Watatsumi|lt=Ōwatatsumi no Mikoto|ja|大綿津見命}}. == Deities == The shrine enshrines Hikosa no Oshinomikoto, Kamunaobi-no-Kami, Daigongen (Honji Buddha: {{ill|Amitābha|ja|阿弥陀如来}}, {{ill|Medicine Buddha|ja|薬師如来}}), and others, including: * {{ill|Hikosa-no-Oshinomikoto|ja|彦狭知命}} * {{ill|Kamunaobi-no-Kami|ja|神直日神}} * {{ill|Daigongen|ja|権現}} * {{ill|Teoki-hofu-no-Mikoto|ja|手置帆負命}} * {{ill|Amenominakanushi|ja|天之御中主神}} * {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞鳴命}} * {{ill|Ukanomitama|lt=Ukanomitama-no-Kami|ja|倉稲魂命}} * {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|菅原道真}} * {{ill|Watatsumi|lt=Watatsumi-no-Mikoto|ja|大綿津見命}} == Cultural Properties == * Main Hall - An early Edo period construction. Designated as an Okayama Prefecture Important Cultural Property in [[1961]].{{Cite web|url=http://www.pref.okayama.jp/cgi-bin/bunka/culture/controller/client/resultDetail.cgi?id=174|title=Yuga Shrine Main Hall|publisher=Okayama Prefecture|accessdate=2018-06-24}} == Access == * Approximately 15 minutes by car from [[Mizushima Interchange]] on the [[Seto-Chūō Expressway]]. * Approximately 15 minutes by taxi from [[Kojima Station]] on the [[Seto-Ōhashi Line]]. * [http://www.shimoden.net/rosen/rosen/oujigatake.html Shimoden Bus Yuga Mountain Line] operates two round-trip services daily from Kojima Station. However, buses do not operate on weekends, holidays, or during New Year's, making them unsuitable for tourism. == Cultural Properties == * '''main hall''' – Built in the early Edo period, it is designated as an {{ill|Important Cultural Property of Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県指定重要文化財}} (1961).{{Cite web|url=http://www.pref.okayama.jp/cgi-bin/bunka/culture/controller/client/resultDetail.cgi?id=174 |title=main hall of Yuga Shrine |publisher=Okayama Prefecture |accessdate=2018-06-24}} == Access == * '''By Car:''' About 15 minutes from [[Seto-Chuo Expressway]]'s {{ill|Mizushima Interchange|en}}. * '''By Taxi:''' About 15 minutes from {{ill|Kojima Station|en}} on the {{ill|Seto-Ohashi Line|en}}. * '''By Bus:''' A limited {{ill|Shimoden Bus|en}} service runs from Kojima Station to Yuga Shrine (twice daily). However, buses do not operate on weekends, holidays, or during the New Year period. == References == {{reflist}} == Further Reading == * Okayama Prefectural High School Social Studies Department It is a {{ill|Standalone Shrine|ja|単立神社}} unaffiliated with the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|en}}. Its modern status under the shrine ranking system is a former {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社}}. == Overview == Mount Yuga has been a center of {{ill|Iwakura rock|lt=Iwakura faith|ja|磐座信仰}} since ancient times. In 733 CE, the monk {{ill|Gyōki|en}} enshrined an {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-faced Kannon|ja|十一面観音}} statue here, establishing a religious complex that merged Shinto and Buddhism under the name '''{{nihongo|{{ill|Yuga Daigongen|ja|瑜伽大権現|en}}|瑜伽大権現}}''' (Yuga Great Incarnation). According to the history of {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)|lt=Kumano Shrine|ja|熊野神社 (倉敷市林)}}, five disciples of {{ill|En no Gyōja|en}}, a prominent ascetic, brought Kumano deities from {{ill|Kii Province|en}} and established Kumano Shrine in the northern foothills of Mount Yuga. The shrine is located in a bustling {{ill|Monzen town|ja|門前町}}, with numerous souvenir shops and food stalls. During the New Year period, the shrine attracts many visitors. A specialty of Mount Yuga is the locally famous "ankoro mochi" (sweet red bean rice cakes). In the {{ill|Meiji period|en}}, the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|en}} (separation of Shinto and Buddhism) policy split Yuga Shrine from Rendaiji Temple. Yuga Shrine now has 52 branch shrines across western Japan. After {{ill|World War II|en}}, the shrine and temple informally resumed cooperation, with temple monks conducting prayers at the shrine. In the Edo period, a practice known as "dual pilgrimage" emerged, encouraging worshippers to visit both {{ill|Yuga Daigongen|ja|瑜伽大権現|en}} and {{ill|Konpira Gongen|en}} in {{ill|Sanuki Province|ja|讃岐国|en}}. == Deities Enshrined == The deities enshrined are {{ill|Hikosasaji no Mikoto|ja|彦狭知命}}, {{ill|Kaminahibi no Kami|ja|直毘神}}, {{ill|Yuga Daigongen|ja|瑜伽大権現|en}} (associated Buddhas: {{ill|Amitābha|ja|阿弥陀如来}}, {{ill|Yakushi Nyorai|ja|薬師如来}}), {{ill|Teokihobufu no Mikoto|ja|手置帆負命}}, {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja|天之御中主神}}, {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞鳴命}}, {{ill|Ukanomitama|ja|倉稲魂命}}, {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|菅原道真}}, and {{ill|Watatsumi|lt=Ōwatatsumi no Mikoto|ja|大綿津見命}}. == Deities == The shrine enshrines Hikosa no Oshinomikoto, Kamunaobi-no-Kami, Daigongen (Honji Buddha: {{ill|Amitābha|ja|阿弥陀如来}}, {{ill|Yakushi Nyorai|ja|薬師如来}}), and others, including: * {{ill|Hikosa-no-Oshinomikoto|ja|彦狭知命}} * {{ill|Kamunaobi-no-Kami|ja|神直日神}} * {{ill|Yuga Daigongen|ja|瑜伽大権現|en}} (associated Buddhas: {{ill|Amitābha|ja|阿弥陀如来}}, {{ill|Yakushi Nyorai|ja|薬師如来}}) * {{ill|Teokihobufu no Mikoto|ja|手置帆負命}} * {{ill|Amenominakanushi|ja|天之御中主神}} * {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞鳴命}} * {{ill|Ukanomitama|lt=Ukanomitama-no-Kami|ja|倉稲魂命}} * {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|菅原道真}} * {{ill|Watatsumi|lt=Watatsumi-no-Mikoto|ja|大綿津見命}} == Cultural Properties == * Main Hall - An early Edo period construction. Designated as an Okayama Prefecture Important Cultural Property in [[1961]].{{Cite web|url=http://www.pref.okayama.jp/cgi-bin/bunka/culture/controller/client/resultDetail.cgi?id=174|title=Yuga Shrine Main Hall|publisher=Okayama Prefecture|accessdate=2018-06-24}} == Access == * Approximately 15 minutes by car from [[Mizushima Interchange]] on the [[Seto-Chūō Expressway]]. * Approximately 15 minutes by taxi from [[Kojima Station]] on the [[Seto-Ōhashi Line]]. * [http://www.shimoden.net/rosen/rosen/oujigatake.html Shimoden Bus Yuga Mountain Line] operates two round-trip services daily from Kojima Station. However, buses do not operate on weekends, holidays, or during New Year's, making them unsuitable for tourism. == Cultural Properties == * '''main hall''' – Built in the early Edo period, it is designated as an {{ill|Important Cultural Property of Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県指定重要文化財}} (1961).{{Cite web|url=http://www.pref.okayama.jp/cgi-bin/bunka/culture/controller/client/resultDetail.cgi?id=174 |title=main hall of Yuga Shrine |publisher=Okayama Prefecture |accessdate=2018-06-24}} == Access == * '''By Car:''' About 15 minutes from [[Seto-Chuo Expressway]]'s {{ill|Mizushima Interchange|en}}. * '''By Taxi:''' About 15 minutes from {{ill|Kojima Station|en}} on the {{ill|Seto-Ohashi Line|en}}. * '''By Bus:''' A limited {{ill|Shimoden Bus|en}} service runs from Kojima Station to Yuga Shrine (twice daily). However, buses do not operate on weekends, holidays, or during the New Year period. == References == {{reflist}} == Further Reading == * Okayama Prefectural High School Social Studies Department History Section, ''Revised Edition: History Walks in Okayama Prefecture,'' Yamakawa Publishing, 1991, pp. 96–97. * On-site informational plaques. == Related Articles == * {{ill|Rendaiji (Kurashiki)|en}} * {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|en}} * {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)|en}} * {{ill|Kotohira-gū|en}} == External Links == * [https://yugasan.or.jp/ Yuga Shrine Hongū Official Website]
