{{nihongo|Autumn Grand festival|November 3}} | main hall = Three-bay-wide [[Hiyoku Irimoya-zukuri]] style | image = [[File:Yuga Jinja 04.jpg|270px]]
Haiden (worship hall) {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=270}} | name = Yuga Shrine Main Shrine | location = 2852 Ko-jima Yuga, [[Kurashiki]], [[Okayama Prefecture]] | coordinates = {{Coord|34|30|21.3|N|133|51|3.8|E|region:JP-33_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = Hikosa no Oshinomikoto, Kamunaobi-no-Kami, Daigongen, among others | Foundation = Traditionally [[Tenpyō]] 5 (733) | Shrine rank = Former Prefectural Shrine (県社) | Annual festival = Spring Grand Festival (First Sunday of April)
Autumn Grand Festival (November 3) | Main hall style = Sanma-sha Hiyoku Irimoya-zukuri | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Yuga Jinja 04.jpg|270px]]
Worship hall {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=270}} }}[[File:Hiroshige Bizen Tanokuchi.jpg|thumb|[[:en:Hiroshige|Utagawa Hiroshige]]]] '''Yuga Shrine Hongū''' ({{lang|ja|由加神社本宮}}) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en}} located on [[Yuga Mountain]] in the Kojima area of southern {{ill|Kurashiki|en|12=draft}}, {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|en}}, Japan. It is a {{ill|Standalone Shrine|ja|単立神社|12=draft}} unaffiliated with the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|en}}. Its modern status under the shrine ranking system is a former {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社|12=draft}}. == Overview == Mount Yuga has been a center of {{ill|Iwakura rock|lt=Iwakura faith|ja|磐座信仰|12=draft}} since ancient times. In 733 CE, the monk {{ill|Gyōki|en}} enshrined an {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-faced Kannon|ja|十一面観音|12=draft}} statue here, establishing a religious complex that merged Shinto and Buddhism under the name '''{{nihongo|{{ill|Yuga Daigongen|ja|瑜伽大権現|en|12=draft}}|瑜伽大権現}}''' (Yuga Great Incarnation). According to the history of {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)|lt=Kumano Shrine|ja|熊野神社 (倉敷市林)|12=draft}}, five disciples of {{ill|En no Gyōja|en}}, a prominent ascetic, brought Kumano deities from {{ill|Kii Province|en|12=draft}} and established Kumano Shrine in the northern foothills of Mount Yuga. They later founded Nachi Shrine on Mount Yuga as one of the "New Kumano Three Mountains." From the mid-{{ill|Edo period|en}}, the shrine became a place of worship for the {{ill|Ikeda clan (Okayama)|lt=Ikeda clan|ja|池田氏|en|12=draft}}, rulers of {{ill|Bizen Province|en}}. The Ikeda clan sponsored the construction of the shrine’s buildings and nearby {{ill|Rendaiji (Kurashiki)|lt=Rendaiji|ja|蓮台寺_(倉敷市)|en|12=draft}}. The shrine is located in a bustling {{ill|Monzen town|ja|門前町|12=draft}}, with numerous souvenir shops and food stalls. During the New Year period, the shrine attracts many visitors. A specialty of Mount Yuga is the locally famous "ankoro mochi" (sweet red bean rice cakes). In the {{ill|Meiji period|en}}, the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|en|12=draft}} (separation of Shinto and Buddhism) policy split Yuga Shrine from Rendaiji Temple. Yuga Shrine now has 52 branch shrines across western Japan. After {{ill|World War II|en}}, the shrine and temple informally resumed cooperation, with temple monks conducting prayers at the shrine. However, Yuga Shrine formally resumed independent religious activities in 1997, leading to a dispute with Rendaiji over property and operations. In the Edo period, a practice known as "dual pilgrimage" emerged, encouraging worshippers to visit both {{ill|Yuga Daigongen|ja|瑜伽大権現|en|12=draft}} and {{ill|Konpira Gongen|en}} in {{ill|Sanuki Province|ja|讃岐国|en|12=draft}}. == Deities == The shrine enshrines Hikosa no Oshinomikoto, Kamunaobi-no-Kami, Daigongen (Honji Buddha: {{ill|Amitābha|ja|阿弥陀如来|12=draft}}, {{ill|Yakushi Nyorai|ja|薬師如来|12=draft}}), and others, including: * {{ill|Hikosa-no-Oshinomikoto|ja|彦狭知命|12=draft}} * {{ill|Kamunaobi-no-Kami|ja|神直日神|12=draft}} * {{ill|Yuga Daigongen|ja|瑜伽大権現|en|12=draft}} (associated Buddhas: {{ill|Amitābha|ja|阿弥陀如来|12=draft}}, {{ill|Yakushi Nyorai|ja|薬師如来|12=draft}}) * {{ill|Teokihobufu no Mikoto|ja|手置帆負命|12=draft}} * {{ill|Amenominakanushi|ja|天之御中主神|12=draft}} * {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞鳴命|12=draft}} * {{ill|Ukanomitama|lt=Ukanomitama-no-Kami|ja|倉稲魂命|12=draft}} * {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|Sugawara no Michizane|12=draft}} * {{ill|Watatsumi|lt=Watatsumi-no-Mikoto|ja|大綿津見命|12=draft}} * == Cultural Properties == * '''main hall''' – Built in the early Edo period, it is designated as an {{ill|Important Cultural Property of Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県指定重要文化財|12=draft}} (1961).{{Cite web|url=http://www.pref.okayama.jp/cgi-bin/bunka/culture/controller/client/resultDetail.cgi?id=174 |title=main hall of Yuga Shrine |publisher=Okayama Prefecture |accessdate=2018-06-24}} == Access == * '''By Car:''' About 15 minutes from [[Seto-Chuo Expressway]]'s {{ill|Mizushima Interchange|en}}. * '''By Taxi:''' About 15 minutes from {{ill|Kojima Station|en|12=draft}} on the {{ill|Seto-Ohashi Line|en}}. * '''By Bus:''' A limited {{ill|Shimoden Bus|en|12=draft}} service runs from Kojima Station to Yuga Shrine (twice daily). However, buses do not operate on weekends, holidays, or during the New Year period. == References == {{reflist}} == Further Reading == * Okayama Prefectural High School Social Studies Department History Section, ''Revised Edition: History Walks in Okayama Prefecture,'' Yamakawa Publishing, 1991, pp. 96–97. * On-site informational plaques. == Related Articles == * {{ill|Rendaiji (Kurashiki)|en}} * {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|en|12=draft}} * {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)|en}} * {{ill|Kotohira-gū|en|12=draft}} == External Links == * [https://yugasan.or.jp/ Yuga Shrine Hongū Official Website] * == References == {{Reflist}} * == External Links == * [https://yugasan.or.jp/ Official website of Yuga Shrine Main Shrine] {{shinto-stub}} {{Buddhism-stub}} {{Yuga Faith}} {{citation needed|Yuga Shrine Honmiya}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Tandoku shrines]] [[Category:Yuga faith]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Yuga Shrine]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Kurashiki]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:Mass editing to refresh the first new Wikipedia redirects]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:Former prefectural shrines in Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Independent shrines]] [[Category:Syncretic shrines]] [[Category:Shrines in Kurashiki]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[Category:New]] }} [[sn:Yuga Shrine]] [[ja:由加神社本宮]] [[en:Yuga Shrine]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Yuga Shrine}}

{{otheruses}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Yuga Shrine Hongū | location = 2852 Yuga, Kojima, Kurashiki City, [[Okayama Prefecture]] | type = 34.505917 | longitude = 133.851056 | map = Japan Okayama | deity = {{ill|Hikosasaji no Mikoto|ja|彦狭知命|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kaminahibi no Kami|ja|直毘神|12=draft}}, {{ill|Yuga Daigongen|ja|瑜伽大権現|en|12=draft}} and others | founded = {{nihongo|Tenpyō 5|733 CE}} | type = Former [[Prefectural Shrine|Prefectural Shrine]] | festivals = {{nihongo|Spring Grand festival|April first Sunday}}
{{nihongo|Autumn Grand festival|November 3}} | main hall = Three-bay-wide [[Hiyoku Irimoya-zukuri]] style | image = [[File:Yuga Jinja 04.jpg|270px]]
Haiden (worship hall) {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=270}} | name = Yuga Shrine Main Shrine | location = 2852 Ko-jima Yuga, [[Kurashiki]], [[Okayama Prefecture]] | coordinates = {{Coord|34|30|21.3|N|133|51|3.8|E|region:JP-33_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = Hikosa no Oshinomikoto, Kamunaobi-no-Kami, Daigongen, among others | Foundation = Traditionally [[Tenpyō]] 5 (733) | Shrine rank = Former Prefectural Shrine (県社) | Annual festival = Spring Grand Festival (First Sunday of April)
Autumn Grand Festival (November 3) | Main hall style = Sanma-sha Hiyoku Irimoya-zukuri | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Yuga Jinja 04.jpg|270px]]
Worship hall {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=270}} }}[[File:Hiroshige Bizen Tanokuchi.jpg|thumb|[[:en:Hiroshige|Utagawa Hiroshige]]]] '''Yuga Shrine Hongū''' ({{lang|ja|由加神社本宮}}) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en}} located on [[Yuga Mountain]] in the Kojima area of southern {{ill|Kurashiki|en|12=draft}}, {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|en}}, Japan. It is a {{ill|Standalone Shrine|ja|単立神社|12=draft}} unaffiliated with the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|en}}. Its modern status under the shrine ranking system is a former {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社|12=draft}}. == Overview == Mount Yuga has been a center of {{ill|Iwakura rock|lt=Iwakura faith|ja|磐座信仰|12=draft}} since ancient times. In 733 CE, the monk {{ill|Gyōki|en}} enshrined an {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-faced Kannon|ja|十一面観音|12=draft}} statue here, establishing a religious complex that merged Shinto and Buddhism under the name '''{{nihongo|{{ill|Yuga Daigongen|ja|瑜伽大権現|en|12=draft}}|瑜伽大権現}}''' (Yuga Great Incarnation). According to the history of {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)|lt=Kumano Shrine|ja|熊野神社 (倉敷市林)|12=draft}}, five disciples of {{ill|En no Gyōja|en}}, a prominent ascetic, brought Kumano deities from {{ill|Kii Province|en|12=draft}} and established Kumano Shrine in the northern foothills of Mount Yuga. They later founded Nachi Shrine on Mount Yuga as one of the "New Kumano Three Mountains." From the mid-{{ill|Edo period|en}}, the shrine became a place of worship for the {{ill|Ikeda clan (Okayama)|lt=Ikeda clan|ja|池田氏|en|12=draft}}, rulers of {{ill|Bizen Province|en}}. The Ikeda clan sponsored the construction of the shrine’s buildings and nearby {{ill|Rendaiji (Kurashiki)|lt=Rendaiji|ja|蓮台寺_(倉敷市)|en|12=draft}}. The shrine is located in a bustling {{ill|Monzen town|ja|門前町|12=draft}}, with numerous souvenir shops and food stalls. During the New Year period, the shrine attracts many visitors. A specialty of Mount Yuga is the locally famous "ankoro mochi" (sweet red bean rice cakes). In the {{ill|Meiji period|en}}, the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|en|12=draft}} (separation of Shinto and Buddhism) policy split Yuga Shrine from Rendaiji Temple. Yuga Shrine now has 52 branch shrines across western Japan. After {{ill|World War II|en}}, the shrine and temple informally resumed cooperation, with temple monks conducting prayers at the shrine. However, Yuga Shrine formally resumed independent religious activities in 1997, leading to a dispute with Rendaiji over property and operations. In the Edo period, a practice known as "dual pilgrimage" emerged, encouraging worshippers to visit both {{ill|Yuga Daigongen|ja|瑜伽大権現|en|12=draft}} and {{ill|Konpira Gongen|en}} in {{ill|Sanuki Province|ja|讃岐国|en|12=draft}}. == Deities == The shrine enshrines Hikosa no Oshinomikoto, Kamunaobi-no-Kami, Daigongen (Honji Buddha: {{ill|Amitābha|ja|阿弥陀如来|12=draft}}, {{ill|Yakushi Nyorai|ja|薬師如来|12=draft}}), and others, including: * {{ill|Hikosa-no-Oshinomikoto|ja|彦狭知命|12=draft}} * {{ill|Kamunaobi-no-Kami|ja|神直日神|12=draft}} * {{ill|Yuga Daigongen|ja|瑜伽大権現|en|12=draft}} (associated Buddhas: {{ill|Amitābha|ja|阿弥陀如来|12=draft}}, {{ill|Yakushi Nyorai|ja|薬師如来|12=draft}}) * {{ill|Teokihobufu no Mikoto|ja|手置帆負命|12=draft}} * {{ill|Amenominakanushi|ja|天之御中主神|12=draft}} * {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞鳴命|12=draft}} * {{ill|Ukanomitama|lt=Ukanomitama-no-Kami|ja|倉稲魂命|12=draft}} * {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|Sugawara no Michizane|12=draft}} * {{ill|Watatsumi|lt=Watatsumi-no-Mikoto|ja|大綿津見命|12=draft}} * == Cultural Properties == * '''main hall''' – Built in the early Edo period, it is designated as an {{ill|Important Cultural Property of Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県指定重要文化財|12=draft}} (1961).{{Cite web|url=http://www.pref.okayama.jp/cgi-bin/bunka/culture/controller/client/resultDetail.cgi?id=174 |title=main hall of Yuga Shrine |publisher=Okayama Prefecture |accessdate=2018-06-24}} == Access == * '''By Car:''' About 15 minutes from [[Seto-Chuo Expressway]]'s {{ill|Mizushima Interchange|en}}. * '''By Taxi:''' About 15 minutes from {{ill|Kojima Station|en|12=draft}} on the {{ill|Seto-Ohashi Line|en}}. * '''By Bus:''' A limited {{ill|Shimoden Bus|en|12=draft}} service runs from Kojima Station to Yuga Shrine (twice daily). However, buses do not operate on weekends, holidays, or during the New Year period. == References == {{reflist}} == Further Reading == * Okayama Prefectural High School Social Studies Department History Section, ''Revised Edition: History Walks in Okayama Prefecture,'' Yamakawa Publishing, 1991, pp. 96–97. * On-site informational plaques. == Related Articles == * {{ill|Rendaiji (Kurashiki)|en}} * {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|en|12=draft}} * {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)|en}} * {{ill|Kotohira-gū|en|12=draft}} == External Links == * [https://yugasan.or.jp/ Yuga Shrine Hongū Official Website] * == References == {{Reflist}} * == External Links == * [https://yugasan.or.jp/ Official website of Yuga Shrine Main Shrine] {{shinto-stub}} {{Buddhism-stub}} {{Yuga Faith}} {{citation needed|Yuga Shrine Honmiya}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Tandoku shrines]] [[Category:Yuga faith]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Yuga Shrine]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Kurashiki]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:Former prefectural shrines in Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Independent shrines]] [[Category:Syncretic shrines]] [[Category:Shrines in Kurashiki]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[Category:New]] }} [[sn:Yuga Shrine]] [[ja:由加神社本宮]] [[en:Yuga Shrine]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Yuga Shrine}}

revision.text:
{{otheruses}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Yuga Shrine Hongū | location = 2852 Yuga, Kojima, Kurashiki City, [[Okayama Prefecture]] | type = 34.505917 | longitude = 133.851056 | map = Japan Okayama | deity = {{ill|Hikosasaji no Mikoto|ja|彦狭知命|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kaminahibi no Kami|ja|直毘神|12=draft}}, {{ill|Yuga Daigongen|ja|瑜伽大権現|en|12=draft}} and others | founded = {{nihongo|Tenpyō 5|733 CE}} | type = Former [[Prefectural Shrine|Prefectural Shrine]] | festivals = {{nihongo|Spring Grand festival|April first Sunday}}
{{nihongo|Autumn Grand festival|November 3}} | main hall = Three-bay-wide [[Hiyoku Irimoya-zukuri]] style | image = [[File:Yuga Jinja 04.jpg|270px]]
Haiden (worship hall) {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=270}} | name = Yuga Shrine Main Shrine | location = 2852 Ko-jima Yuga, [[Kurashiki]], [[Okayama Prefecture]] | coordinates = {{Coord|34|30|21.3|N|133|51|3.8|E|region:JP-33_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = Hikosa no Oshinomikoto, Kamunaobi-no-Kami, Daigongen, among others | Foundation = Traditionally [[Tenpyō]] 5 (733) | Shrine rank = Former Prefectural Shrine (県社) | Annual festival = Spring Grand Festival (First Sunday of April)
Autumn Grand Festival (November 3) | Main hall style = Sanma-sha Hiyoku Irimoya-zukuri | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Yuga Jinja 04.jpg|270px]]
Worship hall {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=270}} }}[[File:Hiroshige Bizen Tanokuchi.jpg|thumb|[[:en:Hiroshige|Utagawa Hiroshige]]]] '''Yuga Shrine Hongū''' ({{lang|ja|由加神社本宮}}) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en}} located on [[Yuga Mountain]] in the Kojima area of southern {{ill|Kurashiki|en|12=draft}}, {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|en}}, Japan. It is a {{ill|Standalone Shrine|ja|単立神社|12=draft}} unaffiliated with the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|en}}. Its modern status under the shrine ranking system is a former {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社|12=draft}}. == Overview == Mount Yuga has been a center of {{ill|Iwakura rock|lt=Iwakura faith|ja|磐座信仰|12=draft}} since ancient times. In 733 CE, the monk {{ill|Gyōki|en}} enshrined an {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-faced Kannon|ja|十一面観音|12=draft}} statue here, establishing a religious complex that merged Shinto and Buddhism under the name '''{{nihongo|{{ill|Yuga Daigongen|ja|瑜伽大権現|en|12=draft}}|瑜伽大権現}}''' (Yuga Great Incarnation). According to the history of {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)|lt=Kumano Shrine|ja|熊野神社 (倉敷市林)|12=draft}}, five disciples of {{ill|En no Gyōja|en}}, a prominent ascetic, brought Kumano deities from {{ill|Kii Province|en|12=draft}} and established Kumano Shrine in the northern foothills of Mount Yuga. They later founded Nachi Shrine on Mount Yuga as one of the "New Kumano Three Mountains." From the mid-{{ill|Edo period|en}}, the shrine became a place of worship for the {{ill|Ikeda clan (Okayama)|lt=Ikeda clan|ja|池田氏|en|12=draft}}, rulers of {{ill|Bizen Province|en}}. The Ikeda clan sponsored the construction of the shrine’s buildings and nearby {{ill|Rendaiji (Kurashiki)|lt=Rendaiji|ja|蓮台寺_(倉敷市)|en|12=draft}}. The shrine is located in a bustling {{ill|Monzen town|ja|門前町|12=draft}}, with numerous souvenir shops and food stalls. During the New Year period, the shrine attracts many visitors. A specialty of Mount Yuga is the locally famous "ankoro mochi" (sweet red bean rice cakes). In the {{ill|Meiji period|en}}, the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|en|12=draft}} (separation of Shinto and Buddhism) policy split Yuga Shrine from Rendaiji Temple. Yuga Shrine now has 52 branch shrines across western Japan. After {{ill|World War II|en}}, the shrine and temple informally resumed cooperation, with temple monks conducting prayers at the shrine. However, Yuga Shrine formally resumed independent religious activities in 1997, leading to a dispute with Rendaiji over property and operations. In the Edo period, a practice known as "dual pilgrimage" emerged, encouraging worshippers to visit both {{ill|Yuga Daigongen|ja|瑜伽大権現|en|12=draft}} and {{ill|Konpira Gongen|en}} in {{ill|Sanuki Province|ja|讃岐国|en|12=draft}}. == Deities == The shrine enshrines Hikosa no Oshinomikoto, Kamunaobi-no-Kami, Daigongen (Honji Buddha: {{ill|Amitābha|ja|阿弥陀如来|12=draft}}, {{ill|Yakushi Nyorai|ja|薬師如来|1=Bhaisajyaguru|lt=Yakushi Nyorai (Me)dicine Buddha|12=draft}}), and others, including: * {{ill|Hikosa-no-Oshinomikoto|ja|彦狭知命|12=draft}} * {{ill|Kamunaobi-no-Kami|ja|神直日神|12=draft}} * {{ill|Yuga Daigongen|ja|瑜伽大権現|en|12=draft}} (associated Buddhas: {{ill|Amitābha|ja|阿弥陀如来|12=draft}}, {{ill|Yakushi Nyorai|ja|薬師如来|1=Bhaisajyaguru|lt=Yakushi Nyorai (Me)dicine Buddha|12=draft}}) * {{ill|Teokihobufu no Mikoto|ja|手置帆負命|1=Sanuki Inbe clan|lt=Teokihobufu no Mikoto|2=ja|3=讃岐忌部氏|12=draft}} * {{ill|Amenominakanushi|ja|天之御中主神|12=draft}} * {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞鳴命|12=draft}} * {{ill|Ukanomitama|lt=Ukanomitama-no-Kami|ja|倉稲魂命|12=draft}} * {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|Sugawara no Michizane|12=draft}} * {{ill|Watatsumi|lt=Watatsumi-no-Mikoto|ja|大綿津見命|12=draft}} * == Cultural Properties == * '''main hall''' – Built in the early Edo period, it is designated as an {{ill|Important Cultural Property of Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県指定重要文化財|12=draft}} (1961).{{Cite web|url=http://www.pref.okayama.jp/cgi-bin/bunka/culture/controller/client/resultDetail.cgi?id=174 |title=main hall of Yuga Shrine |publisher=Okayama Prefecture |accessdate=2018-06-24}} == Access == * '''By Car:''' About 15 minutes from [[Seto-Chuo Expressway]]'s {{ill|Mizushima Interchange|en}}. * '''By Taxi:''' About 15 minutes from {{ill|Kojima Station|en|12=draft}} on the {{ill|Seto-Ohashi Line|en}}. * '''By Bus:''' A limited {{ill|Shimoden Bus|en|12=draft}} service runs from Kojima Station to Yuga Shrine (twice daily). However, buses do not operate on weekends, holidays, or during the New Year period. == References == {{reflist}} == Further Reading == * Okayama Prefectural High School Social Studies Department History Section, ''Revised Edition: History Walks in Okayama Prefecture,'' Yamakawa Publishing, 1991, pp. 96–97. * On-site informational plaques. == Related Articles == * {{ill|Rendaiji (Kurashiki)|en}} * {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|en|12=draft}} * {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)|en}} * {{ill|Kotohira-gū|en|12=draft}} == External Links == * [https://yugasan.or.jp/ Yuga Shrine Hongū Official Website] * == References == {{Reflist}} * == External Links == * [https://yugasan.or.jp/ Official website of Yuga Shrine Main Shrine] {{shinto-stub}} {{Buddhism-stub}} {{Yuga Faith}} {{citation needed|Yuga Shrine Honmiya}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Tandoku shrines]] [[Category:Yuga faith]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Yuga Shrine]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Kurashiki]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:Former prefectural shrines in Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Independent shrines]] [[Category:Syncretic shrines]] [[Category:Shrines in Kurashiki]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[Category:New]] }} [[sn:Yuga Shrine]] [[ja:由加神社本宮]] [[en:Yuga Shrine]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Yuga Shrine}}

revision.text:
{{神社 |名称=由加神社本宮 |所在地= 岡山県倉敷市児島由加2852番地 |緯度度 = 34|緯度分 = 30|緯度秒 = 21.3 |経度度 = 133|経度分 = 51|経度秒 = 3.8 |地図= Japan Okayama |祭神=彦狭知命、神直日神、大権現ほか |創建=（伝）[[天平]]5年（[[733年]]） |社格=県社 |例祭=春季大祭（4月第一日曜）
秋季大祭（11月3日） |本殿=三間社[[比翼入母屋造]] |画像=[[ファイル:Yuga Jinja 04.jpg|270px]]
拝殿 {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=270}} }} [[File:Hiroshige Bizen Tanokuchi.jpg|thumb|[[歌川広重]]]] '''由加神社本宮'''（ゆがじんじゃほんぐう）は、[[岡山県]][[倉敷市]]南部にあたる[[児島地域 (倉敷市)|児島]]の[[由加山]]にある[[神社]]である。[[近代社格制度|旧社格]]は県社。[[神社本庁]]の管轄に属さない[[単立神社]]。 == 概要 == 由加山は太古より[[磐座信仰]]が行われていたとされる。[[天平]]5年（[[733年]]）[[行基]]によりこの地に[[十一面観音]]が祀られ、神社仏閣が一体となった'''[[瑜伽大権現]]（由加大権現）'''と呼ばれる[[神仏習合]]の山となったという。北麓にある[[熊野神社 (倉敷市林)|熊野神社]]の縁起によれば、[[奈良時代]]の初めに[[役小角|役行者]]の高弟5人が[[紀伊国|紀州]][[熊野]]より[[熊野本宮大社]]を捧持してこの地に熊野神社を興し、その後、新熊野三山の一つとして由加山に那智宮を開いたとしている。 [[江戸時代]]中期より[[備前藩]]主[[池田氏]]の祈願所となり、正月・5月・9月には藩主自ら参拝していた。池田氏は当神社の社殿や、蓮台寺の客殿を造営した。 参道には多くの土産物店等が軒を連ね[[門前町]]を形成しており、初詣などの時期には多くの参拝者で賑わう。なお、この門前町で売られている「[[あんころ餅]]」は由加山の名物である。 [[明治時代]]になると[[神仏分離|神仏分離令]]により、由加神社と[[蓮台寺 (倉敷市)|蓮台寺]]に分離された。現在は西日本を中心に全国に末社52社を有する。 [[第二次世界大戦]]後の[[国家神道]]解体により、形式的には分離したままながら、僧職が本殿で祈祷を行う等事実上明治以前の形態に復し、蓮台寺により一体的に運営されていた。このため由加神社としての宗教法人資格は一応保持、神職を配置しつつも、事実上休眠状態に近かった。 1997年3月3日、由加神社が一部の建物を占拠し、宗教法人としての独自活動を開始した。これ以降蓮台寺と由加神社は対立関係となり、蓮台寺が建物の明け渡しを求めるなど裁判沙汰にもなるが、由加神社側が独自の宗教活動を開始した経緯は当時の兄（蓮台寺住職）と弟（由加神社宮司）の間の対立が原因とされている。 江戸時代には、瑜伽大権現（瑜伽山蓮台寺）と[[讃岐国]]の[[金毘羅権現|金毘羅大権現]]（金光院[[松尾寺 (香川県琴平町)|松尾寺]]）を両方参拝するという'''[[両参り]]'''といわれる慣習が成立。そのため、由加山南方の港町である[[琴浦地区#田の口・唐琴|田ノ口]]地区は、由加山の門前町の様相も呈していた。 == 祭神 == 祭神は[[彦狭知命]]、[[直毘神|神直日神]]、[[権現|大権現]]（本地仏：[[阿弥陀如来]]、[[薬師如来]]）、[[手置帆負命]]、[[天之御中主神]]、[[スサノオ|素盞鳴命]]、[[ウカノミタマ|倉稲魂命]]、[[菅原道真]]公、[[ワタツミ|大綿津見命]]。 == 文化財 == {{commons|Category:Yuga-jinja}} *本殿 - 江戸時代初期の建築。岡山県指定重要文化財（1961年指定）{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.pref.okayama.jp/cgi-bin/bunka/culture/controller/client/resultDetail.cgi?id=174 |title=由加神社本殿 |publisher=岡山県 |accessdate=2018-06-24}}・ *拝殿及び幣殿 - 登録有形文化財（2024年指定）[https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/574985 文化遺産オンライン] *三宝荒本殿 - 登録有形文化財（2024年指定）[https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/604596 文化遺産オンライン] *大鳥居 - 登録有形文化財（2024年指定）[https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/541457 文化遺産オンライン] == アクセス == *[[瀬戸中央自動車道]][[水島インターチェンジ|水島IC]]より車で約15分。 *[[瀬戸大橋線]]・[[児島駅]]よりタクシー約15分。 *瀬戸大橋線・[[上の町駅]]より徒歩で約4.5キロメートル。 *かつては[[児島駅]]より下電バス由加山行きが運行されていたが2020年9月30日に廃止されたため、路線バスでのアクセスは不可能。王子ヶ岳線「由加登山口」バス停からは約5キロメートルあるため注意。 == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} == 参考文献 == *岡山県高等学校教育研究会社会科部会歴史分科会 編 『新版 岡山県の歴史散歩』 山川出版社 1991年 96-97ページ *現地説明板 == 関連項目 == * [[蓮台寺 (倉敷市)|蓮台寺]] * [[熊野三山]] * [[熊野神社 (倉敷市林)]] * [[金刀比羅宮]] == 外部リンク == * [https://yugasan.or.jp/ 由加神社本宮ホームページ] * {{Instagram|yugajinjahongu|由加山 由加神社本宮}} {{shinto-stub}} {{Buddhism-stub}} {{神道 横}} {{デフォルトソート:ゆかしんしやほんくう}} [[Category:岡山県の旧県社]] [[Category:単立神社]] [[Category:瑜伽信仰]] [[Category:神仏習合]] [[Category:岡山県指定重要文化財]] [[Category:倉敷市の神社]] [[Category:岡山県の登録有形文化財]]

秋季大祭（11月3日） |本殿=三間社[[比翼入母屋造]] |画像=[[ファイル:Yuga Jinja 04.jpg|270px]]
拝殿 {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=270}} }} [[File:Hiroshige Bizen Tanokuchi.jpg|thumb|[[歌川広重]]]] '''由加神社本宮'''（ゆがじんじゃほんぐう）は、[[岡山県]][[倉敷市]]南部にあたる[[児島地域 (倉敷市)|児島]]の[[由加山]]にある[[神社]]である。[[近代社格制度|旧社格]]は県社。[[神社本庁]]の管轄に属さない[[単立神社]]。 == 概要 == 由加山は太古より[[磐座信仰]]が行われていたとされる。[[天平]]5年（[[733年]]）[[行基]]によりこの地に[[十一面観音]]が祀られ、神社仏閣が一体となった'''[[瑜伽大権現]]（由加大権現）'''と呼ばれる[[神仏習合]]の山となったという。北麓にある[[熊野神社 (倉敷市林)|熊野神社]]の縁起によれば、[[奈良時代]]の初めに[[役小角|役行者]]の高弟5人が[[紀伊国|紀州]][[熊野]]より[[熊野本宮大社]]を捧持してこの地に熊野神社を興し、その後、新熊野三山の一つとして由加山に那智宮を開いたとしている。 [[江戸時代]]中期より[[備前藩]]主[[池田氏]]の祈願所となり、正月・5月・9月には藩主自ら参拝していた。池田氏は当神社の社殿や、蓮台寺の客殿を造営した。 参道には多くの土産物店等が軒を連ね[[門前町]]を形成しており、初詣などの時期には多くの参拝者で賑わう。なお、この門前町で売られている「[[あんころ餅]]」は由加山の名物である。 [[明治時代]]になると[[神仏分離|神仏分離令]]により、由加神社と[[蓮台寺 (倉敷市)|蓮台寺]]に分離された。現在は西日本を中心に全国に末社52社を有する。 [[第二次世界大戦]]後の[[国家神道]]解体により、形式的には分離したままながら、僧職が本殿で祈祷を行う等事実上明治以前の形態に復し、蓮台寺により一体的に運営されていた。このため由加神社としての宗教法人資格は一応保持、神職を配置しつつも、事実上休眠状態に近かった。 1997年3月3日、由加神社が一部の建物を占拠し、宗教法人としての独自活動を開始した。これ以降蓮台寺と由加神社は対立関係となり、蓮台寺が建物の明け渡しを求めるなど裁判沙汰にもなるが、由加神社側が独自の宗教活動を開始した経緯は当時の兄（蓮台寺住職）と弟（由加神社宮司）の間の対立が原因とされている。 江戸時代には、瑜伽大権現（瑜伽山蓮台寺）と[[讃岐国]]の[[金毘羅権現|金毘羅大権現]]（金光院[[松尾寺 (香川県琴平町)|松尾寺]]）を両方参拝するという'''[[両参り]]'''といわれる慣習が成立。そのため、由加山南方の港町である[[琴浦地区#田の口・唐琴|田ノ口]]地区は、由加山の門前町の様相も呈していた。 == 祭神 == 祭神は[[彦狭知命]]、[[直毘神|神直日神]]、[[権現|大権現]]（本地仏：[[阿弥陀如来]]、[[薬師如来]]）、[[手置帆負命]]、[[天之御中主神]]、[[スサノオ|素盞鳴命]]、[[ウカノミタマ|倉稲魂命]]、[[菅原道真]]公、[[ワタツミ|大綿津見命]]。 == 文化財 == {{commons|Category:Yuga-jinja}} *本殿 - 江戸時代初期の建築。岡山県指定重要文化財（1961年指定）{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.pref.okayama.jp/cgi-bin/bunka/culture/controller/client/resultDetail.cgi?id=174 |title=由加神社本殿 |publisher=岡山県 |accessdate=2018-06-24}}・ *拝殿及び幣殿 - 登録有形文化財（2024年指定）[https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/574985 文化遺産オンライン] *三宝荒本殿 - 登録有形文化財（2024年指定）[https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/604596 文化遺産オンライン] *大鳥居 - 登録有形文化財（2024年指定）[https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/541457 文化遺産オンライン] == アクセス == *[[瀬戸中央自動車道]][[水島インターチェンジ|水島IC]]より車で約15分。 *[[瀬戸大橋線]]・[[児島駅]]よりタクシー約15分。 *瀬戸大橋線・[[上の町駅]]より徒歩で約4.5キロメートル。 *かつては[[児島駅]]より下電バス由加山行きが運行されていたが2020年9月30日に廃止されたため、路線バスでのアクセスは不可能。王子ヶ岳線「由加登山口」バス停からは約5キロメートルあるため注意。 == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} == 参考文献 == *岡山県高等学校教育研究会社会科部会歴史分科会 編 『新版 岡山県の歴史散歩』 山川出版社 1991年 96-97ページ *現地説明板 == 関連項目 == * [[蓮台寺 (倉敷市)|蓮台寺]] * [[熊野三山]] * [[熊野神社 (倉敷市林)]] * [[金刀比羅宮]] == 外部リンク == * [https://yugasan.or.jp/ 由加神社本宮ホームページ] * {{Instagram|yugajinjahongu|由加山 由加神社本宮}} {{shinto-stub}} {{Buddhism-stub}} {{神道 横}} {{デフォルトソート:ゆかしんしやほんくう}} [[Category:岡山県の旧県社]] [[Category:単立神社]] [[Category:瑜伽信仰]] [[Category:神仏習合]] [[Category:岡山県指定重要文化財]] [[Category:倉敷市の神社]] [[Category:岡山県の登録有形文化財]]

Haiden (worship hall) {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=270}} | name = Yuga Shrine Main Shrine | location = 2852 Ko-jima Yuga, [[Kurashiki]], [[Okayama Prefecture]] | coordinates = {{Coord|34|30|21.3|N|133|51|3.8|E|region:JP-33_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = Hikosa no Oshinomikoto, Kamunaobi-no-Kami, Daigongen, among others | Foundation = Traditionally [[Tenpyō]] 5 (733) | Shrine Rank = Former Prefectural Shrine (県社) | Annual festival = Spring Grand Festival (First Sunday of April)

Worship hall {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=270}} }}[[File:Hiroshige Bizen Tanokuchi.jpg|thumb|[[:en:Hiroshige|Utagawa Hiroshige]]]] '''Yuga Shrine Hongū''' ({{lang|ja|由加神社本宮}}) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en}} located on [[Yuga Mountain]] in the Kojima area of southern {{ill|Kurashiki|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|en}}, Japan. It is a {{ill|Standalone Shrine|ja|単立神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} unaffiliated with the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|en}}. Its modern status under the shrine Ranking system is a former {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. == Overview == Mount Yuga has been a center of {{ill|Iwakura rock|lt=Iwakura faith|ja|磐座信仰|qq=draft|qq=draft}} since ancient times. In 733 CE, the monk {{ill|Gyōki|en}} enshrined an {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-faced Kannon|ja|十一面観音|qq=draft|qq=draft}} statue here, establishing a religious complex that merged Shinto and Buddhism under the name '''{{nihongo|{{ill|Yuga Daigongen|ja|瑜伽大権現|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}}|瑜伽大権現}}''' (Yuga Great Incarnation). According to the history of {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)|lt=Kumano Shrine|ja|熊野神社 (倉敷市林)|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, five disciples of {{ill|En no Gyōja|en}}, a prominent ascetic, brought Kumano deities from {{ill|Kii Province|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and established Kumano Shrine in the northern foothills of Mount Yuga. They later founded Nachi Shrine on Mount Yuga as one of the "New Kumano Three Mountains." From the mid-{{ill|Edo period|en}}, the shrine became a place of worship for the {{ill|Ikeda clan (Okayama)|lt=Ikeda clan|ja|池田氏|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, rulers of {{ill|Bizen Province|en}}. The Ikeda clan sponsored the construction of the shrine’s buildings and nearby {{ill|Rendaiji (Kurashiki)|lt=Rendaiji|ja|蓮台寺_(倉敷市)|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. The shrine is located in a bustling {{ill|Monzen town|ja|門前町|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, with numerous souvenir shops and food stalls. During the New Year period, the shrine attracts many visitors. A specialty of Mount Yuga is the locally famous "ankoro mochi" (sweet red bean rice cakes). In the {{ill|Meiji period|en}}, the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (separation of Shinto and Buddhism) policy split Yuga Shrine from Rendaiji Temple. Yuga Shrine now has 52 branch shrines across western Japan. After {{ill|World War II|en}}, the shrine and temple informally resumed cooperation, with temple monks conducting prayers at the shrine. However, Yuga Shrine formally resumed independent religious activities in 1997, leading to a dispute with Rendaiji over property and operations. In the Edo period, a practice known as "dual pilgrimage" emerged, encouraging worshippers to visit both {{ill|Yuga Daigongen|ja|瑜伽大権現|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Konpira Gongen|en}} in {{ill|Sanuki Province|ja|讃岐国|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. == Deities == The shrine enshrines Hikosa no Oshinomikoto, Kamunaobi-no-Kami, Daigongen (Honji Buddha: {{ill|Amitābha|ja|阿弥陀如来|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Yakushi Nyorai|ja|薬師如来|1=Bhaisajyaguru|lt=Yakushi Nyorai (Me)dicine Buddha|qq=draft|qq=draft}}), and others, including: * {{ill|Hikosa-no-Oshinomikoto|ja|彦狭知命|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Kamunaobi-no-Kami|ja|神直日神|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Yuga Daigongen|ja|瑜伽大権現|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (associated Buddhas: {{ill|Amitābha|ja|阿弥陀如来|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Yakushi Nyorai|ja|薬師如来|1=Bhaisajyaguru|lt=Yakushi Nyorai (Me)dicine Buddha|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) * {{ill|Teokihobufu no Mikoto|ja|手置帆負命|1=Sanuki Inbe clan|lt=Teokihobufu no Mikoto|2=ja|3=讃岐忌部氏|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Amenominakanushi|ja|天之御中主神|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞鳴命|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Ukanomitama|lt=Ukanomitama-no-Kami|ja|倉稲魂命|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|Sugawara no Michizane|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Watatsumi|lt=Watatsumi-no-Mikoto|ja|大綿津見命|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * == Cultural Properties == * '''main hall''' – Built in the early Edo period, it is designated as an {{ill|Important Cultural Property of Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県指定重要文化財|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (1961).{{Cite web|url=http://www.pref.okayama.jp/cgi-bin/bunka/culture/controller/client/resultDetail.cgi?id=174 |title=main hall of Yuga Shrine |publisher=Okayama Prefecture |accessdate=2018-06-24}} == Access == * '''By Car:''' About 15 minutes from [[Seto-Chuo Expressway]]'s {{ill|Mizushima Interchange|en}}. * '''By Taxi:''' About 15 minutes from {{ill|Kojima Station|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}} on the {{ill|Seto-Ohashi Line|en}}. * '''By Bus:''' A limited {{ill|Shimoden Bus|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}} service runs from Kojima Station to Yuga Shrine (twice daily). However, buses do not operate on weekends, holidays, or during the New Year period. == References == {{reflist}} == Further Reading == * Okayama Prefectural High School Social Studies Department History Section, ''Revised Edition: History Walks in Okayama Prefecture,'' Yamakawa Publishing, 1991, pp. 96–97. * On-site informational plaques. == Related Articles == * {{ill|Rendaiji (Kurashiki)|en}} * {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)|en}} * {{ill|Kotohira-gū|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}} == External Links == * [https://yugasan.or.jp/ Yuga Shrine Hongū Official Website] * == References == {{Reflist}} * == External Links == * [https://yugasan.or.jp/ Official website of Yuga Shrine Main Shrine] {{shinto-stub}} {{Buddhism-stub}} {{Yuga Faith}} {{citation needed|Yuga Shrine Honmiya}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Yuga Shrine}} {{translated page|ja|由加神社本宮|version=104236521|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Tandoku shrines]] [[Category:Yuga faith]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Yuga Shrine]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Kurashiki]] [[Category:Former prefectural shrines in Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Independent shrines]] [[Category:Syncretic shrines]] [[Category:Shrines in Kurashiki]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[Category:New]] }} [[sn:Yuga Shrine]] [[ja:由加神社本宮]] [[en:Yuga Shrine]]

{{nihongo|Autumn Grand festival|November 3}} | main hall = Three-bay-wide [[Hiyoku Irimoya-zukuri]] style | image = [[File:Yuga Jinja 04.jpg|270px]]
Haiden (worship hall) {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=270}} | name = Yuga Shrine Main Shrine | location = 2852 Ko-jima Yuga, [[Kurashiki]], [[Okayama Prefecture]] | coordinates = {{Coord|34|30|21.3|N|133|51|3.8|E|region:JP-33_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = Hikosa no Oshinomikoto, Kamunaobi-no-Kami, Daigongen, among others | Foundation = Traditionally [[Tenpyō]] 5 (733) | Shrine Rank = Former Prefectural Shrine (県社) | Annual festival = Spring Grand Festival (First Sunday of April)
Autumn Grand Festival (November 3) | Main hall style = Sanma-sha Hiyoku Irimoya-zukuri | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Yuga Jinja 04.jpg|270px]]
Worship hall {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=270}} }}[[File:Hiroshige Bizen Tanokuchi.jpg|thumb|[[:en:Hiroshige|Utagawa Hiroshige]]]] '''Yuga Shrine Hongū''' ({{lang|ja|由加神社本宮}}) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en}} located on [[Yuga Mountain]] in the Kojima area of southern {{ill|Kurashiki|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|en}}, Japan. It is a {{ill|Standalone Shrine|ja|単立神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} unaffiliated with the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|en}}. Its modern status under the shrine Ranking system is a former {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. == Overview == Mount Yuga has been a center of {{ill|Iwakura rock|lt=Iwakura faith|ja|磐座信仰|qq=draft|qq=draft}} since ancient times. In 733 CE, the monk {{ill|Gyōki|en}} enshrined an {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-faced Kannon|ja|十一面観音|qq=draft|qq=draft}} statue here, establishing a religious complex that merged Shinto and Buddhism under the name '''{{nihongo|{{ill|Yuga Daigongen|ja|瑜伽大権現|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}}|瑜伽大権現}}''' (Yuga Great Incarnation). According to the history of {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)|lt=Kumano Shrine|ja|熊野神社 (倉敷市林)|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, five disciples of {{ill|En no Gyōja|en}}, a prominent ascetic, brought Kumano deities from {{ill|Kii Province|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and established Kumano Shrine in the northern foothills of Mount Yuga. They later founded Nachi Shrine on Mount Yuga as one of the "New Kumano Three Mountains." From the mid-{{ill|Edo period|en}}, the shrine became a place of worship for the {{ill|Ikeda clan (Okayama)|lt=Ikeda clan|ja|池田氏|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, rulers of {{ill|Bizen Province|en}}. The Ikeda clan sponsored the construction of the shrine’s buildings and nearby {{ill|Rendaiji (Kurashiki)|lt=Rendaiji|ja|蓮台寺_(倉敷市)|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. The shrine is located in a bustling {{ill|Monzen town|ja|門前町|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, with numerous souvenir shops and food stalls. During the New Year period, the shrine attracts many visitors. A specialty of Mount Yuga is the locally famous "ankoro mochi" (sweet red bean rice cakes). In the {{ill|Meiji period|en}}, the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (separation of Shinto and Buddhism) policy split Yuga Shrine from Rendaiji Temple. Yuga Shrine now has 52 branch shrines across western Japan. After {{ill|World War II|en}}, the shrine and temple informally resumed cooperation, with temple monks conducting prayers at the shrine. However, Yuga Shrine formally resumed independent religious activities in 1997, leading to a dispute with Rendaiji over property and operations. In the Edo period, a practice known as "dual pilgrimage" emerged, encouraging worshippers to visit both {{ill|Yuga Daigongen|ja|瑜伽大権現|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Konpira Gongen|en}} in {{ill|Sanuki Province|ja|讃岐国|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. == Deities == The shrine enshrines Hikosa no Oshinomikoto, Kamunaobi-no-Kami, Daigongen (Honji Buddha: {{ill|Amitābha|ja|阿弥陀如来|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Yakushi Nyorai|ja|薬師如来|1=Bhaisajyaguru|lt=Yakushi Nyorai (Me)dicine Buddha|qq=draft|qq=draft}}), and others, including: * {{ill|Hikosa-no-Oshinomikoto|ja|彦狭知命|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Kamunaobi-no-Kami|ja|神直日神|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Yuga Daigongen|ja|瑜伽大権現|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (associated Buddhas: {{ill|Amitābha|ja|阿弥陀如来|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Yakushi Nyorai|ja|薬師如来|1=Bhaisajyaguru|lt=Yakushi Nyorai (Me)dicine Buddha|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) * {{ill|Teokihobufu no Mikoto|ja|手置帆負命|1=Sanuki Inbe clan|lt=Teokihobufu no Mikoto|2=ja|3=讃岐忌部氏|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Amenominakanushi|ja|天之御中主神|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞鳴命|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Ukanomitama|lt=Ukanomitama-no-Kami|ja|倉稲魂命|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|Sugawara no Michizane|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Watatsumi|lt=Watatsumi-no-Mikoto|ja|大綿津見命|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * == Cultural Properties == * '''main hall''' – Built in the early Edo period, it is designated as an {{ill|Important Cultural Property of Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県指定重要文化財|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (1961).{{Cite web|url=http://www.pref.okayama.jp/cgi-bin/bunka/culture/controller/client/resultDetail.cgi?id=174 |title=main hall of Yuga Shrine |publisher=Okayama Prefecture |accessdate=2018-06-24}} == Access == * '''By Car:''' About 15 minutes from [[Seto-Chuo Expressway]]'s {{ill|Mizushima Interchange|en}}. * '''By Taxi:''' About 15 minutes from {{ill|Kojima Station|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}} on the {{ill|Seto-Ohashi Line|en}}. * '''By Bus:''' A limited {{ill|Shimoden Bus|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}} service runs from Kojima Station to Yuga Shrine (twice daily). However, buses do not operate on weekends, holidays, or during the New Year period. == References == {{reflist}} == Further Reading == * Okayama Prefectural High School Social Studies Department History Section, ''Revised Edition: History Walks in Okayama Prefecture,'' Yamakawa Publishing, 1991, pp. 96–97. * On-site informational plaques. == Related Articles == * {{ill|Rendaiji (Kurashiki)|en}} * {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)|en}} * {{ill|Kotohira-gū|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}} == External Links == * [https://yugasan.or.jp/ Yuga Shrine Hongū Official Website] * == References == {{Reflist}} * == External Links == * [https://yugasan.or.jp/ Official website of Yuga Shrine Main Shrine] {{shinto-stub}} {{Buddhism-stub}} {{Yuga Faith}} {{translated page|ja|由加神社本宮|version=104236521|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Tandoku shrines]] [[Category:Yuga faith]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Yuga Shrine]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Kurashiki]] [[Category:Former prefectural shrines in Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Independent shrines]] [[Category:Syncretic shrines]] [[Category:Shrines in Kurashiki]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[Category:New]] }} [[sn:Yuga Shrine]] [[ja:由加神社本宮]] [[en:Yuga Shrine]]

{{ill|Ukanomitama|lt=Ukanomitama-no-Kami|ja|倉稲魂命|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|Sugawara no Michizane|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Watatsumi|lt=Watatsumi-no-Mikoto|ja|大綿津見命|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * == Cultural Properties == * '''main hall''' – Built in the early Edo period, it is designated as an {{ill|Important Cultural Property of Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県指定重要文化財|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (1961).{{Cite web|url=http://www.pref.okayama.jp/cgi-bin/bunka/culture/controller/client/resultDetail.cgi?id=174 |title=main hall of Yuga Shrine |publisher=Okayama Prefecture |accessdate=2018-06-24}} == Access == * '''By Car:''' About 15 minutes from [[Seto-Chuo Expressway]]'s {{ill|Mizushima Interchange|en}}. * '''By Taxi:''' About 15 minutes from {{ill|Kojima Station|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}} on the {{ill|Seto-Ohashi Line|en}}. * '''By Bus:''' A limited {{ill|Shimoden Bus|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}} service runs from Kojima Station to Yuga Shrine (twice daily). However, buses do not operate on weekends, holidays, or during the New Year period. == References == {{reflist}} == Further Reading == * Okayama Prefectural High School Social Studies Department History Section, ''Revised Edition: History Walks in Okayama Prefecture,'' Yamakawa Publishing, 1991, pp. 96–97. * On-site informational plaques. == Related Articles == * {{ill|Rendaiji (Kurashiki)|en}} * {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)|en}} * {{ill|Kotohira-gū|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}} == External Links == * [https://yugasan.or.jp/ Yuga Shrine Hongū Official Website] * == References == {{Reflist}} * == External Links == * [https://yugasan.or.jp/ Official website of Yuga Shrine Main Shrine] {{shinto-stub}} {{Buddhism-stub}} {{Yuga Faith}} {{translated page|ja|由加神社本宮|version=104236521|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Tandoku shrines]] [[Category:Yuga faith]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Yuga Shrine]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Kurashiki]] [[Category:Former prefectural shrines in Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Independent shrines]] [[Category:Syncretic shrines]] [[Category:Shrines in Kurashiki]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]] }} [[sn:Yuga Shrine]] [[ja:由加神社本宮]] [[en:Yuga Shrine]]

{{otheruses}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Yuga Shrine Hongū | location = 2852 Yuga, Kojima, Kurashiki City, [[Okayama Prefecture]] | type = 34.505917 | longitude = 133.851056 | map = Japan Okayama | deity = {{ill|Hikosasaji no Mikoto|ja|彦狭知命|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Kaminahibi no Kami|ja|直毘神|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Yuga Daigongen|ja|瑜伽大権現|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and others | founded = {{nihongo|Tenpyō 5|733 CE}} | type = Former [[Prefectural Shrine|Prefectural Shrine]] | festivals = {{nihongo|Spring Grand festival|April first Sunday}}
{{nihongo|Autumn Grand festival|November 3}} | main hall = Three-bay-wide [[Hiyoku Irimoya-zukuri]] style | image = [[File:Yuga Jinja 04.jpg|270px]]
Haiden (worship hall) {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=270}} | name = Yuga Shrine Main Shrine | location = 2852 Ko-jima Yuga, [[Kurashiki]], [[Okayama Prefecture]] | coordinates = {{Coord|34|30|21.3|N|133|51|3.8|E|region:JP-33_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = Hikosa no Oshinomikoto, Kamunaobi-no-Kami, Daigongen, among others | Foundation = Traditionally [[Tenpyō]] 5 (733) | Shrine Rank = Former Prefectural Shrine (県社) | Annual festival = Spring Grand Festival (First Sunday of April)
Autumn Grand Festival (November 3) | Main hall style = Sanma-sha Hiyoku Irimoya-zukuri | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Yuga Jinja 04.jpg|270px]]
Worship hall {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=270}} }}[[File:Hiroshige Bizen Tanokuchi.jpg|thumb|[[:en:Hiroshige|Utagawa Hiroshige]]]] '''Yuga Shrine Hongū''' ({{lang|ja|由加神社本宮}}) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en}} located on [[Yuga Mountain]] in the Kojima area of southern {{ill|Kurashiki|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|en}}, Japan. It is a {{ill|Standalone Shrine|ja|単立神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} unaffiliated with the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|en}}. Its modern status under the shrine Ranking system is a former {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. == Overview == Mount Yuga has been a center of {{ill|Iwakura rock|lt=Iwakura faith|ja|磐座信仰|qq=draft|qq=draft}} since ancient times. In 733 CE, the monk {{ill|Gyōki|en}} enshrined an {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-faced Kannon|ja|十一面観音|qq=draft|qq=draft}} statue here, establishing a religious complex that merged Shinto and Buddhism under the name '''{{nihongo|{{ill|Yuga Daigongen|ja|瑜伽大権現|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}}|瑜伽大権現}}''' (Yuga Great Incarnation). According to the history of {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)|lt=Kumano Shrine|ja|熊野神社 (倉敷市林)|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, five disciples of {{ill|En no Gyōja|en}}, a prominent ascetic, brought Kumano deities from {{ill|Kii Province|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and established Kumano Shrine in the northern foothills of Mount Yuga. They later founded Nachi Shrine on Mount Yuga as one of the "New Kumano Three Mountains." From the mid-{{ill|Edo period|en}}, the shrine became a place of worship for the {{ill|Ikeda clan (Okayama)|lt=Ikeda clan|ja|池田氏|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, rulers of {{ill|Bizen Province|en}}. The Ikeda clan sponsored the construction of the shrine’s buildings and nearby {{ill|Rendaiji (Kurashiki)|lt=Rendaiji|ja|蓮台寺_(倉敷市)|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. The shrine is located in a bustling {{ill|Monzen town|ja|門前町|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, with numerous souvenir shops and food stalls. During the New Year period, the shrine attracts many visitors. A specialty of Mount Yuga is the locally famous "ankoro mochi" (sweet red bean rice cakes). In the {{ill|Meiji period|en}}, the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (separation of Shinto and Buddhism) policy split Yuga Shrine from Rendaiji Temple. Yuga Shrine now has 52 branch shrines across western Japan. After {{ill|World War II|en}}, the shrine and temple informally resumed cooperation, with temple monks conducting prayers at the shrine. However, Yuga Shrine formally resumed independent religious activities in 1997, leading to a dispute with Rendaiji over property and operations. In the Edo period, a practice known as "dual pilgrimage" emerged, encouraging worshippers to visit both {{ill|Yuga Daigongen|ja|瑜伽大権現|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Konpira Gongen|en}} in {{ill|Sanuki Province|ja|讃岐国|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. == Deities == The shrine enshrines Hikosa no Oshinomikoto, Kamunaobi-no-Kami, Daigongen (Honji Buddha: {{ill|Amitābha|ja|阿弥陀如来|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Yakushi Nyorai|ja|薬師如来|1=Bhaisajyaguru|lt=Yakushi Nyorai (Me)dicine Buddha|qq=draft|qq=draft}}), and others, including: * {{ill|Hikosa-no-Oshinomikoto|ja|彦狭知命|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Kamunaobi-no-Kami|ja|神直日神|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Yuga Daigongen|ja|瑜伽大権現|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (associated Buddhas: {{ill|Amitābha|ja|阿弥陀如来|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Yakushi Nyorai|ja|薬師如来|1=Bhaisajyaguru|lt=Yakushi Nyorai (Me)dicine Buddha|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) * {{ill|Teokihobufu no Mikoto|ja|手置帆負命|1=Sanuki Inbe clan|lt=Teokihobufu no Mikoto|2=ja|3=讃岐忌部氏|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Amenominakanushi|ja|天之御中主神|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞鳴命|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Ukanomitama|lt=Ukanomitama-no-Kami|ja|倉稲魂命|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|Sugawara no Michizane|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Watatsumi|lt=Watatsumi-no-Mikoto|ja|大綿津見命|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * == Cultural Properties == * '''main hall''' – Built in the early Edo period, it is designated as an {{ill|Important Cultural Property of Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県指定重要文化財|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (1961).{{Cite web|url=http://www.pref.okayama.jp/cgi-bin/bunka/culture/controller/client/resultDetail.cgi?id=174 |title=main hall of Yuga Shrine |publisher=Okayama Prefecture |accessdate=2018-06-24}} == Access == * '''By Car:''' About 15 minutes from [[Seto-Chuo Expressway]]'s {{ill|Mizushima Interchange|en}}. * '''By Taxi:''' About 15 minutes from {{ill|Kojima Station|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}} on the {{ill|Seto-Ohashi Line|en}}. * '''By Bus:''' A limited {{ill|Shimoden Bus|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}} service runs from Kojima Station to Yuga Shrine (twice daily). However, buses do not operate on weekends, holidays, or during the New Year period. == References == {{reflist}} == Further Reading == * Okayama Prefectural High School Social Studies Department History Section, ''Revised Edition: History Walks in Okayama Prefecture,'' Yamakawa Publishing, 1991, pp. 96–97. * On-site informational plaques. == Related Articles == * {{ill|Rendaiji (Kurashiki)|en}} * {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)|en}} * {{ill|Kotohira-gū|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}} == External Links == * [https://yugasan.or.jp/ Yuga Shrine Hongū Official Website] * == References == {{Reflist}} * == External Links == * [https://yugasan.or.jp/ Official website of Yuga Shrine Main Shrine] {{shinto-stub}} {{Buddhism-stub}} {{Yuga Faith}} {{translated page|ja|由加神社本宮|version=104236521|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Tandoku shrines]] [[Category:Yuga faith]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:Yuga Shrine]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Kurashiki]] [[Category:Former prefectural shrines in Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Independent shrines]] [[Category:Syncretic shrines]] [[Category:Shrines in Kurashiki]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]] }} [[sn:Yuga Shrine]] [[ja:由加神社本宮]] [[en:Yuga Shrine]]

In the Edo period, a practice known as "dual pilgrimage" emerged, encouraging worshippers to visit both {{ill|Yuga Daigongen|ja|瑜伽大権現|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Konpira Gongen|en}} in {{ill|Sanuki Province|ja|讃岐国|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. == Deities == The shrine enshrines Hikosa no Oshinomikoto, Kamunaobi-no-Kami, Daigongen (Honji Buddha: {{ill|Amitābha|ja|阿弥陀如来|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Yakushi Nyorai|ja|薬師如来|1=Bhaisajyaguru|lt=Yakushi Nyorai (Me)dicine Buddha|qq=draft|qq=draft}}), and others, including: * {{ill|Hikosa-no-Oshinomikoto|ja|彦狭知命|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Kamunaobi-no-Kami|ja|神直日神|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Yuga Daigongen|ja|瑜伽大権現|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (associated Buddhas: {{ill|Amitābha|ja|阿弥陀如来|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Yakushi Nyorai|ja|薬師如来|1=Bhaisajyaguru|lt=Yakushi Nyorai (Me)dicine Buddha|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) * {{ill|Teokihobufu no Mikoto|ja|手置帆負命|1=Sanuki Inbe clan|lt=Teokihobufu no Mikoto|2=ja|3=讃岐忌部氏|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Amenominakanushi|ja|天之御中主神|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞鳴命|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Ukanomitama|lt=Ukanomitama-no-Kami|ja|倉稲魂命|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|Sugawara no Michizane|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Watatsumi|lt=Watatsumi-no-Mikoto|ja|大綿津見命|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * == Cultural Properties == * '''main hall''' – Built in the early Edo period, it is designated as an {{ill|Important Cultural Property of Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県指定重要文化財|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (1961).{{Cite web|url=http://www.pref.okayama.jp/cgi-bin/bunka/culture/controller/client/resultDetail.cgi?id=174 |title=main hall of Yuga Shrine |publisher=Okayama Prefecture |accessdate=2018-06-24}} == Access == * '''By Car:''' About 15 minutes from [[Seto-Chuo Expressway]]'s {{ill|Mizushima Interchange|en}}. * '''By Taxi:''' About 15 minutes from {{ill|Kojima Station|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}} on the {{ill|Seto-Ohashi Line|en}}. * '''By Bus:''' A limited {{ill|Shimoden Bus|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}} service runs from Kojima Station to Yuga Shrine (twice daily). However, buses do not operate on weekends, holidays, or during the New Year period. == References == {{reflist}} == Further Reading == * Okayama Prefectural High School Social Studies Department History Section, ''Revised Edition: History Walks in Okayama Prefecture,'' Yamakawa Publishing, 1991, pp. 96–97. * On-site informational plaques. == Related Articles == * {{ill|Rendaiji (Kurashiki)|en}} * {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)|en}} * {{ill|Kotohira-gū|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}} == External Links == * [https://yugasan.or.jp/ Yuga Shrine Hongū Official Website] * == References == {{Reflist}} * == External Links == * [https://yugasan.or.jp/ Official website of Yuga Shrine Main Shrine] {{shinto-stub}} {{Buddhism-stub}} {{Yuga Faith}} {{translated page|ja|由加神社本宮|version=104236521|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Tandoku shrines]] [[Category:Yuga faith]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:Yuga Shrine]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Kurashiki]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Independent shrines]] [[Category:Syncretic shrines]] [[Category:Shrines in Kurashiki]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]] }} [[sn:Yuga Shrine]] [[ja:由加神社本宮]] [[en:Yuga Shrine]]

revision.text:
{{otheruses}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Yuga Shrine Hongū | location = 2852 Yuga, Kojima, Kurashiki City, [[Okayama Prefecture]] | type = 34.505917 | longitude = 133.851056 | map = Japan Okayama | deity = {{ill|Hikosasaji no Mikoto|ja|彦狭知命|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Kaminahibi no Kami|ja|直毘神|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Yuga Daigongen|ja|瑜伽大権現|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and others | founded = {{nihongo|Tenpyō 5|733 CE}} | type = Former [[Prefectural Shrine|Prefectural Shrine]] | festivals = {{nihongo|Spring Grand festival|April first Sunday}}
{{nihongo|Autumn Grand festival|November 3}} | main hall = Three-bay-wide [[Hiyoku Irimoya-zukuri]] style | image = [[File:Yuga Jinja 04.jpg|270px]]
Haiden (worship hall) {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=270}} | name = Yuga Shrine Main Shrine | location = 2852 Ko-jima Yuga, [[Kurashiki]], [[Okayama Prefecture]] | coordinates = {{Coord|34|30|21.3|N|133|51|3.8|E|region:JP-33_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = Hikosa no Oshinomikoto, Kamunaobi-no-Kami, Daigongen, among others | Foundation = Traditionally [[Tenpyō]] 5 (733) | Shrine Rank = Former Prefectural Shrine (県社) | Annual festival = Spring Grand Festival (First Sunday of April)
Autumn Grand Festival (November 3) | Main hall style = Sanma-sha Hiyoku Irimoya-zukuri | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Yuga Jinja 04.jpg|270px]]
Worship hall {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=270}} }}[[File:Hiroshige Bizen Tanokuchi.jpg|thumb|[[:en:Hiroshige|Utagawa Hiroshige]]]] '''Yuga Shrine Hongū''' ({{lang|ja|由加神社本宮}}) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en}} located on [[Yuga Mountain]] in the Kojima area of southern {{ill|Kurashiki|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|en}}, Japan. It is a {{ill|Standalone Shrine|ja|単立神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} unaffiliated with the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|en}}. Its modern status under the shrine Ranking system is a former {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. == Overview == Mount Yuga has been a center of {{ill|Iwakura rock|lt=Iwakura faith|ja|磐座信仰|qq=draft|qq=draft}} since ancient times. In 733 CE, the monk {{ill|Gyōki|en}} enshrined an {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-faced Kannon|ja|十一面観音|qq=draft|qq=draft}} statue here, establishing a religious complex that merged Shinto and Buddhism under the name '''{{nihongo|{{ill|Yuga Daigongen|ja|瑜伽大権現|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}}|瑜伽大権現}}''' (Yuga Great Incarnation). According to the history of {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)|lt=Kumano Shrine|ja|熊野神社 (倉敷市林)|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, five disciples of {{ill|En no Gyōja|en}}, a prominent ascetic, brought Kumano deities from {{ill|Kii Province|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and established Kumano Shrine in the northern foothills of Mount Yuga. They later founded Nachi Shrine on Mount Yuga as one of the "New Kumano Three Mountains." From the mid-{{ill|Edo period|en}}, the shrine became a place of worship for the {{ill|Ikeda clan (Okayama)|lt=Ikeda clan|ja|池田氏|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, rulers of {{ill|Bizen Province|en}}. The Ikeda clan sponsored the construction of the shrine’s buildings and nearby {{ill|Rendaiji (Kurashiki)|lt=Rendaiji|ja|蓮台寺_(倉敷市)|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. The shrine is located in a bustling {{ill|Monzen town|ja|門前町|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, with numerous souvenir shops and food stalls. During the New Year period, the shrine attracts many visitors. A specialty of Mount Yuga is the locally famous "ankoro mochi" (sweet red bean rice cakes). In the {{ill|Meiji period|en}}, the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (separation of Shinto and Buddhism) policy split Yuga Shrine from Rendaiji Temple. Yuga Shrine now has 52 branch shrines across western Japan. After {{ill|World War II|en}}, the shrine and temple informally resumed cooperation, with temple monks conducting prayers at the shrine. However, Yuga Shrine formally resumed independent religious activities in 1997, leading to a dispute with Rendaiji over property and operations. In the Edo period, a practice known as "dual pilgrimage" emerged, encouraging worshippers to visit both {{ill|Yuga Daigongen|ja|瑜伽大権現|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Konpira Gongen|en}} in {{ill|Sanuki Province|ja|讃岐国|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. == Deities == The shrine enshrines Hikosa no Oshinomikoto, Kamunaobi-no-Kami, Daigongen (Honji Buddha: {{ill|Amitābha|ja|阿弥陀如来|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Yakushi Nyorai|ja|薬師如来|1=Bhaisajyaguru|lt=Yakushi Nyorai (Me)dicine Buddha|qq=draft|qq=draft}}), and others, including: * {{ill|Hikosa-no-Oshinomikoto|ja|彦狭知命|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Kamunaobi-no-Kami|ja|神直日神|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Yuga Daigongen|ja|瑜伽大権現|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (associated Buddhas: {{ill|Amitābha|ja|阿弥陀如来|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Yakushi Nyorai|ja|薬師如来|1=Bhaisajyaguru|lt=Yakushi Nyorai (Me)dicine Buddha|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) * {{ill|Teokihobufu no Mikoto|ja|手置帆負命|1=Sanuki Inbe clan|lt=Teokihobufu no Mikoto|2=ja|3=讃岐忌部氏|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Amenominakanushi|ja|天之御中主神|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞鳴命|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Ukanomitama|lt=Ukanomitama-no-Kami|ja|倉稲魂命|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|Sugawara no Michizane|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Watatsumi|lt=Watatsumi-no-Mikoto|ja|大綿津見命|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * == Cultural Properties == * '''main hall''' – Built in the early Edo period, it is designated as an {{ill|Important Cultural Property of Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県指定重要文化財|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (1961).{{Cite web|url=http://www.pref.okayama.jp/cgi-bin/bunka/culture/controller/client/resultDetail.cgi?id=174 |title=main hall of Yuga Shrine |publisher=Okayama Prefecture |accessdate=2018-06-24}} == Access == * '''By Car:''' About 15 minutes from [[Seto-Chuo Expressway]]'s {{ill|Mizushima Interchange|en}}. * '''By Taxi:''' About 15 minutes from {{ill|Kojima Station|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}} on the {{ill|Seto-Ohashi Line|en}}. * '''By Bus:''' A limited {{ill|Shimoden Bus|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}} service runs from Kojima Station to Yuga Shrine (twice daily). However, buses do not operate on weekends, holidays, or during the New Year period. == References == {{reflist}} == Further Reading == * Okayama Prefectural High School Social Studies Department History Section, ''Revised Edition: History Walks in Okayama Prefecture,'' Yamakawa Publishing, 1991, pp. 96–97. * On-site informational plaques. == Related Articles == * {{ill|Rendaiji (Kurashiki)|en}} * {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)|en}} * {{ill|Kotohira-gū|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}} == External Links == * [https://yugasan.or.jp/ Yuga Shrine Hongū Official Website] * == References == {{Reflist}} * == External Links == * [https://yugasan.or.jp/ Official website of Yuga Shrine Main Shrine] {{shinto-stub}} {{Buddhism-stub}} {{Yuga Faith}} {{translated page|ja|由加神社本宮|version=104236521|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Tandoku shrines]] [[Category:Yuga faith]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:Yuga Shrine]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Kurashiki]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Okayama Prefecture Prefecture]] [[Category:Independent shrines]] [[Category:Syncretic shrines]] [[Category:Shrines in Kurashiki]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]] }} [[sn:Yuga Shrine]] [[ja:由加神社本宮]] [[en:Yuga Shrine]]

Worship hall {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=270}} }}[[File:Hiroshige Bizen Tanokuchi.jpg|thumb|[[:en:Hiroshige|Utagawa Hiroshige]]]] '''Yuga Shrine Hongū''' ({{lang|ja|由加神社本宮}}) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto shrine|qq=}} located on [[Yuga Mountain]] in the Kojima area of southern {{ill|Kurashiki|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurashiki|qq=}}, {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okayama Prefecture|qq=}}, Japan. It is a {{ill|Standalone Shrine|ja|単立神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Standalone Shrine|qq=}} unaffiliated with the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Association of Shinto Shrines|qq=}}. Its modern status under the shrine Ranking system is a former {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Shrine|qq=}}. == Overview == Mount Yuga has been a center of {{ill|Iwakura rock|lt=Iwakura faith|ja|磐座信仰|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwakura rock|qq=}} since ancient times. In 733 CE, the monk {{ill|Gyōki|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gyōki|qq=}} enshrined an {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-faced Kannon|ja|十一面観音|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ekādaśamukha|qq=}} statue here, establishing a religious complex that merged Shinto and Buddhism under the name '''{{nihongo|{{ill|Yuga Daigongen|ja|瑜伽大権現|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuga Daigongen|qq=}}|瑜伽大権現}}''' (Yuga Great Incarnation). According to the history of {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)|lt=Kumano Shrine|ja|熊野神社 (倉敷市林)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)|qq=}}, five disciples of {{ill|En no Gyōja|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/En no Gyōja|qq=}}, a prominent ascetic, brought Kumano deities from {{ill|Kii Province|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kii Province|qq=}} and established Kumano Shrine in the northern foothills of Mount Yuga. They later founded Nachi Shrine on Mount Yuga as one of the "New Kumano Three Mountains." From the mid-{{ill|Edo period|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Edo period|qq=}}, the shrine became a place of worship for the {{ill|Ikeda clan (Okayama)|lt=Ikeda clan|ja|池田氏|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikeda clan (Okayama)|qq=}}, rulers of {{ill|Bizen Province|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bizen Province|qq=}}. The Ikeda clan sponsored the construction of the shrine’s buildings and nearby {{ill|Rendaiji (Kurashiki)|lt=Rendaiji|ja|蓮台寺_(倉敷市)|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rendaiji (Kurashiki)|qq=}}. The shrine is located in a bustling {{ill|Monzen town|ja|門前町|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Monzen town|qq=}}, with numerous souvenir shops and food stalls. During the New Year period, the shrine attracts many visitors. A specialty of Mount Yuga is the locally famous "ankoro mochi" (sweet red bean rice cakes). In the {{ill|Meiji period|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji period|qq=}}, the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinbutsu bunri|qq=}} (separation of Shinto and Buddhism) policy split Yuga Shrine from Rendaiji Temple. Yuga Shrine now has 52 branch shrines across western Japan. After {{ill|World War II|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/World War II|qq=}}, the shrine and temple informally resumed cooperation, with temple monks conducting prayers at the shrine. However, Yuga Shrine formally resumed independent religious activities in 1997, leading to a dispute with Rendaiji over property and operations. In the Edo period, a practice known as "dual pilgrimage" emerged, encouraging worshippers to visit both {{ill|Yuga Daigongen|ja|瑜伽大権現|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuga Daigongen|qq=}} and {{ill|Konpira Gongen|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konpira Gongen|qq=}} in {{ill|Sanuki Province|ja|讃岐国|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanuki Province|qq=}}. == Deities == The shrine enshrines Hikosa no Oshinomikoto, Kamunaobi-no-Kami, Daigongen (Honji Buddha: {{ill|Amitābha|ja|阿弥陀如来|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amitābha|qq=}}, {{ill|Yakushi Nyorai|ja|薬師如来|1=Bhaisajyaguru|lt=Yakushi Nyorai (Me)dicine Buddha|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yakushi Nyorai|13=User:Immanuelle/Bhaisajyaguru|qq=}}), and others, including: * {{ill|Hikosa-no-Oshinomikoto|ja|彦狭知命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hikosa-no-Oshinomikoto|qq=}} * {{ill|Kamunaobi-no-Kami|ja|神直日神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamunaobi-no-Kami|qq=}} * {{ill|Yuga Daigongen|ja|瑜伽大権現|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuga Daigongen|qq=}} (associated Buddhas: {{ill|Amitābha|ja|阿弥陀如来|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amitābha|qq=}}, {{ill|Yakushi Nyorai|ja|薬師如来|1=Bhaisajyaguru|lt=Yakushi Nyorai (Me)dicine Buddha|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yakushi Nyorai|13=User:Immanuelle/Bhaisajyaguru|qq=}}) * {{ill|Teokihobufu no Mikoto|ja|手置帆負命|1=Sanuki Inbe clan|lt=Teokihobufu no Mikoto|2=ja|3=讃岐忌部氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Teokihobufu no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanuki Inbe clan|qq=}} * {{ill|Amenominakanushi|ja|天之御中主神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amenominakanushi|qq=}} * {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞鳴命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|qq=}} * {{ill|Ukanomitama|lt=Ukanomitama-no-Kami|ja|倉稲魂命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ukanomitama|qq=}} * {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|Sugawara no Michizane|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugawara no Michizane|qq=}} * {{ill|Watatsumi|lt=Watatsumi-no-Mikoto|ja|大綿津見命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Watatsumi|qq=}} * == Cultural Properties == * '''main hall''' – Built in the early Edo period, it is designated as an {{ill|Important Cultural Property of Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県指定重要文化財|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Cultural Property of Okayama Prefecture|qq=}} (1961).{{Cite web|url=http://www.pref.okayama.jp/cgi-bin/bunka/culture/controller/client/resultDetail.cgi?id=174 |title=main hall of Yuga Shrine |publisher=Okayama Prefecture |accessdate=2018-06-24}} == Access == * '''By Car:''' About 15 minutes from [[Seto-Chuo Expressway]]'s {{ill|Mizushima Interchange|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mizushima Interchange|qq=}}. * '''By Taxi:''' About 15 minutes from {{ill|Kojima Station|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojima Station|qq=}} on the {{ill|Seto-Ohashi Line|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seto-Ohashi Line|qq=}}. * '''By Bus:''' A limited {{ill|Shimoden Bus|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimoden Bus|qq=}} service runs from Kojima Station to Yuga Shrine (twice daily). However, buses do not operate on weekends, holidays, or during the New Year period. == References == {{reflist}} == Further Reading == * Okayama Prefectural High School Social Studies Department History Section, ''Revised Edition: History Walks in Okayama Prefecture,'' Yamakawa Publishing, 1991, pp. 96–97. * On-site informational plaques. == Related Articles == * {{ill|Rendaiji (Kurashiki)|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rendaiji (Kurashiki)|qq=}} * {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|qq=}} * {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)|qq=}} * {{ill|Kotohira-gū|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kotohira-gū|qq=}} == External Links == * [https://yugasan.or.jp/ Yuga Shrine Hongū Official Website] * == References == {{Reflist}} * == External Links == * [https://yugasan.or.jp/ Official website of Yuga Shrine Main Shrine] {{shinto-stub}} {{Buddhism-stub}} {{Yuga Faith}} {{translated page|ja|由加神社本宮|version=104236521|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Tandoku shrines]] [[Category:Yuga faith]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:Yuga Shrine]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Kurashiki]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Independent shrines]] [[Category:Syncretic shrines]] [[Category:Shrines in Kurashiki]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]] }} [[sn:Yuga Shrine]] [[ja:由加神社本宮]] [[en:Yuga Shrine]]

It is a {{ill|Standalone Shrine|ja|単立神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Standalone Shrine|qq=}} unaffiliated with the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Association of Shinto Shrines|qq=}}. Its modern status under the shrine Ranking system is a former {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Shrine|qq=}}. == Overview == Mount Yuga has been a center of {{ill|Iwakura rock|lt=Iwakura faith|ja|磐座信仰|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwakura rock|qq=}} since ancient times. In 733 CE, the monk {{ill|Gyōki|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gyōki|qq=}} enshrined an {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|lt=Eleven-faced Kannon|ja|十一面観音|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ekādaśamukha|qq=}} statue here, establishing a religious complex that merged Shinto and Buddhism under the name '''{{nihongo|{{ill|Yuga Daigongen|ja|瑜伽大権現|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuga Daigongen|qq=}}|瑜伽大権現}}''' (Yuga Great Incarnation). According to the history of {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)|lt=Kumano Shrine|ja|熊野神社 (倉敷市林)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)|qq=}}, five disciples of {{ill|En no Gyōja|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/En no Gyōja|qq=}}, a prominent ascetic, brought Kumano deities from {{ill|Kii Province|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kii Province|qq=}} and established Kumano Shrine in the northern foothills of Mount Yuga. They later founded Nachi Shrine on Mount Yuga as one of the "New Kumano Three Mountains." From the mid-{{ill|Edo period|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Edo period|qq=}}, the shrine became a place of worship for the {{ill|Ikeda clan (Okayama)|lt=Ikeda clan|ja|池田氏|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikeda clan (Okayama)|qq=}}, rulers of {{ill|Bizen Province|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bizen Province|qq=}}. The Ikeda clan sponsored the construction of the shrine’s buildings and nearby {{ill|Rendaiji (Kurashiki)|lt=Rendaiji|ja|蓮台寺_(倉敷市)|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rendaiji (Kurashiki)|qq=}}. The shrine is located in a bustling {{ill|Monzen town|ja|門前町|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Monzen town|qq=}}, with numerous souvenir shops and food stalls. During the New Year period, the shrine attracts many visitors. A specialty of Mount Yuga is the locally famous "ankoro mochi" (sweet red bean rice cakes). In the {{ill|Meiji period|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji period|qq=}}, the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinbutsu bunri|qq=}} (separation of Shinto and Buddhism) policy split Yuga Shrine from Rendaiji Temple. Yuga Shrine now has 52 branch shrines across western Japan. After {{ill|World War II|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/World War II|qq=}}, the shrine and temple informally resumed cooperation, with temple monks conducting prayers at the shrine. However, Yuga Shrine formally resumed independent religious activities in 1997, leading to a dispute with Rendaiji over property and operations. In the Edo period, a practice known as "dual pilgrimage" emerged, encouraging worshippers to visit both {{ill|Yuga Daigongen|ja|瑜伽大権現|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuga Daigongen|qq=}} and {{ill|Konpira Gongen|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konpira Gongen|qq=}} in {{ill|Sanuki Province|ja|讃岐国|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanuki Province|qq=}}. == Deities == The shrine enshrines Hikosa no Oshinomikoto, Kamunaobi-no-Kami, Daigongen (Honji Buddha: {{ill|Amitābha|ja|阿弥陀如来|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amitābha|qq=}}, {{ill|Yakushi Nyorai|ja|薬師如来|1=Bhaisajyaguru|lt=Yakushi Nyorai (Me)dicine Buddha|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yakushi Nyorai|13=User:Immanuelle/Bhaisajyaguru|qq=}}), and others, including: * {{ill|Hikosa-no-Oshinomikoto|ja|彦狭知命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hikosa-no-Oshinomikoto|qq=}} * {{ill|Kamuna'obi|ja|神直日神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamunaobi-no-Kami|qq=}} * {{ill|Yuga Daigongen|ja|瑜伽大権現|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuga Daigongen|qq=}} (associated Buddhas: {{ill|Amitābha|ja|阿弥陀如来|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amitābha|qq=}}, {{ill|Yakushi Nyorai|ja|薬師如来|1=Bhaisajyaguru|lt=Yakushi Nyorai (Me)dicine Buddha|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yakushi Nyorai|13=User:Immanuelle/Bhaisajyaguru|qq=}}) * {{ill|Teokihobufu no Mikoto|ja|手置帆負命|1=Sanuki Inbe clan|lt=Teokihobufu no Mikoto|2=ja|3=讃岐忌部氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Teokihobufu no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanuki Inbe clan|qq=}} * {{ill|Ame-no-Minakanushi|ja|天之御中主神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amenominakanushi|qq=}} * {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞鳴命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|qq=}} * {{ill|Ukanomitama|lt=Ukanomitama-no-Kami|ja|倉稲魂命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ukanomitama|qq=}} * {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|ja|Sugawara no Michizane|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugawara no Michizane|qq=}} * {{ill|Watatsumi|lt=Watatsumi-no-Mikoto|ja|大綿津見命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Watatsumi|qq=}} * == Cultural Properties == * '''main hall''' – Built in the early Edo period, it is designated as an {{ill|Important Cultural Property of Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県指定重要文化財|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Cultural Property of Okayama Prefecture|qq=}} (1961).{{Cite web|url=http://www.pref.okayama.jp/cgi-bin/bunka/culture/controller/client/resultDetail.cgi?id=174 |title=main hall of Yuga Shrine |publisher=Okayama Prefecture |accessdate=2018-06-24}} == Access == * '''By Car:''' About 15 minutes from [[Seto-Chuo Expressway]]'s {{ill|Mizushima Interchange|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mizushima Interchange|qq=}}. * '''By Taxi:''' About 15 minutes from {{ill|Kojima Station|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojima Station|qq=}} on the {{ill|Seto-Ohashi Line|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seto-Ohashi Line|qq=}}. * '''By Bus:''' A limited {{ill|Shimoden Bus|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimoden Bus|qq=}} service runs from Kojima Station to Yuga Shrine (twice daily). However, buses do not operate on weekends, holidays, or during the New Year period. == References == {{reflist}} == Further Reading == * Okayama Prefectural High School Social Studies Department History Section, ''Revised Edition: History Walks in Okayama Prefecture,'' Yamakawa Publishing, 1991, pp. 96–97. * On-site informational plaques. == Related Articles == * {{ill|Rendaiji (Kurashiki)|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rendaiji (Kurashiki)|qq=}} * {{ill|Kumano Sanzan|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|qq=}} * {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)|qq=}} * {{ill|Kotohira-gū|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kotohira-gū|qq=}} == External Links == * [https://yugasan.or.jp/ Yuga Shrine Hongū Official Website] * == References == {{Reflist}} * == External Links == * [https://yugasan.or.jp/ Official website of Yuga Shrine Main Shrine] {{shinto-stub}} {{Buddhism-stub}} {{Yuga Faith}} {{translated page|ja|由加神社本宮|version=104236521|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Tandoku shrines]] [[Category:Yuga faith]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:Yuga Shrine]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Kurashiki]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Independent shrines]] [[Category:Syncretic shrines]] [[Category:Shrines in Kurashiki]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]] }} [[sn:Yuga Shrine]] [[ja:由加神社本宮]] [[en:Yuga Shrine]]
