[[sn:commons:Category:Yuga-jinja]][[ja:由加神社本宮]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11577571]] Wikidata name: Yuga-jinja [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:34, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{otheruses}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Yuga Shrine Hongū | location = 2852 Yuga, Kojima, Kurashiki City, [[:en:Okayama Prefecture|Okayama Prefecture]] | type = 34.505917 | longitude = 133.851056 | map = Japan Okayama | deity = {{ill|Hikosasaji no Mikoto|ja|彦狭知命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hikosasaji no Mikoto|qq=|1=Hikosachi no Mikoto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q135327097}}, {{ill|Kaminahibi no Kami|ja|直毘神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaminahibi no Kami|qq=|1=Naobinokami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11581794|1=Ōnaobi no Kami}}, {{ill|Yuga Daigongen|ja|瑜伽大権現|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuga Daigongen|qq=}} and others | founded = {{nihongo|Tenpyō 5|733 CE}} | type = Former [[Prefectural Shrine|Prefectural Shrine]] | festivals = {{nihongo|Spring Grand festival|April first Sunday}}
{{nihongo|Autumn Grand festival|November 3}} | main hall = Three-bay-wide [[Hiyoku Irimoya-zukuri]] style | image = File:Yuga Jinja 04.jpg | name = Yuga Shrine Main Shrine | location = 2852 Ko-jima Yuga, [[:en:Kurashiki|Kurashiki]], [[:en:Okayama Prefecture|Okayama Prefecture]] | coordinates = {{Coord|34.505917|N|133.851056|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = Hikosa no Oshinomikoto, Kamunaobi-no-Kami, Daigongen, among others | Foundation = Traditionally [[Tenpyō]] 5 (733) | Shrine Rank = Former Prefectural Shrine (県社) | Annual festival = Spring Grand Festival (First Sunday of April)
Autumn Grand Festival (November 3) | Main hall style = Sanma-sha Hiyoku Irimoya-zukuri | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | location = {{ill|Kurashiki|WD=Q467487}} }}[[File:Hiroshige Bizen Tanokuchi.jpg|thumb|[[:en:Hiroshige|Utagawa Hiroshige]]]] '''Yuga Shrine Hongū''' ({{lang|ja|由加神社本宮}}) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto shrine|qq=}} located on [[Yuga Mountain]] in the Kojima area of southern {{ill|Kurashiki|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurashiki|qq=}}, {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okayama Prefecture|qq=}}, Japan. It is a {{ill|Independent Shinto shrine|lt=Independent Shinto shrine|lt=Standalone Shrine|ja|単立神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Standalone Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 単立|redirects to 単立|1=Independent Shinto shrine|2=ja|3=単立|1=Independent Shinto shrine|2=ja|3=単立|redirects to 単立|1=Independent Shinto shrine|2=ja|3=単立|}} unaffiliated with the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Association of Shinto Shrines|qq=}}. Its modern status under the shrine Ranking system is a former {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社|1=Prefectural Shrine|WD=Q90139226}}{{efn|Ken-sha|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}. == Overview == Mount Yuga has been a center of {{ill|Iwakura rock|lt=Iwakura faith|ja|磐座信仰|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwakura rock|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 磐座}} since ancient times. In 733 CE, the monk {{ill|Gyōki|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gyōki|qq=}} enshrined an [[:en:Ekādaśamukha|Eleven-faced Kannon]] statue here, establishing a religious complex that merged Shinto and Buddhism under the name '''{{nihongo|{{ill|Yuga Daigongen|ja|瑜伽大権現|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuga Daigongen|qq=}}|瑜伽大権現}}''' (Yuga Great Incarnation). According to the history of {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)|lt=Kumano Shrine|ja|熊野神社 (倉敷市林)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)|qq=}}, five disciples of {{ill|En no Gyōja|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/En no Gyōja|qq=}}, a prominent ascetic, brought Kumano deities from {{ill|Kii Province|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kii Province|qq=}} and established Kumano Shrine in the northern foothills of Mount Yuga. They later founded Nachi Shrine on Mount Yuga as one of the "New Kumano Three Mountains." From the mid-{{ill|Edo period|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Edo period|qq=}}, the shrine became a place of worship for the [[:en:Ikeda clan|Ikeda clan]], rulers of {{ill|Bizen Province|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bizen Province|qq=}}. The Ikeda clan sponsored the construction of the shrine’s buildings and nearby {{ill|Yugasan Rendaiji|lt=Rendaiji|ja|蓮台寺_(倉敷市)|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yugasan Rendaiji|qq=}}. The shrine is located in a bustling {{ill|Monzen-machi|ja|門前町|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Monzen-machi|qq=}}, with numerous souvenir shops and food stalls. During the New Year period, the shrine attracts many visitors. A specialty of Mount Yuga is the locally famous "ankoro mochi" (sweet red bean rice cakes). In the {{ill|Meiji period|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji period|qq=}}, the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinbutsu bunri|qq=}} (separation of Shinto and Buddhism) policy split Yuga Shrine from Rendaiji Temple. Yuga Shrine now has 52 branch shrines across western Japan. After {{ill|World War II|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/World War II|qq=}}, the shrine and temple informally resumed cooperation, with temple monks conducting prayers at the shrine. However, Yuga Shrine formally resumed independent religious activities in 1997, leading to a dispute with Rendaiji over property and operations. In the Edo period, a practice known as "dual pilgrimage" emerged, encouraging worshippers to visit both {{ill|Yuga Daigongen|ja|瑜伽大権現|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuga Daigongen|qq=}} and {{ill|Konpira Gongen|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konpira Gongen|qq=}} in [[:en:Sanuki Province|Sanuki Province]]. == Deities == The shrine enshrines Hikosa no Oshinomikoto, Kamunaobi-no-Kami, Daigongen (Honji Buddha: [[:en:Amitābha|Amitābha]], [[:en:Bhaisajyaguru|Yakushi Nyorai Medicine Buddha]]), and others, including: * {{ill|Hikosachi no Mikoto|lt=Hikosachi no Mikoto|lt=Hikosa-no-Oshinomikoto|ja|彦狭知命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hikosa-no-Oshinomikoto|qq=}} * {{ill|Kamuna'obi|ja|神直日神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamunaobi-no-Kami|qq=}} * {{ill|Yuga Daigongen|ja|瑜伽大権現|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuga Daigongen|qq=}} (associated Buddhas: [[:en:Amitābha|Amitābha]], [[:en:Bhaisajyaguru|Yakushi Nyorai Medicine Buddha]]) * {{ill|Teokihobufu no Mikoto|ja|手置帆負命|1=Sanuki Inbe clan|lt=Teokihobufu no Mikoto|2=ja|3=讃岐忌部氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Teokihobufu no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanuki Inbe clan|qq=}} * [[:en:Ame-no-Minakanushi|Ame-no-Minakanushi]] * {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞鳴命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スサノオ}} * [[sn:commons:Category:Yuga-jinja]][[ja:由加神社本宮]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11577571]] Wikidata name: Yuga-jinja [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:34, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{otheruses}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Yuga Shrine Hongū | location = 2852 Yuga, Kojima, Kurashiki City, [[:en:Okayama Prefecture|Okayama Prefecture]] | type = 34.505917 | longitude = 133.851056 | map = Japan Okayama | deity = {{ill|Hikosasaji no Mikoto|ja|彦狭知命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hikosasaji no Mikoto|1=Hikosachi no Mikoto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q135327097}}, {{ill|Kaminahibi no Kami|ja|直毘神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaminahibi no Kami|1=Naobinokami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11581794|1=Ōnaobi no Kami}}, {{ill|Yuga Daigongen|ja|瑜伽大権現|en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuga Daigongen|qq=}} and others | founded = {{nihongo|Tenpyō 5|733 CE}} | type = Former [[Prefectural Shrine|Prefectural Shrine]] | festivals = {{nihongo|Spring Grand festival|April first Sunday}}
{{nihongo|Autumn Grand festival|November 3}} | main hall = Three-bay-wide [[Hiyoku Irimoya-zukuri]] style | image = File:Yuga Jinja 04.jpg | name = Yuga Shrine Main Shrine | location = 2852 Ko-jima Yuga, [[:en:Kurashiki|Kurashiki]], [[:en:Okayama Prefecture|Okayama Prefecture]] | coordinates = {{Coord|34.505917|N|133.851056|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = Hikosa no Oshinomikoto, Kamunaobi-no-Kami, Daigongen, among others | Foundation = Traditionally [[Tenpyō]] 5 (733) | Shrine Rank = Former Prefectural Shrine (県社) | Annual festival = Spring Grand Festival (First Sunday of April)
Autumn Grand Festival (November 3) | Main hall style = Sanma-sha Hiyoku Irimoya-zukuri | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | location = {{ill|Kurashiki|WD=Q467487}} }}[[File:Hiroshige Bizen Tanokuchi.jpg|thumb|[[:en:Hiroshige|Utagawa Hiroshige]]]] '''Yuga Shrine Hongū''' ({{lang|ja|由加神社本宮}}) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto shrine|qq=}} located on [[Yuga Mountain]] in the Kojima area of southern {{ill|Kurashiki|en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurashiki|qq=}}, {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okayama Prefecture|qq=}}, Japan. It is a {{ill|Independent Shinto shrine|lt=Independent Shinto shrine|lt=Standalone Shrine|ja|単立神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Standalone Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 単立|redirects to 単立|1=Independent Shinto shrine|2=ja|3=単立|1=Independent Shinto shrine|2=ja|3=単立|redirects to 単立|1=Independent Shinto shrine|2=ja|3=単立|}} unaffiliated with the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Association of Shinto Shrines|qq=}}. Its modern status under the shrine Ranking system is a former {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社|1=Prefectural Shrine|WD=Q90139226}}{{efn|Ken-sha|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}. == Overview == Mount Yuga has been a center of {{ill|Iwakura rock|lt=Iwakura faith|ja|磐座信仰|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwakura rock|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 磐座}} since ancient times. In 733 CE, the monk {{ill|Gyōki|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gyōki|qq=}} enshrined an [[:en:Ekādaśamukha|Eleven-faced Kannon]] statue here, establishing a religious complex that merged Shinto and Buddhism under the name '''{{nihongo|{{ill|Yuga Daigongen|ja|瑜伽大権現|en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuga Daigongen|qq=}}|瑜伽大権現}}''' (Yuga Great Incarnation). According to the history of {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)|lt=Kumano Shrine|ja|熊野神社 (倉敷市林)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)|qq=}}, five disciples of {{ill|En no Gyōja|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/En no Gyōja|qq=}}, a prominent ascetic, brought Kumano deities from {{ill|Kii Province|en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kii Province|qq=}} and established Kumano Shrine in the northern foothills of Mount Yuga. They later founded Nachi Shrine on Mount Yuga as one of the "New Kumano Three Mountains." From the mid-{{ill|Edo period|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Edo period|qq=}}, the shrine became a place of worship for the [[:en:Ikeda clan|Ikeda clan]], rulers of {{ill|Bizen Province|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bizen Province|qq=}}. The Ikeda clan sponsored the construction of the shrine’s buildings and nearby {{ill|Yugasan Rendaiji|lt=Rendaiji|ja|蓮台寺_(倉敷市)|en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yugasan Rendaiji|qq=}}. The shrine is located in a bustling {{ill|Monzen-machi|ja|門前町|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Monzen-machi|qq=}}, with numerous souvenir shops and food stalls. During the New Year period, the shrine attracts many visitors. A specialty of Mount Yuga is the locally famous "ankoro mochi" (sweet red bean rice cakes). In the {{ill|Meiji period|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji period|qq=}}, the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinbutsu bunri|qq=}} (separation of Shinto and Buddhism) policy split Yuga Shrine from Rendaiji Temple. Yuga Shrine now has 52 branch shrines across western Japan. After {{ill|World War II|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/World War II|qq=}}, the shrine and temple informally resumed cooperation, with temple monks conducting prayers at the shrine. However, Yuga Shrine formally resumed independent religious activities in 1997, leading to a dispute with Rendaiji over property and operations. In the Edo period, a practice known as "dual pilgrimage" emerged, encouraging worshippers to visit both {{ill|Yuga Daigongen|ja|瑜伽大権現|en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuga Daigongen|qq=}} and {{ill|Konpira Gongen|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konpira Gongen|qq=}} in [[:en:Sanuki Province|Sanuki Province]]. == Deities == The shrine enshrines Hikosa no Oshinomikoto, Kamunaobi-no-Kami, Daigongen (Honji Buddha: [[:en:Amitābha|Amitābha]], [[:en:Bhaisajyaguru|Yakushi Nyorai Medicine Buddha]]), and others, including: * {{ill|Hikosachi no Mikoto|lt=Hikosachi no Mikoto|lt=Hikosa-no-Oshinomikoto|ja|彦狭知命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hikosa-no-Oshinomikoto|qq=}} * {{ill|Kamuna'obi|ja|神直日神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamunaobi-no-Kami|qq=}} * {{ill|Yuga Daigongen|ja|瑜伽大権現|en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuga Daigongen|qq=}} (associated Buddhas: [[:en:Amitābha|Amitābha]], [[:en:Bhaisajyaguru|Yakushi Nyorai Medicine Buddha]]) * {{ill|Teokihobufu no Mikoto|ja|手置帆負命|1=Sanuki Inbe clan|lt=Teokihobufu no Mikoto|2=ja|3=讃岐忌部氏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Teokihobufu no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanuki Inbe clan|qq=}} * [[:en:Ame-no-Minakanushi|Ame-no-Minakanushi]] * {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞鳴命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スサノオ}} * {{ill|Ukanomitama|lt=Ukanomitama-no-Kami|ja|倉稲魂命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ukanomitama|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ウカノミタマ}} * {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|1=Sugawara no Michizane|2=ja|3=菅原道真|ja|Sugawara no Michizane|1=Sugawara no Michizane|2=ja|3=菅原道真|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugawara no Michizane|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q382005}} * {{ill|Watatsumi|lt=Watatsumi-no-Mikoto|ja|大綿津見命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Watatsumi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * == Cultural Properties == * '''main hall''' – Built in the early Edo period, it is designated as an {{ill|Important Cultural Property of Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県指定重要文化財|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Cultural Property of Okayama Prefecture|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (1961).{{Cite web|url=http://www.pref.okayama.jp/cgi-bin/bunka/culture/controller/client/resultDetail.cgi?id=174 |title=main hall of Yuga Shrine from Kojima Station to Yuga Shrine (twice daily). However, buses do not operate on weekends, holidays, or during the New Year period. == References == {{reflist}} == Further Reading == * Okayama Prefectural High School Social Studies Department History Section, ''Revised Edition: History Walks in Okayama Prefecture,'' Yamakawa Publishing, 1991, pp. 96–97. * On-site informational plaques. == Related Articles == * {{ill|Yugasan Rendaiji|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yugasan Rendaiji|qq=}} * {{ill|Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Sanzan|en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|qq=}} * {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)|qq=}} * {{ill|Kotohira-gū|en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kotohira-gū|qq=}} == External Links == * [https://yugasan.or.jp/ Yuga Shrine Hongū Official Website] * == References == {{Reflist}} * == External Links == * [https://yugasan.or.jp/ Official website of Yuga Shrine Main Shrine] {{shinto-stub}} {{Buddhism-stub}} {{Yuga Faith}} {{translated page|ja|由加神社本宮|version=104236521|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Yuga faith]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Kurashiki]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Independent shrines]] }} [[ja:由加神社本宮]] [[Category:Registered Tangible Cultural Property of Japan in Okayama prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties Designated by Okayama Prefecture]] {{Commons category|Yuga-jinja}} {{wikidata link|Q11577571}}

Station to Yuga Shrine (twice daily). However, buses do not operate on weekends, holidays, or during the New Year period. == References == {{reflist}} == Further Reading == * Okayama Prefectural High School Social Studies Department History Section, ''Revised Edition: History Walks in Okayama Prefecture,'' Yamakawa Publishing, 1991, pp. 96–97. * On-site informational plaques. == Related Articles == * {{ill|Yugasan Rendaiji|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yugasan Rendaiji|qq=}} * {{ill|Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Sanzan|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|qq=}} * {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)|qq=}} * {{ill|Kotohira-gū|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kotohira-gū|qq=}} == External Links == * [https://yugasan.or.jp/ Yuga Shrine Hongū Official Website] * == References == {{Reflist}} * == External Links == * [https://yugasan.or.jp/ Official website of Yuga Shrine Main Shrine] {{shinto-stub}} {{Buddhism-stub}} {{Yuga Faith}} {{translated page|ja|由加神社本宮|version=104236521|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Yuga faith]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Kurashiki]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Independent shrines]] }} [[ja:由加神社本宮]] [[Category:Registered Tangible Cultural Property of Japan in Okayama prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties Designated by Okayama Prefecture]] {{Commons category|Yuga-jinja}} {{wikidata link|Q11577571}}

revision.text:
[[sn:commons:Category:Yuga-jinja]][[ja:由加神社本宮]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11577571]] Wikidata name: Yuga-jinja [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:34, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{otheruses}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Yuga Shrine Hongū | location = 2852 Yuga, Kojima, Kurashiki City, [[:en:Okayama Prefecture|Okayama Prefecture]] | type = 34.505917 | longitude = 133.851056 | map = Japan Okayama | deity = {{ill|Hikosasaji no Mikoto|ja|彦狭知命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hikosasaji no Mikoto|1=Hikosachi no Mikoto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q135327097}}, {{ill|Kaminahibi no Kami|ja|直毘神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaminahibi no Kami|1=Naobinokami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11581794|1=Ōnaobi no Kami}}, {{ill|Yuga Daigongen|ja|瑜伽大権現|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuga Daigongen|qq=}} and others | founded = {{nihongo|Tenpyō 5|733 CE}} | type = Former [[Prefectural Shrine|Prefectural Shrine]] | festivals = {{nihongo|Spring Grand festival|April first Sunday}}
{{nihongo|Autumn Grand festival|November 3}} | main hall = Three-bay-wide [[Hiyoku Irimoya-zukuri]] style | image = File:Yuga Jinja 04.jpg | name = Yuga Shrine Main Shrine | location = 2852 Ko-jima Yuga, [[:en:Kurashiki|Kurashiki]], [[:en:Okayama Prefecture|Okayama Prefecture]] | coordinates = {{Coord|34.505917|N|133.851056|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = Hikosa no Oshinomikoto, Kamunaobi-no-Kami, Daigongen, among others | Foundation = Traditionally [[Tenpyō]] 5 (733) | Shrine Rank = Former Prefectural Shrine (県社) | Annual festival = Spring Grand Festival (First Sunday of April)
Autumn Grand Festival (November 3) | Main hall style = Sanma-sha Hiyoku Irimoya-zukuri | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | location = {{ill|Kurashiki|WD=Q467487}} }}[[File:Hiroshige Bizen Tanokuchi.jpg|thumb|[[:en:Hiroshige|Utagawa Hiroshige]]]] '''Yuga Shrine Hongū''' ({{lang|ja|由加神社本宮}}) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto shrine|qq=}} located on [[Yuga Mountain]] in the Kojima area of southern {{ill|Kurashiki|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurashiki|qq=}}, {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okayama Prefecture|qq=}}, Japan. It is a {{ill|Independent Shinto shrine|lt=Independent Shinto shrine|lt=Standalone Shrine|ja|単立神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Standalone Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 単立|redirects to 単立|1=Independent Shinto shrine|2=ja|3=単立|1=Independent Shinto shrine|2=ja|3=単立|redirects to 単立|1=Independent Shinto shrine|2=ja|3=単立|}} unaffiliated with the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Association of Shinto Shrines|qq=}}. Its modern status under the shrine Ranking system is a former {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社|1=Prefectural Shrine|WD=Q90139226}}{{efn|Ken-sha|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}. == Overview == Mount Yuga has been a center of {{ill|Iwakura rock|lt=Iwakura faith|ja|磐座信仰|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwakura rock|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 磐座}} since ancient times. In 733 CE, the monk {{ill|Gyōki|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gyōki|qq=}} enshrined an [[:en:Ekādaśamukha|Eleven-faced Kannon]] statue here, establishing a religious complex that merged Shinto and Buddhism under the name '''{{nihongo|{{ill|Yuga Daigongen|ja|瑜伽大権現|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuga Daigongen|qq=}}|瑜伽大権現}}''' (Yuga Great Incarnation). According to the history of {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)|lt=Kumano Shrine|ja|熊野神社 (倉敷市林)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)|qq=}}, five disciples of {{ill|En no Gyōja|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/En no Gyōja|qq=}}, a prominent ascetic, brought Kumano deities from {{ill|Kii Province|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kii Province|qq=}} and established Kumano Shrine in the northern foothills of Mount Yuga. They later founded Nachi Shrine on Mount Yuga as one of the "New Kumano Three Mountains." From the mid-{{ill|Edo period|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Edo period|qq=}}, the shrine became a place of worship for the [[:en:Ikeda clan|Ikeda clan]], rulers of {{ill|Bizen Province|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bizen Province|qq=}}. The Ikeda clan sponsored the construction of the shrine’s buildings and nearby {{ill|Yugasan Rendaiji|lt=Rendaiji|ja|蓮台寺_(倉敷市)|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yugasan Rendaiji|qq=}}. The shrine is located in a bustling {{ill|Monzen-machi|ja|門前町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Monzen-machi|qq=}}, with numerous souvenir shops and food stalls. During the New Year period, the shrine attracts many visitors. A specialty of Mount Yuga is the locally famous "ankoro mochi" (sweet red bean rice cakes). In the {{ill|Meiji period|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji period|qq=}}, the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinbutsu bunri|qq=}} (separation of Shinto and Buddhism) policy split Yuga Shrine from Rendaiji Temple. Yuga Shrine now has 52 branch shrines across western Japan. After {{ill|World War II|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/World War II|qq=}}, the shrine and temple informally resumed cooperation, with temple monks conducting prayers at the shrine. However, Yuga Shrine formally resumed independent religious activities in 1997, leading to a dispute with Rendaiji over property and operations. In the Edo period, a practice known as "dual pilgrimage" emerged, encouraging worshippers to visit both {{ill|Yuga Daigongen|ja|瑜伽大権現|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuga Daigongen|qq=}} and {{ill|Konpira Gongen|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konpira Gongen|qq=}} in [[:en:Sanuki Province|Sanuki Province]]. == Deities == The shrine enshrines Hikosa no Oshinomikoto, Kamunaobi-no-Kami, Daigongen (Honji Buddha: [[:en:Amitābha|Amitābha]], [[:en:Bhaisajyaguru|Yakushi Nyorai Medicine Buddha]]), and others, including: * {{ill|Hikosachi no Mikoto|lt=Hikosachi no Mikoto|lt=Hikosa-no-Oshinomikoto|ja|彦狭知命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hikosa-no-Oshinomikoto|qq=}} * {{ill|Kamuna'obi|ja|神直日神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamunaobi-no-Kami|qq=}} * {{ill|Yuga Daigongen|ja|瑜伽大権現|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuga Daigongen|qq=}} (associated Buddhas: [[:en:Amitābha|Amitābha]], [[:en:Bhaisajyaguru|Yakushi Nyorai Medicine Buddha]]) * {{ill|Teokihobufu no Mikoto|ja|手置帆負命|1=Sanuki Inbe clan|lt=Teokihobufu no Mikoto|2=ja|3=讃岐忌部氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Teokihobufu no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanuki Inbe clan|qq=}} * [[:en:Ame-no-Minakanushi|Ame-no-Minakanushi]] * {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞鳴命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スサノオ}} * {{ill|Ukanomitama|lt=Ukanomitama-no-Kami|ja|倉稲魂命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ukanomitama|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ウカノミタマ}} * {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|1=Sugawara no Michizane|2=ja|3=菅原道真|ja|Sugawara no Michizane|1=Sugawara no Michizane|2=ja|3=菅原道真|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugawara no Michizane|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q382005}} * {{ill|Watatsumi|lt=Watatsumi-no-Mikoto|ja|大綿津見命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Watatsumi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * == Cultural Properties == * '''main hall''' – Built in the early Edo period, it is designated as an {{ill|Important Cultural Property of Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県指定重要文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Cultural Property of Okayama Prefecture|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (1961).{{Cite web|url=http://www.pref.okayama.jp/cgi-bin/bunka/culture/controller/client/resultDetail.cgi?id=174 |title=main hall of Yuga Shrine |publisher=Okayama Prefecture |accessdate=2018-06-24}} == Access == * '''By Car:''' About 15 minutes from [[:en:Seto-Chuo Expressway|Seto-Chuo Expressway]]'s {{ill|Mizushima Interchange|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mizushima Interchange|qq=}}. * '''By Taxi:''' About 15 minutes from {{ill|Kojima Station|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojima Station|qq=}} on the {{ill|Seto-Ohashi Line|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seto-Ohashi Line|qq=}}. * '''By Bus:''' A limited {{ill|Shimoden Bus|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimoden Bus|qq=}} service runs from Kojima Station to Yuga Shrine (twice daily). However, buses do not operate on weekends, holidays, or during the New Year period. == References == {{reflist}} == Further Reading == * Okayama Prefectural High School Social Studies Department History Section, ''Revised Edition: History Walks in Okayama Prefecture,'' Yamakawa Publishing, 1991, pp. 96–97. * On-site informational plaques. == Related Articles == * {{ill|Yugasan Rendaiji|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yugasan Rendaiji|qq=}} * {{ill|Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Sanzan|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|qq=}} * {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)|qq=}} * {{ill|Kotohira-gū|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kotohira-gū|qq=}} == External Links == * [https://yugasan.or.jp/ Yuga Shrine Hongū Official Website] * == References == {{Reflist}} * == External Links == * [https://yugasan.or.jp/ Official website of Yuga Shrine Main Shrine] {{shinto-stub}} {{Buddhism-stub}} {{Yuga Faith}} {{translated page|ja|由加神社本宮|version=104236521|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Yuga faith]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Kurashiki]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Independent shrines]] }} [[ja:由加神社本宮]] [[Category:Registered Tangible Cultural Property of Japan in Okayama prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties Designated by Okayama Prefecture]] {{Commons category|Yuga-jinja}} {{wikidata link|Q11577571}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

However, buses do not operate on weekends, holidays, or during the New Year period. == References == {{reflist}} == Further Reading == * Okayama Prefectural High School Social Studies Department History Section, ''Revised Edition: History Walks in Okayama Prefecture,'' Yamakawa Publishing, 1991, pp. 96–97. * On-site informational plaques. == Related Articles == * {{ill|Yugasan Rendaiji|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yugasan Rendaiji|qq=}} * {{ill|Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Sanzan|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|qq=}} * {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)|qq=}} * {{ill|Kotohira-gū|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kotohira-gū|qq=}} == External Links == * [https://yugasan.or.jp/ Yuga Shrine Hongū Official Website] * == References == {{Reflist}} * == External Links == * [https://yugasan.or.jp/ Official website of Yuga Shrine Main Shrine] {{shinto-stub}} {{Buddhism-stub}} {{Yuga Faith}} {{translated page|ja|由加神社本宮|version=104236521|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Yuga faith]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Kurashiki]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Independent shrines]] }} [[ja:由加神社本宮]] [[Category:Registered Tangible Cultural Property of Japan in Okayama prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties Designated by Okayama Prefecture]] {{Commons category|Yuga-jinja}} {{wikidata link|Q11577571}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]]

[[sn:commons:Category:Yuga-jinja]][[ja:由加神社本宮]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11577571]] Wikidata name: Yuga-jinja [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:34, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{otheruses}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Yuga Shrine Hongū | location = 2852 Yuga, Kojima, Kurashiki City, [[:en:Okayama Prefecture|Okayama Prefecture]] | type = 34.505917 | longitude = 133.851056 | map = Japan Okayama | deity = {{ill|Hikosasaji no Mikoto|ja|彦狭知命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hikosasaji no Mikoto|1=Hikosachi no Mikoto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q135327097}}, {{ill|Kaminahibi no Kami|ja|直毘神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaminahibi no Kami|1=Naobinokami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11581794|1=Ōnaobi no Kami}}, {{ill|Yuga Daigongen|ja|瑜伽大権現|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuga Daigongen|qq=}} and others | founded = {{nihongo|Tenpyō 5|733 CE}} | type = Former [[Prefectural Shrine|Prefectural Shrine]] | festivals = {{nihongo|Spring Grand festival|April first Sunday}}
{{nihongo|Autumn Grand festival|November 3}} | main hall = Three-bay-wide [[Hiyoku Irimoya-zukuri]] style | image = File:Yuga Jinja 04.jpg | name = Yuga Shrine Main Shrine | location = 2852 Ko-jima Yuga, [[:en:Kurashiki|Kurashiki]], [[:en:Okayama Prefecture|Okayama Prefecture]] | coordinates = {{Coord|34.505917|N|133.851056|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = Hikosa no Oshinomikoto, Kamunaobi-no-Kami, Daigongen, among others | Foundation = Traditionally [[Tenpyō]] 5 (733) | Shrine Rank = Former Prefectural Shrine (県社) | Annual festival = Spring Grand Festival (First Sunday of April)
Autumn Grand Festival (November 3) | Main hall style = Sanma-sha Hiyoku Irimoya-zukuri | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | location = {{ill|Kurashiki|WD=Q467487}} }}[[File:Hiroshige Bizen Tanokuchi.jpg|thumb|[[:en:Hiroshige|Utagawa Hiroshige]]]] '''Yuga Shrine Hongū''' ({{lang|ja|由加神社本宮}}) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto shrine|qq=}} located on [[Yuga Mountain]] in the Kojima area of southern {{ill|Kurashiki|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurashiki|qq=}}, {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okayama Prefecture|qq=}}, Japan. It is a {{ill|Independent Shinto shrine|lt=Independent Shinto shrine|lt=Standalone Shrine|ja|単立神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Standalone Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 単立|redirects to 単立|1=Independent Shinto shrine|2=ja|3=単立|1=Independent Shinto shrine|2=ja|3=単立|redirects to 単立|1=Independent Shinto shrine|2=ja|3=単立|}} unaffiliated with the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Association of Shinto Shrines|qq=}}. Its modern status under the shrine Ranking system is a former {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社|1=Prefectural Shrine|WD=Q90139226}}{{efn|Ken-sha|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}. == Overview == Mount Yuga has been a center of {{ill|Iwakura rock|lt=Iwakura faith|ja|磐座信仰|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwakura rock|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 磐座}} since ancient times. In 733 CE, the monk {{ill|Gyōki|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gyōki|qq=}} enshrined an [[:en:Ekādaśamukha|Eleven-faced Kannon]] statue here, establishing a religious complex that merged Shinto and Buddhism under the name '''{{nihongo|{{ill|Yuga Daigongen|ja|瑜伽大権現|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuga Daigongen|qq=}}|瑜伽大権現}}''' (Yuga Great Incarnation). According to the history of {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)|lt=Kumano Shrine|ja|熊野神社 (倉敷市林)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)|qq=}}, five disciples of {{ill|En no Gyōja|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/En no Gyōja|qq=}}, a prominent ascetic, brought Kumano deities from {{ill|Kii Province|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kii Province|qq=}} and established Kumano Shrine in the northern foothills of Mount Yuga. They later founded Nachi Shrine on Mount Yuga as one of the "New Kumano Three Mountains." From the mid-{{ill|Edo period|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Edo period|qq=}}, the shrine became a place of worship for the [[:en:Ikeda clan|Ikeda clan]], rulers of {{ill|Bizen Province|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bizen Province|qq=}}. The Ikeda clan sponsored the construction of the shrine’s buildings and nearby {{ill|Yugasan Rendaiji|lt=Rendaiji|ja|蓮台寺_(倉敷市)|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yugasan Rendaiji|qq=}}. The shrine is located in a bustling {{ill|Monzen-machi|ja|門前町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Monzen-machi|qq=}}, with numerous souvenir shops and food stalls. During the New Year period, the shrine attracts many visitors. A specialty of Mount Yuga is the locally famous "ankoro mochi" (sweet red bean rice cakes). In the {{ill|Meiji period|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji period|qq=}}, the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinbutsu bunri|qq=}} (separation of Shinto and Buddhism) policy split Yuga Shrine from Rendaiji Temple. Yuga Shrine now has 52 branch shrines across western Japan. After {{ill|World War II|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/World War II|qq=}}, the shrine and temple informally resumed cooperation, with temple monks conducting prayers at the shrine. However, Yuga Shrine formally resumed independent religious activities in 1997, leading to a dispute with Rendaiji over property and operations. In the Edo period, a practice known as "dual pilgrimage" emerged, encouraging worshippers to visit both {{ill|Yuga Daigongen|ja|瑜伽大権現|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuga Daigongen|qq=}} and {{ill|Konpira Gongen|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konpira Gongen|qq=}} in [[:en:Sanuki Province|Sanuki Province]]. == Deities == The shrine enshrines Hikosa no Oshinomikoto, Kamunaobi-no-Kami, Daigongen (Honji Buddha: [[:en:Amitābha|Amitābha]], [[:en:Bhaisajyaguru|Yakushi Nyorai Medicine Buddha]]), and others, including: * {{ill|Hikosachi no Mikoto|lt=Hikosachi no Mikoto|lt=Hikosa-no-Oshinomikoto|ja|彦狭知命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hikosa-no-Oshinomikoto|qq=}} * {{ill|Kamuna'obi|ja|神直日神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamunaobi-no-Kami|qq=}} * {{ill|Yuga Daigongen|ja|瑜伽大権現|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuga Daigongen|qq=}} (associated Buddhas: [[:en:Amitābha|Amitābha]], [[:en:Bhaisajyaguru|Yakushi Nyorai Medicine Buddha]]) * {{ill|Teokihobufu no Mikoto|ja|手置帆負命|1=Sanuki Inbe clan|lt=Teokihobufu no Mikoto|2=ja|3=讃岐忌部氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Teokihobufu no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanuki Inbe clan|qq=}} * [[:en:Ame-no-Minakanushi|Ame-no-Minakanushi]] * {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞鳴命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スサノオ}} * {{ill|Ukanomitama|lt=Ukanomitama-no-Kami|ja|倉稲魂命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ukanomitama|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ウカノミタマ}} * {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|1=Sugawara no Michizane|2=ja|3=菅原道真|ja|Sugawara no Michizane|1=Sugawara no Michizane|2=ja|3=菅原道真|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugawara no Michizane|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q382005}} * {{ill|Watatsumi|lt=Watatsumi-no-Mikoto|ja|大綿津見命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Watatsumi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * == Cultural Properties == * '''main hall''' – Built in the early Edo period, it is designated as an {{ill|Important Cultural Property of Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県指定重要文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Cultural Property of Okayama Prefecture|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (1961).{{Cite web|url=http://www.pref.okayama.jp/cgi-bin/bunka/culture/controller/client/resultDetail.cgi?id=174 |title=main hall of Yuga Shrine |publisher=Okayama Prefecture |accessdate=2018-06-24}} == Access == * '''By Car:''' About 15 minutes from [[:en:Seto-Chuo Expressway|Seto-Chuo Expressway]]'s {{ill|Mizushima {{ill|Seto-Ohashi Line|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seto-Ohashi Line|qq=}}. * '''By Bus:''' A limited {{ill|Shimoden Bus|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimoden Bus|qq=}} service runs from Kojima Station to Yuga Shrine (twice daily). However, buses do not operate on weekends, holidays, or during the New Year period. == References == {{reflist}} == Further Reading == * Okayama Prefectural High School Social Studies Department History Section, ''Revised Edition: History Walks in Okayama Prefecture,'' Yamakawa Publishing, 1991, pp. 96–97. * On-site informational plaques. == Related Articles == * {{ill|Yugasan Rendaiji|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yugasan Rendaiji|qq=}} * {{ill|Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Sanzan|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|qq=}} * {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)|qq=}} * {{ill|Kotohira-gū|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kotohira-gū|qq=}} == External Links == * [https://yugasan.or.jp/ Yuga Shrine Hongū Official Website] * == References == {{Reflist}} * == External Links == * [https://yugasan.or.jp/ Official website of Yuga Shrine Main Shrine] {{shinto-stub}} {{Buddhism-stub}} {{Yuga Faith}} {{translated page|ja|由加神社本宮|version=104236521|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Yuga faith]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Kurashiki]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Independent shrines]] }} [[ja:由加神社本宮]] [[Category:Registered Tangible Cultural Property of Japan in Okayama prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties Designated by Okayama Prefecture]] {{Commons category|Yuga-jinja}} {{wikidata link|Q11577571}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

However, buses do not operate on weekends, holidays, or during the New Year period. == References == {{reflist}} == Further Reading == * Okayama Prefectural High School Social Studies Department History Section, ''Revised Edition: History Walks in Okayama Prefecture,'' Yamakawa Publishing, 1991, pp. 96–97. * On-site informational plaques. == Related Articles == * {{ill|Yugasan Rendaiji|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yugasan Rendaiji|qq=}} * {{ill|Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Sanzan|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|qq=}} * {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)|qq=}} * {{ill|Kotohira-gū|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kotohira-gū|qq=}} == External Links == * [https://yugasan.or.jp/ Yuga Shrine Hongū Official Website] * == References == {{Reflist}} * == External Links == * [https://yugasan.or.jp/ Official website of Yuga Shrine Main Shrine] {{shinto-stub}} {{Buddhism-stub}} {{Yuga Faith}} {{translated page|ja|由加神社本宮|version=104236521|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Yuga faith]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Kurashiki]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Independent shrines]] }} [[ja:由加神社本宮]] [[Category:Registered Tangible Cultural Property of Japan in Okayama prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties Designated by Okayama Prefecture]] {{Commons category|Yuga-jinja}} {{wikidata link|Q11577571}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

[[sn:commons:Category:Yuga-jinja]][[ja:由加神社本宮]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11577571]] Wikidata name: Yuga-jinja [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:34, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{otheruses}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Yuga Shrine Hongū | location = 2852 Yuga, Kojima, Kurashiki City, [[:en:Okayama Prefecture|Okayama Prefecture]] | type = 34.505917 | longitude = 133.851056 | map = Japan Okayama | deity = {{ill|Hikosasaji no Mikoto|ja|彦狭知命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hikosasaji no Mikoto|1=Hikosachi no Mikoto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q135327097}}, {{ill|Kaminahibi no Kami|ja|直毘神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaminahibi no Kami|1=Naobinokami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11581794|1=Ōnaobi no Kami}}, {{ill|Yuga Daigongen|ja|瑜伽大権現|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuga Daigongen|qq=}} and others | founded = {{nihongo|Tenpyō 5|733 CE}} | type = Former [[Prefectural Shrine|Prefectural Shrine]] | festivals = {{nihongo|Spring Grand festival|April first Sunday}}
{{nihongo|Autumn Grand festival|November 3}} | main hall = Three-bay-wide [[Hiyoku Irimoya-zukuri]] style | image = File:Yuga Jinja 04.jpg | name = Yuga Shrine Main Shrine | location = 2852 Ko-jima Yuga, [[:en:Kurashiki|Kurashiki]], [[:en:Okayama Prefecture|Okayama Prefecture]] | coordinates = {{Coord|34.505917|N|133.851056|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = Hikosa no Oshinomikoto, Kamunaobi-no-Kami, Daigongen, among others | Foundation = Traditionally [[Tenpyō]] 5 (733) | Shrine Rank = Former Prefectural Shrine (県社) | Annual festival = Spring Grand Festival (First Sunday of April)
Autumn Grand Festival (November 3) | Main hall style = Sanma-sha Hiyoku Irimoya-zukuri | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | location = {{ill|Kurashiki|WD=Q467487}} }}[[File:Hiroshige Bizen Tanokuchi.jpg|thumb|[[:en:Hiroshige|Utagawa Hiroshige]]]] '''Yuga Shrine Hongū''' ({{lang|ja|由加神社本宮}}) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto shrine|qq=}} located on [[Yuga Mountain]] in the Kojima area of southern {{ill|Kurashiki|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurashiki|qq=}}, {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okayama Prefecture|qq=}}, Japan. It is a {{ill|Independent Shinto shrine|lt=Independent Shinto shrine|lt=Standalone Shrine|ja|単立神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Standalone Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 単立|redirects to 単立|1=Independent Shinto shrine|2=ja|3=単立|1=Independent Shinto shrine|2=ja|3=単立|redirects to 単立|1=Independent Shinto shrine|2=ja|3=単立|}} unaffiliated with the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Association of Shinto Shrines|qq=}}. Its modern status under the shrine Ranking system is a former {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社|1=Prefectural Shrine|WD=Q90139226}}{{efn|Ken-sha|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}. == Overview == Mount Yuga has been a center of {{ill|Iwakura rock|lt=Iwakura faith|ja|磐座信仰|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwakura rock|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 磐座}} since ancient times. In 733 CE, the monk {{ill|Gyōki|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gyōki|qq=}} enshrined an [[:en:Ekādaśamukha|Eleven-faced Kannon]] statue here, establishing a religious complex that merged Shinto and Buddhism under the name '''{{nihongo|{{ill|Yuga Daigongen|ja|瑜伽大権現|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuga Daigongen|qq=}}|瑜伽大権現}}''' (Yuga Great Incarnation). According to the history of {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)|lt=Kumano Shrine|ja|熊野神社 (倉敷市林)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)|qq=}}, five disciples of {{ill|En no Gyōja|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/En no Gyōja|qq=}}, a prominent ascetic, brought Kumano deities from {{ill|Kii Province|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kii Province|qq=}} and established Kumano Shrine in the northern foothills of Mount Yuga. They later founded Nachi Shrine on Mount Yuga as one of the "New Kumano Three Mountains." From the mid-{{ill|Edo period|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Edo period|qq=}}, the shrine became a place of worship for the [[:en:Ikeda clan|Ikeda clan]], rulers of {{ill|Bizen Province|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bizen Province|qq=}}. The Ikeda clan sponsored the construction of the shrine’s buildings and nearby {{ill|Yugasan Rendaiji|lt=Rendaiji|ja|蓮台寺_(倉敷市)|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yugasan Rendaiji|qq=}}. The shrine is located in a bustling {{ill|Monzen-machi|ja|門前町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Monzen-machi|qq=}}, with numerous souvenir shops and food stalls. During the New Year period, the shrine attracts many visitors. A specialty of Mount Yuga is the locally famous "ankoro mochi" (sweet red bean rice cakes). In the {{ill|Meiji period|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji period|qq=}}, the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinbutsu bunri|qq=}} (separation of Shinto and Buddhism) policy split Yuga Shrine from Rendaiji Temple. Yuga Shrine now has 52 branch shrines across western Japan. After {{ill|World War II|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/World War II|qq=}}, the shrine and temple informally resumed cooperation, with temple monks conducting prayers at the shrine. However, Yuga Shrine formally resumed independent religious activities in 1997, leading to a dispute with Rendaiji over property and operations. In the Edo period, a practice known as "dual pilgrimage" emerged, encouraging worshippers to visit both {{ill|Yuga Daigongen|ja|瑜伽大権現|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuga Daigongen|qq=}} and {{ill|Konpira Gongen|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konpira Gongen|qq=}} in [[:en:Sanuki Province|Sanuki Province]]. == Deities == The shrine enshrines Hikosa no Oshinomikoto, Kamunaobi-no-Kami, Daigongen (Honji Buddha: [[:en:Amitābha|Amitābha]], [[:en:Bhaisajyaguru|Yakushi Nyorai Medicine Buddha]]), and others, including: * {{ill|Hikosachi no Mikoto|lt=Hikosachi no Mikoto|lt=Hikosa-no-Oshinomikoto|ja|彦狭知命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hikosa-no-Oshinomikoto|qq=}} * {{ill|Kamuna'obi|ja|神直日神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamunaobi-no-Kami|qq=}} * {{ill|Yuga Daigongen|ja|瑜伽大権現|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuga Daigongen|qq=}} (associated Buddhas: [[:en:Amitābha|Amitābha]], [[:en:Bhaisajyaguru|Yakushi Nyorai Medicine Buddha]]) * {{ill|Teokihobufu no Mikoto|ja|手置帆負命|1=Sanuki Inbe clan|lt=Teokihobufu no Mikoto|2=ja|3=讃岐忌部氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Teokihobufu no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanuki Inbe clan|qq=}} * [[:en:Ame-no-Minakanushi|Ame-no-Minakanushi]] * {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞鳴命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スサノオ}} * {{ill|Ukanomitama|lt=Ukanomitama-no-Kami|ja|倉稲魂命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ukanomitama|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ウカノミタマ}} * {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|1=Sugawara no Michizane|2=ja|3=菅原道真|ja|Sugawara no Michizane|1=Sugawara no Michizane|2=ja|3=菅原道真|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugawara no Michizane|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q382005}} * {{ill|Watatsumi|lt=Watatsumi-no-Mikoto|ja|大綿津見命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Watatsumi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * == Cultural Properties == * '''main hall''' – Built in the early Edo period, it is designated as an {{ill|Important Cultural Property of Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県指定重要文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Cultural Property of Okayama Prefecture|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (1961).{{Cite web|url=http://www.pref.okayama.jp/cgi-bin/bunka/culture/controller/client/resultDetail.cgi?id=174 |title=main hall of Yuga Shrine |publisher=Okayama Prefecture |accessdate=2018-06-24}} == Access == * '''By Car:''' About 15 minutes from [[:en:Seto-Chuo Expressway|Seto-Chuo Expressway]]'s {{ill|Mizushima Interchange|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mizushima Interchange|qq=}}. * '''By Taxi:''' About 15 minutes from {{ill|Kojima Station|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojima Station|qq=}} on the {{ill|Seto-Ohashi Line|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seto-Ohashi Line|qq=}}. * '''By Bus:''' A limited {{ill|Shimoden Bus|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimoden Bus|qq=}} service runs from Kojima Station to Yuga Shrine (twice daily). However, buses do not operate on weekends, holidays, or during the New Year period. == References == {{reflist}} == Further Reading == * Okayama Prefectural High School Social Studies Department History Section, ''Revised Edition: History Walks in Okayama Prefecture,'' Yamakawa Publishing, 1991, pp. 96–97. * On-site informational plaques. == Related Articles == * {{ill|Yugasan Rendaiji|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yugasan Rendaiji|qq=}} * {{ill|Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Sanzan|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|qq=}} * {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)|qq=}} * {{ill|Kotohira-gū|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kotohira-gū|qq=}} == External Links == * [https://yugasan.or.jp/ Yuga Shrine Hongū Official Website] * == References == {{Reflist}} * == External Links == * [https://yugasan.or.jp/ Official website of Yuga Shrine Main Shrine] {{shinto-stub}} {{Buddhism-stub}} {{Yuga Faith}} {{translated page|ja|由加神社本宮|version=104236521|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Yuga faith]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Kurashiki]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Independent shrines]] }} [[ja:由加神社本宮]] [[Category:Registered Tangible Cultural Property of Japan in Okayama prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties Designated by Okayama Prefecture]] {{Commons category|Yuga-jinja}} {{wikidata link|Q11577571}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

Station to Yuga Shrine (twice daily). However, buses do not operate on weekends, holidays, or during the New Year period. == References == {{reflist}} == Further Reading == * Okayama Prefectural High School Social Studies Department History Section, ''Revised Edition: History Walks in Okayama Prefecture,'' Yamakawa Publishing, 1991, pp. 96–97. * On-site informational plaques. == Related Articles == * {{ill|Yugasan Rendaiji|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yugasan Rendaiji|qq=}} * {{ill|Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Sanzan|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|qq=}} * {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)|qq=}} * {{ill|Kotohira-gū|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kotohira-gū|qq=}} == External Links == * [https://yugasan.or.jp/ Yuga Shrine Hongū Official Website] * == References == {{Reflist}} * == External Links == * [https://yugasan.or.jp/ Official website of Yuga Shrine Main Shrine] {{shinto-stub}} {{Buddhism-stub}} {{Yuga Faith}} {{translated page|ja|由加神社本宮|version=104236521|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Yuga faith]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Kurashiki]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Independent shrines]] }} [[ja:由加神社本宮]] [[Category:Registered Tangible Cultural Property of Japan in Okayama prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties Designated by Okayama Prefecture]] {{Commons category|Yuga-jinja}} {{wikidata link|Q11577571}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

[[sn:commons:Category:Yuga-jinja]][[ja:由加神社本宮]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11577571]] Wikidata name: Yuga-jinja [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:34, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{otheruses}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Yuga Shrine Hongū | location = 2852 Yuga, Kojima, Kurashiki City, [[:en:Okayama Prefecture|Okayama Prefecture]] | type = 34.505917 | longitude = 133.851056 | map = Japan Okayama | deity = {{ill|Hikosasaji no Mikoto|ja|彦狭知命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hikosasaji no Mikoto|1=Hikosachi no Mikoto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q135327097}}, {{ill|Kaminahibi no Kami|ja|直毘神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaminahibi no Kami|1=Naobinokami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11581794|1=Ōnaobi no Kami}}, {{ill|Yuga Daigongen|ja|瑜伽大権現|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuga Daigongen|qq=}} and others | founded = {{nihongo|Tenpyō 5|733 CE}} | type = Former [[Prefectural Shrine|Prefectural Shrine]] | festivals = {{nihongo|Spring Grand festival|April first Sunday}}
{{nihongo|Autumn Grand festival|November 3}} | main hall = Three-bay-wide [[Hiyoku Irimoya-zukuri]] style | image = File:Yuga Jinja 04.jpg | name = Yuga Shrine Main Shrine | location = 2852 Ko-jima Yuga, [[:en:Kurashiki|Kurashiki]], [[:en:Okayama Prefecture|Okayama Prefecture]] | coordinates = {{Coord|34.505917|N|133.851056|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = Hikosa no Oshinomikoto, Kamunaobi-no-Kami, Daigongen, among others | Foundation = Traditionally [[Tenpyō]] 5 (733) | Shrine Rank = Former Prefectural Shrine (県社) | Annual festival = Spring Grand Festival (First Sunday of April)
Autumn Grand Festival (November 3) | Main hall style = Sanma-sha Hiyoku Irimoya-zukuri | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | location = {{ill|Kurashiki|WD=Q467487}} }}[[File:Hiroshige Bizen Tanokuchi.jpg|thumb|[[:en:Hiroshige|Utagawa Hiroshige]]]] '''Yuga Shrine Hongū''' ({{lang|ja|由加神社本宮}}) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto shrine|qq=}} located on [[Yuga Mountain]] in the Kojima area of southern {{ill|Kurashiki|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurashiki|qq=}}, {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okayama Prefecture|qq=}}, Japan. It is a {{ill|Independent Shinto shrine|lt=Independent Shinto shrine|lt=Standalone Shrine|ja|単立神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Standalone Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 単立|redirects to 単立|1=Independent Shinto shrine|2=ja|3=単立|1=Independent Shinto shrine|2=ja|3=単立|redirects to 単立|1=Independent Shinto shrine|2=ja|3=単立|}} unaffiliated with the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Association of Shinto Shrines|qq=}}. Its modern status under the shrine Ranking system is a former {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社|1=Prefectural Shrine|WD=Q90139226}}{{efn|Ken-sha|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}. == Overview == Mount Yuga has been a center of {{ill|Iwakura rock|lt=Iwakura faith|ja|磐座信仰|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwakura rock|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 磐座}} since ancient times. In 733 CE, the monk {{ill|Gyōki|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gyōki|qq=}} enshrined an [[:en:Ekādaśamukha|Eleven-faced Kannon]] statue here, establishing a religious complex that merged Shinto and Buddhism under the name '''{{nihongo|{{ill|Yuga Daigongen|ja|瑜伽大権現|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuga Daigongen|qq=}}|瑜伽大権現}}''' (Yuga Great Incarnation). According to the history of {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)|lt=Kumano Shrine|ja|熊野神社 (倉敷市林)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)|qq=}}, five disciples of {{ill|En no Gyōja|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/En no Gyōja|qq=}}, a prominent ascetic, brought Kumano deities from {{ill|Kii Province|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kii Province|qq=}} and established Kumano Shrine in the northern foothills of Mount Yuga. They later founded Nachi Shrine on Mount Yuga as one of the "New Kumano Three Mountains." From the mid-{{ill|Edo period|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Edo period|qq=}}, the shrine became a place of worship for the [[:en:Ikeda clan|Ikeda clan]], rulers of {{ill|Bizen Province|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bizen Province|qq=}}. The Ikeda clan sponsored the construction of the shrine’s buildings and nearby {{ill|Yugasan Rendaiji|lt=Rendaiji|ja|蓮台寺_(倉敷市)|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yugasan Rendaiji|qq=}}. The shrine is located in a bustling {{ill|Monzen-machi|ja|門前町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Monzen-machi|qq=}}, with numerous souvenir shops and food stalls. During the New Year period, the shrine attracts many visitors. A specialty of Mount Yuga is the locally famous "ankoro mochi" (sweet red bean rice cakes). In the {{ill|Meiji period|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji period|qq=}}, the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinbutsu bunri|qq=}} (separation of Shinto and Buddhism) policy split Yuga Shrine from Rendaiji Temple. Yuga Shrine now has 52 branch shrines across western Japan. After {{ill|World War II|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/World War II|qq=}}, the shrine and temple informally resumed cooperation, with temple monks conducting prayers at the shrine. However, Yuga Shrine formally resumed independent religious activities in 1997, leading to a dispute with Rendaiji over property and operations. In the Edo period, a practice known as "dual pilgrimage" emerged, encouraging worshippers to visit both {{ill|Yuga Daigongen|ja|瑜伽大権現|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuga Daigongen|qq=}} and {{ill|Konpira Gongen|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konpira Gongen|qq=}} in [[:en:Sanuki Province|Sanuki Province]]. == Deities == The shrine enshrines Hikosa no Oshinomikoto, Kamunaobi-no-Kami, Daigongen (Honji Buddha: [[:en:Amitābha|Amitābha]], [[:en:Bhaisajyaguru|Yakushi Nyorai Medicine Buddha]]), and others, including: * {{ill|Hikosachi no Mikoto|lt=Hikosachi no Mikoto|lt=Hikosa-no-Oshinomikoto|ja|彦狭知命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hikosa-no-Oshinomikoto|qq=}} * {{ill|Kamuna'obi|ja|神直日神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamunaobi-no-Kami|qq=}} * {{ill|Yuga Daigongen|ja|瑜伽大権現|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuga Daigongen|qq=}} (associated Buddhas: [[:en:Amitābha|Amitābha]], [[:en:Bhaisajyaguru|Yakushi Nyorai Medicine Buddha]]) * {{ill|Teokihobufu no Mikoto|ja|手置帆負命|1=Sanuki Inbe clan|lt=Teokihobufu no Mikoto|2=ja|3=讃岐忌部氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Teokihobufu no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanuki Inbe clan|qq=}} * [[:en:Ame-no-Minakanushi|Ame-no-Minakanushi]] * {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞鳴命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スサノオ}} * {{ill|Ukanomitama|lt=Ukanomitama-no-Kami|ja|倉稲魂命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ukanomitama|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ウカノミタマ}} * {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|1=Sugawara no Michizane|2=ja|3=菅原道真|ja|Sugawara no Michizane|1=Sugawara no Michizane|2=ja|3=菅原道真|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugawara no Michizane|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q382005}} * {{ill|Watatsumi|lt=Watatsumi-no-Mikoto|ja|大綿津見命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Watatsumi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * == Cultural Properties == * '''main hall''' – Built in the early Edo period, it is designated as an {{ill|Important Cultural Property of Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県指定重要文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Cultural Property of Okayama Prefecture|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (1961).{{Cite web|url=http://www.pref.okayama.jp/cgi-bin/bunka/culture/controller/client/resultDetail.cgi?id=174 |title=main hall of Yuga Shrine |publisher=Okayama Prefecture |accessdate=2018-06-24}} == Access == * '''By Car:''' About 15 minutes from [[:en:Seto-Chuo Expressway|Seto-Chuo Expressway]]'s {{ill|Mizushima Interchange|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mizushima Interchange|qq=}}. * '''By Taxi:''' About 15 minutes from {{ill|Kojima Station|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojima Station|qq=}} on the {{ill|Seto-Ohashi Line|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seto-Ohashi Line|qq=}}. * '''By Bus:''' A limited {{ill|Shimoden Bus|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimoden Bus|qq=}} service runs from Kojima Station to Yuga Shrine (twice daily). However, buses do not operate on weekends, holidays, or during the New Year period. == References == {{reflist}} == Further Reading == * Okayama Prefectural High School Social Studies Department History Section, ''Revised Edition: History Walks in Okayama Prefecture,'' Yamakawa Publishing, 1991, pp. 96–97. * On-site informational plaques. == Related Articles == * {{ill|Yugasan Rendaiji|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yugasan Rendaiji|qq=}} * {{ill|Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Sanzan|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|qq=}} * {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)|qq=}} * {{ill|Kotohira-gū|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kotohira-gū|qq=}} == External Links == * [https://yugasan.or.jp/ Yuga Shrine Hongū Official Website] * == References == {{Reflist}} * == External Links == * [https://yugasan.or.jp/ Official website of Yuga Shrine Main Shrine] {{shinto-stub}} {{Buddhism-stub}} {{Yuga Faith}} {{translated page|ja|由加神社本宮|version=104236521|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Yuga faith]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Kurashiki]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Independent shrines]] }} [[ja:由加神社本宮]] [[Category:Registered Tangible Cultural Property of Japan in Okayama prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties Designated by Okayama Prefecture]] {{Commons category|Yuga-jinja}} {{wikidata link|Q11577571}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]] {{wikidata link|Q11577571}} [[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

{{ill|Seto-Ohashi Line|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seto-Ohashi Line|qq=}}. * '''By Bus:''' A limited {{ill|Shimoden Bus|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimoden Bus|qq=}} service runs from Kojima Station to Yuga Shrine (twice daily). However, buses do not operate on weekends, holidays, or during the New Year period. == References == {{reflist}} == Further Reading == * Okayama Prefectural High School Social Studies Department History Section, ''Revised Edition: History Walks in Okayama Prefecture,'' Yamakawa Publishing, 1991, pp. 96–97. * On-site informational plaques. == Related Articles == * {{ill|Yugasan Rendaiji|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yugasan Rendaiji|qq=}} * {{ill|Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Sanzan|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|qq=}} * {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)|qq=}} * {{ill|Kotohira-gū|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kotohira-gū|qq=}} == External Links == * [https://yugasan.or.jp/ Yuga Shrine Hongū Official Website] * == References == {{Reflist}} * == External Links == * [https://yugasan.or.jp/ Official website of Yuga Shrine Main Shrine] {{shinto-stub}} {{Buddhism-stub}} {{Yuga Faith}} {{translated page|ja|由加神社本宮|version=104236521|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Yuga faith]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Kurashiki]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Independent shrines]] }} [[ja:由加神社本宮]] [[Category:Registered Tangible Cultural Property of Japan in Okayama prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties Designated by Okayama Prefecture]] {{Commons category|Yuga-jinja}} {{wikidata link|Q11577571}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] {{wikidata link|Q11577571}}

[[sn:commons:Category:Yuga-jinja]][[ja:由加神社本宮]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11577571]] Wikidata name: Yuga-jinja [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:34, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{otheruses}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Yuga Shrine Hongū | location = 2852 Yuga, Kojima, Kurashiki City, [[:en:Okayama Prefecture|Okayama Prefecture]] | type = 34.505917 | longitude = 133.851056 | map = Japan Okayama | deity = {{ill|Hikosasaji no Mikoto|ja|彦狭知命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hikosasaji no Mikoto|1=Hikosachi no Mikoto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q135327097}}, {{ill|Kaminahibi no Kami|ja|直毘神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaminahibi no Kami|1=Naobinokami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11581794|1=Ōnaobi no Kami}}, {{ill|Yuga Daigongen|ja|瑜伽大権現|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuga Daigongen|qq=}} and others | founded = {{nihongo|Tenpyō 5|733 CE}} | type = Former [[Prefectural Shrine|Prefectural Shrine]] | festivals = {{nihongo|Spring Grand festival|April first Sunday}}
{{nihongo|Autumn Grand festival|November 3}} | main hall = Three-bay-wide [[Hiyoku Irimoya-zukuri]] style | image = File:Yuga Jinja 04.jpg | name = Yuga Shrine Main Shrine | location = 2852 Ko-jima Yuga, [[:en:Kurashiki|Kurashiki]], [[:en:Okayama Prefecture|Okayama Prefecture]] | coordinates = {{Coord|34.505917|N|133.851056|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = Hikosa no Oshinomikoto, Kamunaobi-no-Kami, Daigongen, among others | Foundation = Traditionally [[Tenpyō]] 5 (733) | Shrine Rank = Former Prefectural Shrine (県社) | Annual festival = Spring Grand Festival (First Sunday of April)
Autumn Grand Festival (November 3) | Main hall style = Sanma-sha Hiyoku Irimoya-zukuri | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | location = {{ill|Kurashiki|WD=Q467487}} }}[[File:Hiroshige Bizen Tanokuchi.jpg|thumb|[[:en:Hiroshige|Utagawa Hiroshige]]]] '''Yuga Shrine Hongū''' ({{lang|ja|由加神社本宮}}) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto shrine|qq=}} located on [[Yuga Mountain]] in the Kojima area of southern {{ill|Kurashiki|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurashiki|qq=}}, {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okayama Prefecture|qq=}}, Japan. It is a {{ill|Independent Shinto shrine|lt=Independent Shinto shrine|lt=Standalone Shrine|ja|単立神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Standalone Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 単立|redirects to 単立|1=Independent Shinto shrine|2=ja|3=単立|1=Independent Shinto shrine|2=ja|3=単立|redirects to 単立|1=Independent Shinto shrine|2=ja|3=単立|}} unaffiliated with the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Association of Shinto Shrines|qq=}}. Its modern status under the shrine Ranking system is a former {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社|1=Prefectural Shrine|WD=Q90139226}}{{efn|Ken-sha|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}. == Overview == Mount Yuga has been a center of {{ill|Iwakura rock|lt=Iwakura faith|ja|磐座信仰|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwakura rock|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 磐座}} since ancient times. In 733 CE, the monk {{ill|Gyōki|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gyōki|qq=}} enshrined an [[:en:Ekādaśamukha|Eleven-faced Kannon]] statue here, establishing a religious complex that merged Shinto and Buddhism under the name '''{{nihongo|{{ill|Yuga Daigongen|ja|瑜伽大権現|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuga Daigongen|qq=}}|瑜伽大権現}}''' (Yuga Great Incarnation). According to the history of {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)|lt=Kumano Shrine|ja|熊野神社 (倉敷市林)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)|qq=}}, five disciples of {{ill|En no Gyōja|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/En no Gyōja|qq=}}, a prominent ascetic, brought Kumano deities from {{ill|Kii Province|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kii Province|qq=}} and established Kumano Shrine in the northern foothills of Mount Yuga. They later founded Nachi Shrine on Mount Yuga as one of the "New Kumano Three Mountains." From the mid-{{ill|Edo period|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Edo period|qq=}}, the shrine became a place of worship for the [[:en:Ikeda clan|Ikeda clan]], rulers of {{ill|Bizen Province|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bizen Province|qq=}}. The Ikeda clan sponsored the construction of the shrine’s buildings and nearby {{ill|Yugasan Rendaiji|lt=Rendaiji|ja|蓮台寺_(倉敷市)|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yugasan Rendaiji|qq=}}. The shrine is located in a bustling {{ill|Monzen-machi|ja|門前町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Monzen-machi|qq=}}, with numerous souvenir shops and food stalls. During the New Year period, the shrine attracts many visitors. A specialty of Mount Yuga is the locally famous "ankoro mochi" (sweet red bean rice cakes). In the {{ill|Meiji period|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji period|qq=}}, the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinbutsu bunri|qq=}} (separation of Shinto and Buddhism) policy split Yuga Shrine from Rendaiji Temple. Yuga Shrine now has 52 branch shrines across western Japan. After {{ill|World War II|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/World War II|qq=}}, the shrine and temple informally resumed cooperation, with temple monks conducting prayers at the shrine. However, Yuga Shrine formally resumed independent religious activities in 1997, leading to a dispute with Rendaiji over property and operations. In the Edo period, a practice known as "dual pilgrimage" emerged, encouraging worshippers to visit both {{ill|Yuga Daigongen|ja|瑜伽大権現|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuga Daigongen|qq=}} and {{ill|Konpira Gongen|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konpira Gongen|qq=}} in [[:en:Sanuki Province|Sanuki Province]]. == Deities == The shrine enshrines Hikosa no Oshinomikoto, Kamunaobi-no-Kami, Daigongen (Honji Buddha: [[:en:Amitābha|Amitābha]], [[:en:Bhaisajyaguru|Yakushi Nyorai Medicine Buddha]]), and others, including: * {{ill|Hikosachi no Mikoto|lt=Hikosachi no Mikoto|lt=Hikosa-no-Oshinomikoto|ja|彦狭知命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hikosa-no-Oshinomikoto|qq=}} * {{ill|Kamuna'obi|ja|神直日神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamunaobi-no-Kami|qq=}} * {{ill|Yuga Daigongen|ja|瑜伽大権現|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuga Daigongen|qq=}} (associated Buddhas: [[:en:Amitābha|Amitābha]], [[:en:Bhaisajyaguru|Yakushi Nyorai Medicine Buddha]]) * {{ill|Teokihobufu no Mikoto|ja|手置帆負命|1=Sanuki Inbe clan|lt=Teokihobufu no Mikoto|2=ja|3=讃岐忌部氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Teokihobufu no Mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanuki Inbe clan|qq=}} * [[:en:Ame-no-Minakanushi|Ame-no-Minakanushi]] * {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|素盞鳴命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スサノオ}} * {{ill|Ukanomitama|lt=Ukanomitama-no-Kami|ja|倉稲魂命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ukanomitama|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ウカノミタマ}} * {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|1=Sugawara no Michizane|2=ja|3=菅原道真|ja|Sugawara no Michizane|1=Sugawara no Michizane|2=ja|3=菅原道真|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugawara no Michizane|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q382005}} * {{ill|Watatsumi|lt=Watatsumi-no-Mikoto|ja|大綿津見命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Watatsumi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * == Cultural Properties == * '''main hall''' – Built in the early Edo period, it is designated as an {{ill|Important Cultural Property of Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県指定重要文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Cultural Property of Okayama Prefecture|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (1961).{{Cite web|url=http://www.pref.okayama.jp/cgi-bin/bunka/culture/controller/client/resultDetail.cgi?id=174 |title=main hall of Yuga Shrine |publisher=Okayama Prefecture |accessdate=2018-06-24}} == Access == * '''By Car:''' About 15 minutes from [[:en:Seto-Chuo Expressway|Seto-Chuo Expressway]]'s {{ill|Mizushima Interchange|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mizushima Interchange|qq=}}. * '''By Taxi:''' About 15 minutes from {{ill|Kojima Station|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojima Station|qq=}} on the {{ill|Seto-Ohashi Line|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seto-Ohashi Line|qq=}}. * '''By Bus:''' A limited {{ill|Shimoden Bus|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimoden Bus|qq=}} service runs from Kojima Station to Yuga Shrine (twice daily). However, buses do not operate on weekends, holidays, or during the New Year period. == References == {{reflist}} == Further Reading == * Okayama Prefectural High School Social Studies Department History Section, ''Revised Edition: History Walks in Okayama Prefecture,'' Yamakawa Publishing, 1991, pp. 96–97. * On-site informational plaques. == Related Articles == * {{ill|Yugasan Rendaiji|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yugasan Rendaiji|qq=}} * {{ill|Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Sanzan|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Sanzan|qq=}} * {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)|qq=}} * {{ill|Kotohira-gū|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kotohira-gū|qq=}} == External Links == * [https://yugasan.or.jp/ Yuga Shrine Hongū Official Website] * == References == {{Reflist}} * == External Links == * [https://yugasan.or.jp/ Official website of Yuga Shrine Main Shrine] {{shinto-stub}} {{Buddhism-stub}} {{Yuga Faith}} {{translated page|ja|由加神社本宮|version=104236521|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Yuga faith]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Kurashiki]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Okayama Prefecture]] [[Category:Independent shrines]] }} [[ja:由加神社本宮]] [[Category:Registered Tangible Cultural Property of Japan in Okayama prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties Designated by Okayama Prefecture]] {{Commons category|Yuga-jinja}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] {{wikidata link|Q11577571}}

According to the history of {{ill|Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)|lt=Kumano Shrine|ja|熊野神社 (倉敷市林)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Shrine (Kurashiki)|qq=}}, five disciples of {{ill|En no Gyōja|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/En no Gyōja|qq=}}, a prominent ascetic, brought Kumano deities from {{ill|Kii Province|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kii Province|qq=}} and established Kumano Shrine in the northern foothills of Mount Yuga. The Ikeda clan sponsored the construction of the shrine’s buildings and nearby {{ill|Yugasan Rendaiji|lt=Rendaiji|ja|蓮台寺_(倉敷市)|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yugasan Rendaiji|qq=}}. During the New Year period, the shrine attracts many visitors. A specialty of Mount Yuga is the locally famous "ankoro mochi" (sweet red bean rice cakes). In the {{ill|Meiji period|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji period|qq=}}, the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinbutsu bunri|qq=}} (separation of Shinto and Buddhism) policy split Yuga Shrine from Rendaiji Temple. Yuga Shrine now has 52 branch shrines across western Japan. After {{ill|World War II|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/World War II|qq=}}, the shrine and temple informally resumed cooperation, with temple monks conducting prayers at the shrine. However, Yuga Shrine formally resumed independent religious activities in 1997, leading to a dispute with Rendaiji over property and operations. In the Edo period, a practice known as "dual pilgrimage" emerged, encouraging worshippers to visit both {{ill|Yuga Daigongen|ja|瑜伽大権現|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuga Daigongen|qq=}} and {{ill|Konpira Gongen|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konpira Gongen|qq=}} in [[:en:Sanuki Province|Sanuki Province]]. == Deities == The shrine enshrines Hikosa no Oshinomikoto, Kamunaobi-no-Kami, Mikoto|lt=Hikosa-no-Oshinomikoto|ja|彦狭知命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hikosa-no-Oshinomikoto|qq=}} * {{ill|Kamuna'obi|ja|神直日神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamunaobi-no-Kami|qq=}} * {{ill|Yuga
